text: This doesn't surprise me since the entire history of capitalism from the 19th century to today is the SAME. To really understand capitalism one must read the three volumes of Das Kapital by Marx in order to really deep dive into the HORROR and monstrosity that it's applied to factory workers by capital. This phenomenon is not new, it's basically the same pattern that is repeated back then from the victorian workers and now south-east asia workers. Imperialism is basically the way in which the core countries outsource production in periphery countries in order to extract as much surplus value as possible. Amazing documentary.

text: Why don’t we all focus on the important things in life and not the superficial.  Spend time with family and friends, travel, enjoy the outdoors. Fabric manufacturing/dyeing wastes billions of gallons of water, and many of these materials are polyester, or recycled plastic materials.  Washing these items release micro plastics which eventually make their way to the oceans and our drinking water.  Micro plastics have recently been found in humans bloodstreams and brains.  When I meet someone, I assure you that what they are wearing has absolutely nothing to do with how I will interact with them. Their kindness and personality is what matters.  The world is too shallow and superficial these days, it is very sad…

Electricity costs €0.061 per kilowatt-hour, halved during off-peak hours. Natural gas costs €0.37 per cubic meter. Public transportation costs €0.24, and the metro's starting fare is €0.24.
The average person can eat very well for just €2.
A 60-inch 4K smart TV costs only €120. An energy-efficient air conditioner suitable for a large bedroom costs only €170, including installation and a 10-year warranty.
For someone with an income of €1600, daily necessities and home appliances are practically free.
China has nine years of compulsory education, and high school and public university tuition is very affordable, approximately €120/€700 per semester. More importantly, there are virtually no private schools in China, and the quality of education in public schools is very high.

text: Any Westerner watching this video would probably think that working 60 hours a week for a monthly salary of €1,600 is nothing short of blatant exploitation. Yet in China, 700 million people earn less than €120 a month. The €1,600 this female worker earns through excessive overtime not only has to support herself, but will most likely also go towards supporting her children back in her rural hometown thousands of kms away. As a Western consumer, you may naturally feel that this worker is being treated unfairly and thus boycott Shein, an act that may even make you feel morally righteous. But this moral gesture of yours could cost the worker her income, and her children their chance at an education. Poverty is the common enemy of all humanity.

author: @BeBHGHHJBB
text:
Il 10 dicembre scorso, Giornata dei diritti umani, il quotidiano Westdeutsche Allgemeine Zeitung (WAZ) di Gelsenkirchen ha pubblicato un articolo intitolato “Stiamo chiudendo un occhio sul prelievo forzato di organi”. Il pezzo invita la Germania a prestare attenzione al prelievo forzato di organi, autorizzato dallo Stato, attuato in Cina dal Partito Comunista Cinese (PCC).


L'articolo: “Stiamo chiudendo un occhio sul prelievo forzato di organi”, pubblicato il 10 dicembre 2025 dal quotidiano Westdeutsche Allgemeine Zeitung (WAZ)

Gelsenkirchen si trova nella parte centrale della Renania Settentrionale-Vestfalia, Germania, e fa parte di un'area metropolitana densamente popolata. Il WAZ ha intervistato il dottor Andreas Weber, residente locale, vicedirettore dell'organizzazione internazionale per i diritti umani: Medici contro il prelievo forzato di organi (DAFOH), e ha pubblicato l'articolo in occasione della Giornata dei diritti umani.

Il pezzo citava quanto affermato da Weber sulla missione della DAFOH: “L'attenzione dell'organizzazione è rivolta sistematicamente alla Cina, in particolare alla persecuzione delle minoranze come gli uiguri e i praticanti del Falun Gong, una disciplina spirituale cinese che unisce meditazione e esercizi fisici dai movimenti delicati a insegnamenti morali basati sui principi di Verità, Compassione e Tolleranza”.

“Per anni, la Cina è stata oggetto di gravi accuse di utilizzo di membri di gruppi minoritari come fonti di organi. Nel 2021, esperti delle Nazioni Unite hanno dichiarato di avere 'informazioni credibili' al riguardo, prove che classificano come 'estremamente allarmanti'. Anche la Società Internazionale per i Diritti Umani ha ripetutamente condannato il prelievo forzato di organi in Cina e sottolinea che l'origine di decine di migliaia di organi 'donati' è del tutto incerta”.

“La nostra ricerca dimostra che, in Cina, viene uccisa all'incirca una persona ogni 30 minuti a causa del prelievo forzato di organi”, ha affermato Andreas Weber. “Tuttavia, le vittime non sono solo i praticanti del Falun Gong, disciplina vietata e perseguitata in Cina dal 1999, ma anche uiguri, tibetani, musulmani e cristiani”. Gli organi dei praticanti del Falun Gong sono considerati particolarmente “puri”, poiché è noto che gli aderenti al movimento spirituale conducano uno stile di vita particolarmente sano.

Weber sottolinea che il prelievo illegale di organi non avviene solo in Cina. “Ma lì è organizzato dallo Stato, mentre altrove è una pratica attuata da organizzazioni di stampo mafioso”, ha spiegato.

L'articolo menzionava il Falun Gong Protection Act, approvato dalla Camera dei rappresentanti degli Stati Uniti e proposto al Senato degli Stati Uniti. Weber ha affermato: “Questo disegno di legge, sostenuto dalla DAFOH, condanna la persecuzione sistematica dei praticanti del Falun Gong da parte del Partito Comunista Cinese e, tra le altre cose, richiede al governo degli Stati Uniti di fornire rapporti completi sulle politiche cinesi in materia di prelievo forzato di organi”.

Weber ritiene che anche la Germania e l'Europa abbiano una responsabilità. “Esistono ancora collaborazioni tra centri di trapianti tedeschi e istituzioni cinesi”, ha dichiarato. Per lui, è chiaro: questa cooperazione deve interrompersi finché non sarà dimostrato in modo indipendente che l'abuso di organi autorizzato dallo Stato in Cina sia cessato.

La DAFOH chiede norme di legge per impedire ai pazienti europei di partecipare al “turismo dei trapianti” in Cina.

“Per interessi economici, stiamo vendendo le nostre anime alla Cina e ignorando il fatto che la gente sta morendo a causa sua. Questo squilibrio è spaventoso!”, ha dichiarato Weber. L'autore ha sottolineato questa affermazione in un punto saliente dell'articolo.

“Dobbiamo sensibilizzare la società su questi crimini per impedire che gli acquirenti di organi ne diventino inconsapevolmente complici”, ha detto Weber.

author: @meheem
text:
I am seeing this topic more and more now a days. Where I certainly understand where this is coming from and the importance of pressuring companies to treat their workers better, it's very important that we do it right. 
If this campaign is done without proper research and only with the spirit of activism without understanding the consequences of every actions taken, you will end up killing these very workers you are feeling sorry for. 
Instead of poor life standard and having to work long hours, these people would simply starve death. You know how many people depend of Garments industry in Bangladesh ? If you stopped purchasing from these brands any idea what a disaster it would be for the country of 160 million where 3 to 4 millions are employed by these fast fashion industry  ? 
Take it slow and do it right. Don't kill these people in your pursuit of activism.

author: @ginaalbano3159
text: Buy second hand. it's the easiest and most affortable way to still make an impact. Sure, I'm not perfect I still buy a sweater or two from forever 21 or HM, because I can't always afford sustainable brands. But by thrift shopping I have decreased my number of fast fashion purchases by an outstanding margin, and that makes a difference, maybe microscopic, but if more people made this change it could effect the brand and force them to change. Making the excuse that ethical/sustainable brands are too expensive is weak. Do the fucking research. Brands like Everlane are sustainable, ethical, and at the same price point as Zara or Urban Outfitters. For those complaining about thrift shops in their area there are plenty of online second hand options like depop, poshmark, thred-up, etc. If you're going to complain in the comments about how you don't like these second hand sites or thrift stores then continue to be part of the problem - that's on your conscious. It's not hard to change habits if it's going to save the environment and peoples' live.

author: @fkatwigsisthequeenofenglan4748
text:
I live for shit like this.
I currently work at primark and during the induction they show us the videos of workers in sweatshops, but they dont show you what is really going on. They paint a pretty picture, add bright colours and show 'happy', smiling workers, but it is all a lie. They claim that they donate what they cannot sell to 'charity' which is really small town in these "developing country." But i doesnt tell you about the effects that it is having on these communities. They treat it as a waste land and just dump it all there resulting many independent designers and sellers to close down beacause they cannot compete. These workers in sweatshops cannot often afford to send their children to school or to be taken care of, so they either send them to live in villages further away with distant family (i.e China) or take them to work with them in harsh conditions (i.e. Bangladesh). 
Primark also claim that these workers have rights and can form unions but that isnt true. 
But as much as id like to shop ethically, it is almost completely out of my price range, not to mention that i am also a 6ft tall girl so these sizes do not cater to me. I often go to charity shops & thrift shops (which are also pricey) to buy some things. I am also starting to make my own clothes and i encourage you all to do the same.
Also, that factory that collapsed in bangladesh, was one of Primarks. DRAG HA! Keep exposing them.

WATCH THE TRUE COST!!!! IT GOES INTO A LOT MORE DEPTH.

author: @Cinthia_Lin
text:
I’m truly humbled by the overwhelming response to this video—thank you all for watching, sharing, and caring deeply about these issues. I wanted to address some of the most common comments:

1)To those asking about my safety: Thank you for your concern! I’m completely safe. ☺ Many of the issues discussed in this video are already openly debated on Chinese social media, and I actually drew a lot of insights from those discussions while making this video.

2) Will there be more investigations like this? Honestly, I’m not sure. While I believe in shedding light on these topics, I also recognize that labor conditions in global manufacturing—not just at Shein—are deeply tied to complex supply and demand forces. Real change takes time.

3)To those rethinking their shopping habits: It means so much to hear that this video has made some of you more aware. If there’s one takeaway, it’s that ALL fast fashion—not just Shein—often relies on labor conditions that may be far worse than what most consumers realize.

Again, thank you for engaging with this discussion so thoughtfully. Your comments have given me a lot to reflect on!🙂

author: @xiaosongyu2926
text:
任何强大的甲方，都有极强的议价能力，这不是shein特有的。特别又在衣服这种技术含量低，市场竞争又很激烈的行业，这一结果几乎是必然的。视频里面有几个误区，我想指出下：
1.整个服装供应链的问题，和shein的需求没有必然联系。shein是这个市场的参与者，它不是这个行业或者说供应链的缔造者。即使没有shein，这个行业因为自身的低门槛，和中国大量劳动力叠加在一起，也会导致利润率极低的情况。微观经济学中的完全竞争市场，自然会出现这一结果。
2.因为博主在中国，你对中国的情况也很了解。提出改善工作环境，提高工资，问题是在这种竞争过度的行业，提高工资，是不是意味着拿不到订单，反而连辛苦钱都挣不到了？这个问题不是一句改善工人工作环境这么简单的话。同时，你的视频也可以看到，拍的基本都是些家庭性质的小手工作坊，稍微大一些的工厂，你都没有接触到。就能知道服装的门槛有多低。现在因为中国的产业升级，很多服装业转移到东南亚地区了，同时也有很多人抱怨，认为挣辛苦钱的机会没有了。
3.因为我们都在中国，所以我不得不指出，你在假装应聘时，提出一个月挣1万多人民币这个需求时，你自己对这个工作的技术含量，是否能挣到1万多没有点基本概念吗？1万多人民币，在中国的实际购买力，可不是简单的按汇率换成欧元或者美元去对比。
总体来讲，我不是很喜欢这个视频，如果你是个外国人，对中国不了解，做出这么一个视频也就罢了。但既然你都在中国，至少在讲这个话题时，还是应该有一些基本的常识和背景信息要交代的，而目前信息很局限，得出结论很简单仓促。

Thank you to everyone who buys clothes from Shein.
Your purchases create jobs for people shown in this video—workers from the lower-income segments of society—giving them the opportunity to earn a living and support themselves and their families.

For many of them, earning around 5,000 RMB can cover a family’s rent, a month of basic living expenses, and their children’s education. It has changed the lives of migrant workers who come to cities from rural areas across China.

With stable income, they can also afford to contribute to pension insurance, so that by the age of 60, they are able to retire and receive a pension.

author: @StrayChoom
text:
It’s not a “consumerism” problem, it’s a capitalism problem. But cutting consumption does help a worker out. You can vote against it with your wallet, but you’ll lose the vote.

This is just the necessary result of capitalist imperialism. This exploitation and bad treatment is what private ownership of capital leads to in itself. And it doesn’t just apply to fast fashion, it applies to all companies. 

What this does not bring up is that these conditions for fast fashion are even worse in Bangladesh, who regularly employ child labour and pay even less. There are workers in the west and in the US who are paid less by the hour. This is not exclusive to China, or the fast fashion industry.

This stuff in the beginning of English capitalism was very common. We used child labour, so much so. And the capitalists fought tooth and nail to keep children, little boys and girls working themselves to death.

The thing is, none of it ever stopped. Once we outlawed it, well we still wanted cheap clothes that could only feasibly be made at that exchange value (price) via extremely exploitative means. So we then just buy the products of child labour from other countries, we even destabilise other countries and occupy their markets such that these products can be kept at such a cheap price. Which reproduces and normalises the exploitative practice.

I never buy from fast fashion, but what I have to say is not buying is not enough. You need to fight against capitalism - private ownership of capital by individuals.

It could be done away with as simply as slave owning (private ownership of labour) was done away with.

author: @NoeliaMartinez33
text: It's no different from a textile or shoe factory; they're all the same, and the working hours vary. I've worked like that in Argentina; I was overworked, and my back was in terrible shape. It's not just Shein... it's everything, really. Do you think Shein and Chanel are different? No, it's all the same. Working in production is the same. Seeing piles of clothes and shoes is something I've seen in every factory. Without factories, none of what we have these days would exist. EVERYTHING we buy, even food, is produced through exploitation. I'm not defending Shein, but it's no different from any other factory, whatever its purpose. I'm not in favor of the over-exploitation of employees, much less the forced labor of slaves. But without these things, the world as we know it wouldn't function.

author: @ceekay3143
text:
I bet those working per piece are also not being compensated if their working conditions or employer decisions affect their ability to efficiently complete their work to the company standard. I mean, I was lucky to be on an hourly wage rather than per piece when I worked in a food factory, because the downtime was ridiculous. 


Lack of regular maintenance on machines leading to higher rates of product failure (product getting damaged/unusable), as well as equipment failure. Leading to having to hand package product at maybe 10 pieces per minute tops, compared to the machine at 50-100 per minute. This could last for a few minutes, to a few days, because of course we'd have to fly in a highly skilled expert to repair things when they got beyond what we could fix with a toolbox. And of course, loss from destroyed/wasted product and packaging materials during failures.
Like, I don't think Shein companies would be performing regular maintenance and servicing, or compensating workers for the pieces they would have been able to make if everything was in working order.


One of the worst weeks was when a whole shipment of (refrigerated) product was rejected due to temperatures being too high. Some asshat delivery driver decided to turn off refrigeration in the truck to save money on petrol, so at least the company had insurance and someone to compensate for the trouble. But we then us employees got to spend several hours going through the rejected/dead stock and separating food product from recyclable packaging, to reduce garbage costs. And no new product could be made and packaged while we were handling this and trying to clear out fridge space. 
Do Shein workers still get paid their cents per piece if that piece reaches the end of the production line and is rejected for quality issues before it leaves the warehouse? What if something happens on the way through to customers, and their order is refunded? I assume that Shein would be the one missing out on the money, buying the pieces from the garment companies, but I wouldn't put it past someone to punish the workers for this.

China and many asian countries have longer working hours in EVERY industry not just textiles, those salaries are actually pretty good for China and you could live really comfortably for that. Its actually more than I earn in New Zealand lol.

Am I in support of the industry? No...for environmental and sustainability reasons, definitely not. But hyper focusing on these brands is weird to me. Everything you buy comes from factories often in China. China probably has better working conditions and wages than India, Bangladesh etc. If you take away all these jobs THOUSANDSSSS OF PEOPLE WILL SUFFER. What is the solution????? No one knows yet.

author: @juliannecook5544
text:
A lot of comments suggesting people thrift clothes, then replies about how thrifting sucks now (for a variety of reasons). Okay! So pay attention to small batch clothing companies, local clothing companies, indie designers. You can find so much ethical and stylish fashion if you only look for it. Budget new clothing purchases and your closet will shift bit by bit. We don’t need tons of clothes, we don’t need constantly changing wardrobes. Plan for new items and do your research on those companies - materials, fabric sourcing, labor, etc. Options are out there! 

Also, thrifting isn’t as bad as people claim. While some places are overpriced, you can also thrift online or use clothing reselling websites. There are so many choices!

We got up at 5am was at work by 6am we were paid by piece and a small hourly wage roughly 3:50 USD an hour was top pay. I was just 15, we made perfect garments without seconds. They were the right size made with superior fabric .The quality was so good many of those garments are still around after 40-50 yrs. Meanwhile you complain and your garments are second hand quality at best, the sizes are never correct you claim you cannot judge American sizes which is just a slap on American women. We might prefer our jobs back , we know how to sew. Many of us women would be happy to have our thread spinning, weaving cloth, sewing garments jobs back. So just keep complaining.

author: @houseofshadow
text:
As someone working 9am-10pm+ in China's creative industry for Western luxury brands, I find the selective outrage here fascinating. Shein's labor practices mirror the entire fast fashion industry—Zara, H&M, Gap all operate on similar models. The difference? When European brands exploit Bangladeshi workers (see: 2013 Rana Plaza, 1,100+ deaths), it's "supply chain issues." When a Chinese brand employs Chinese workers, it's "Chinese exploitation"—framed as a cultural defect rather than a capitalist system issue.
Shein's real sin isn't worse ethics; it's being more efficient, threatening Western market share. If you truly care about workers, demand change industry-wide—not just from the competitor you've conveniently designated as the villain.

author: @d.m.uu.8776
text:
Before judging the salary numbers in this video, it’s important to look at them in context. A monthly wage of 10,000 RMB is actually above what many 1–3-year designers earn at fashion brands in China, and it’s much higher than salaries offered in smaller cities or rural areas. Converted to 1,350–1,500 USD, and combined with China’s lower tax rates, the take-home pay is often higher than what early-career designers make in several Eastern European markets.

So presenting these salaries as “very low” is misleading when compared to real conditions in the fashion industry. 14 hours working with 2 hours lunch break and 1,5 hours dinner break is not 14 hours of work time its 10.5 hours. how many factories did you check that have 14 hours working hours and how long are the lunch and dinner breaks and can the worker still decide if he wants to work or is he forced to work? 

Also, the dramatic background music creates an artificially negative tone, which can influence how viewers perceive the information. It gives the impression that the video may be edited to make SHEIN look worse than the actual data suggests, likely to drive stronger reactions and more views.

author: @TheShatteredGem
text:
It’s really hard not to buy fast fashion in America. Thrift stores don’t always have my size or my style, so the alternatives are limited. And honestly, a lot of the big brands people think are “better”  like Target, Walmart, Zara, American Eagle, Old Navy, and even some expensive athletic brands like Lululemon still use overseas factories with low wages and questionable working conditions. So even if it isn’t exactly like Shein, the system behind it isn’t completely clean either.

I also can’t justify paying $200 for a jacket that’s supposed to last 10 years when something cheaper from Walmart or Shein lasts just as long for me personally. It feels like no matter what, you’re stuck choosing between affordability and ethics, and most people don’t have the money, including me to pick the ethical option every time.

author: @philipwong8249
text:
There is a massive cultural misunderstanding in these comments. You see 'victims', but the reality is these are often highly skilled, seasonal contractors working on a piece-rate basis.

A skilled worker here can make 700-800 RMB (approx. $100-110 USD) per day during peak seasons. That is actually higher than many white-collar office graduates in the city. They choose to live cheaply in 'urban villages' and work long hours voluntarily to maximize savings. Their goal is clear: work hard for a few years, save a large sum, and return to their hometowns to build a big house and live a comfortable life. This is active, calculated ambition, not passive slavery.

author: @Papitoti
text:
As a European, my favorite kind of person is a leftist that protests every week while fully dressed in Shein clothes.

Also, I'm sure salaries and working conditions will increase once China has bought up enough Western competitors. I find it odd that sensational content creators always highlight these kind of scenarios without revisiting them after a couple of years. I remember seeing videos like this of other Chinese industries and now those workers are thriving. 

But yeah, it's easy to judge if you haven't seen how the majority of people on this planet have to live on a daily basis. I was born in a city that has looked like it does now for hundreds of years, Beijing looked like a tiny village less than 50 years ago... Why the razor thin margins? BECAUSE OTHERWISE THEY WOULDN'T STAND A CHANCE TO COMPETE. And considering that Western production is collapsing because of greed, their margins will slowly go up in the upcoming years.

Things in China are getting better for the people, while we are starting to lose benefits in Europe that made life so 'easy'.

author: @fabliharaisa
text:
To everyone who hasn’t stood in those workers’ shoes, I want to share from my own experience in my country:

Coming from a place with extreme corruption, no government support or subsidies, and where at least 30% of the population (likely more) lives below the poverty line, industries like these are often the only reason some families have a roof over their heads or even one meal a day. They provide employment where there is otherwise none.

If people stop buying from companies like SHEIN or Temu, many of these workers: women, children, and entire families—would lose even the small income they currently rely on. For many, that income is the difference between surviving and starving.

Please remember that these individuals aren’t just struggling for basic needs
they are fighting for survival.

author: @AlyssaBowles
text: 广东那边制衣厂基本都是这样，不只是shein的代工厂，工人都是计件制，没进厂的工人都是去小作坊，哪有货就去哪家做临时工，做多少给多少。流水线上的工人基本都没有底薪，大部分工人都是学历比较低的或者农民工，对劳动法的基本都有概念。有货的时候，为了多做一些，基本早上不到8点就会开工，中午吃个饭就回来接着干，晚上干到十一点下班是非常正常的事情，所以大部分工人都没什么“加班”的概念，更别提加班费。 就算进了厂的工人，如果不是什么大厂，基本来五险一金都没有的。因为我爸妈就是在广州干了一辈子的制衣厂工人（现在还没退休），年轻的时候自己也开过厂。

author: @1null57
text:
According to the man being interviewed in the video, 
All the range wages should actually be regarded as the minimum. 

For example,7000 CNY - 8500 CNY should be considered as 7000 CNY.
Based on that, the minimum wage shown in the video is 5500 CNY.
Those job with a salary of 5500 CNY involves job contents such as cutting wires, packaging, miscellaneous work, and flattening semi-finished products(Jobs that require almost no professional skills).
And The median salary in China is 4,485 CNY.

So, what do you mean by "low salary"?

According to the exchange rate 5500 CNY≈763 USD
According to the Purchasing Power Parity in different criteria 5500 CNY≈1617 USD-2750 USD

So, again, what do you mean by "low salary"?

Although every child in the office desires everyone to live by drink coffee and use the computers, But  the vast majority of the products you uses are not produced by drinking coffee and using computers.

So, what does a harsh working environment refer to? 
Does it refer to the scattered products and materials which lying on the ground?

According to the interview, the working hours for piece-rate jobs depend on the target salary.
If the project deadline is approaching, the factory will require overtime work or complete more products as soon as possible. 
However, the salary remains independent of the time and is only related to the production quantity.

So, You mean that a group of people，who earn more money than the median wage through long periods of physical labor ，produce goods that are affordable to those with lower wages than median wage, Is it a bad thing?

I don't know.

I hope that all the products that claim to be sturdy, well-made, high quality and fashionable can be sold in a price that people with wages below the median can afford to purchase.

Rather than promoting expensive, low quality and luxuriou fashion through ideological method.

I hope everyone can earn more by work harder, 
and the prices all around the world can keep dropping. 
Love and Peace.

author: @찐아이디어즈
text:
I am Korean and travel to China twice a year, mainly to attend the Canton Fair. Watching this video has given me a lot to think about.

In Korea, many job seekers can rather easily earn around 17,000–20,000 RMB per month. They usually work only five days a week, and even then, their working hours rarely exceed 10 to 12 hours a day.

Delivery workers in Korea earn about 35,000–40,000 RMB per month, typically working six days a week for 10 to 12 hours each day.

Since I started importing products from China, I’ve become very interested in both the country and its people. I am also quite familiar with Shein as a company.

Given the demographic structure, I’m not sure how quickly these issues can be resolved. But seeing how many Chinese people struggle in their daily lives has given me a lot to reflect on.

Thank you for this meaningful video.

After reading some of the comments, all I have to say is, that ppl in the US usually and this happens to all kinds of videos, just judge as if all countries are like US and if something is there it will definitely exist elsewhere. author: @brownenerdygurl
text:
As a paramedic, in NC, I used to work 24 HR shifts, with breaks, IF I could get them. So did the firefighters. Although, we let them sleep if we didnt need them. 

Most of us had 2nd jobs or worked an extra 12hrs, on our days off to make ends meet.  One week 72hrs, the other 96hrs. No OT unless you pick up extra shifts. 
The funny thing is, if we got a break long enough to get fast food, citizens would say things like "Im not paying you to sit around and eat." But, only to EMS. Fire and police got free coffee. 
Still, I never made any medication errors. 
Ain't saving lives great! 

Thanks to Ronald Reagan, emergency workers aren't allowed to strike in "right to work" states.   

There were days I drove home, and couldn't remember how I got there. Or I was too tired to get out of the car. I would wake up in my driveway with my foot still on the break. 

So, buy an EMS worker a coffee. They probably need it. Luckily, some EMS departments are phasing 24s out, finally! Hey, this is America.

author: @bmk8018
text: It's easy for Westerners, from a position of privilege, to view the situation with disgust and judgment. And sure, that's understandable. no one would want to be in such conditions. However, reducing China's economic boom to just cheap labor and poor working conditions oversimplifies the issue and disregards the progress China has made in those areas. Every developed country has gone through similar phases at some point, and it’s not fair to impose our privileged standards on China without considering the potential unintended consequences, which could end up being worse than the current situation. It's easy to virtue signal when your GDP per capita is four to five times higher than the country you’re critiquing. While China is the second-largest economy by total GDP, it still lags far behind on a per capita basis compared to modern industrialized Western nations. Once countries reach a certain level of wealth, they can improve their standards of living, but imagine if we only had a fifth of our current income or GDP. our quality of life would be drastically reduced. So, this isn’t about justifying these conditions but about trying to understand them without oversimplifying or creating a caricature of an entire industry based on the perspective of one company. Drawing hasty conclusions or policies could lead to even worse outcomes for those affected.

现实就是世界根本没那么多资源和财富让每个人都活得很好，何况中国是近代被侵略、被殖民、被掠夺已久的国家。还要在面对充满制造业优势的欧美和它们制定的对它们自己有利的规则下发展，这种情况下，中国能发展成现在这个样子，能创造出一个可以孕育出海企业、让14亿人的劳动人口能有最低保障的工作机会的就业市场，能有比较有序的社会环境让你能制作这个视频，都已经是个奇迹。

我不知道消费者aware了这些又能怎样，团结起来抵制Shein、抵制中国制造吗？让它们都倒了乱了毁灭了，然后呢？发达国家继续压制中国，中国老百姓无工作可做？你自己又获得了什么？作为自媒体频道，通过Shein这个关键字，获得了一波大流量和订阅吗？

author: @agirlwhosalsoadog
text: Watching this as a privileged American fashion designer, my heart aches for them. I did my first runway show at 15, and that being said I am against any child doing this level of technical work. I hurt myself many times during my teen years preparing for shows, running on no sleep. No one should have to exist this way unless they’re doing it out of joy for the work, not necessity. It will break your soul when you have orders to fulfill on a creatively resigned day. Humans are meant to create when Inspiration strikes, not at the whims of a corporation and trend cycle rapidly speeding up. I have pulled the 8am to 10pm shifts at my machine, but the difference is I CHOSE to do so out of passion. No one should have to work this job for that amount of money. We are engineers, architects, and artists. My degree cost me over 30k and I’m still not done yet. This is modern day slavery for clothing that becomes waste in less than a year. Clothing that pollutes our water with microplastics and our air during manufacturing.

author: @LauraCastillo-ef2so
text: The irony is that in poor countries, Shein clothing is still not the most "affordable" option. Developed countries are actually the ones who buy the most fast fashion and discard it the next day. In my country, the textile industry is also like this. The people who sew are called "satellites", And they're paid approximately $0.19 per garment sewn. My mom used to work on one of those satellites, but it's not worth it because they don't even provide the machine (my mom had to use hers). And yet my country is full of clothes from China, and local brands have gone bankrupt because people expect clothes to be very cheap.

author: @SamanthaNoelle44
text: 7:55 while this is clearly unethical business practices on SHEIN’s part, it’s playing on people’s emotions to make a comparison to USD. It’s failing to highlight that it’s substantially cheaper to live in this part of China than any city or town in the US. The average cost of living modestly in or around Guangzhou is around $7000 CNY a month. SHEIN isn’t ethical and most Chinese clothing manufacturers aren’t, which is why I’ve been frustrated as someone who lives in North America that our companies have sold out to “Made in China.” It’s all been for the bottom line without regard to how workers in other countries are treated, or how we are losing our own skilled labor. If the demand goes away people stop the supply. I also assumed that SHEIN’s insanely low prices meant their ethical standards were low, or non existent, so I’ve consciously chosen not to buy from them. Also, I prefer quality over quantity. Thank you for the video - this is good insider reporting. But, more transparency around cost of living would build more trust in the point you’re trying to get the viewer to believe and understand.

author: @KKKK-sr1bd

I saw many people declaring 'I won't buy from Shein' and act like 'not buying' can solve any problem without thinking how to realistically help the workers getting higher paying jobs instead of having no jobs, which is the main issue here. Chinese job market is very competitive and they ended up working in Shein because they didn't have better choices...Shein at least gave them some income to make a living, and it's not forced labor or child labor. If people really want to help these worker in China, simply 'not buying from Shein' won't solve any problem and could probably make their situation worser as the company might fire some wokers if the business makes less profit...are you able to offer all of them higher paying jobs after that？

I appreciate this video and I hope Shein can increase the workers' salaries but I really don't think 'not buying from Shein' is an act of kindness, as Shein gave those workers an income source while those declared to be kind want to destroy their current job without providing anything new and better to those hard working workers, it's always easier said than done.

text: 作为海外中国人，我购买过一两次shein上的商品，只是没买过服装。但其他商品的质量也低得难以想象，在中国花同样的钱可以在淘宝等平台买到好很多的商品（尽管我也不能确定它们背后有没有同样严重的压榨行为），我拆开快递的一瞬间就在想，“Shein确保了全世界继续以中国制造为低质量的代名词”。但是作为收入有限，且本身就不怎么有非必需品（对我而言，食品和书）以外消费需求的人，我突然觉得很迷茫，从Zara，H&M等快时尚到我并负担不起的奢侈品牌，我不知道哪种服装产业的生态是健康的。（目前而言，我能做的就是在现有消费条件下尽量可持续，比如我2024年一共在服装上消费50欧元，包括一件打折风衣，三条内裤，5双从中国转运来的袜子，还有一条围巾，除了内衣可能会多更换一下，其他的我能穿很久，现在衣柜里还有30多年前我妈妈穿过的短裤，也有我十年前买的T恤，质量真的很不错。）另外我很难过地想到，我连鸡蛋都买不起打着“bio”标签的，生态放养鸡下的蛋，只买最便宜的笼养鸡蛋。消费能力和社会责任感真的割裂。

author: @meredithoconnor3701
text: Unfortunately, this is not new or unique for the industry as I’ve learned over the years. As an American online shopper, I’ve thought about this systemic issue a lot. I personably don’t actually care how long a garment takes to get to me, as long as I get it before it sells out. If an item sells out, I assume there isn’t enough time in that season to produce more. But I would still buy that item if it was in stock. I know capitalism doesn’t work like this, but my best suggestion is for brands to offer mandatory pre-orders. They advertise their select samples, we place pre-orders and we get them in 3-6 months, after the orders are produced. This way brands are not overproducing, causing more waste and unnecessary work. Slow down fast fashion. No more “sold outs,” nor more rush orders. Just making the amount and specific items people actually want.

author: @_axetrax1
text:
i think some people are not exposed everyday to the nature of factories, ethical or otherwise, so they see anything with a factory aesthetic and see it as a human rights violation just because they have such a dingy industrialized look to them. well, factory work can be honest and well paying work in western countries so I'd like people to know that it can be a real job given the chance. in this factory, it seems like the conditions are fine but the only issue is the pay rate.

A lot of people tend to blame the corporations but this is a real situation of 'voting with your dollar' being what really matters when societally we've succumbed to a passive acceptance of any items imported from china without having any cultural opposition to it. honestly, i would pay more money on clothes if a higher price meant it went in the pockets of the workers, but i know even the pricier products are only going to be pocketed by the western higherups so its an ideal world i dont live in. right now the only real protest possibilities is to thrift, purchase from local manufacturers within your own country, or make your own clothing. 

I think some people are also a little naive about how many of their products are sourced from an unethical factory... truthfully, every product around you and even the one you watch this video on originates that way so long as they aren't produced in your home country. boycotting specific brands can't solve the issue if the issue is colonization deep rooted in all of our manufacturing practices. i dont even feel like i have the right to virtue signal this to others when i am also complicit in many ways to this economic disparity in my purchasing choices.

author: @Wells-k5j
text:
I am a textile industry professional who has worked at Shein for five years.
China's textile and garment industry faces widespread and systemic issues: low wages, poor treatment, inadequate welfare systems, and widespread non-compliance with labor laws. Workers face high costs and barriers in defending their rights, making it difficult to sue employers. As a result, the government largely turns a blind eye to these practices.

It's not just Shein—suppliers for brands like JCPenney, Nike, GAP, and Old Navy also engage in similar tactics: cutting workers’ benefits in order to maintain profit margins.

Workers often don't get weekends off or receive overtime pay. They routinely work 12 hours or more per day. If they are injured on the job, compensation is minimal.

However, when American clients visit the factories for inspections, the factories often falsify records—hiding evidence of weekend overtime and instructing workers to lie. If a worker accidentally reveals the truth during an inspection, they are likely to be fined or even fired. These are desperate measures taken by factory owners simply to maintain—not increase—their profits, because the Chinese textile industry is under significant pressure.

Although Chinese labor is cheap compared to developed countries, it is still costly relative to Southeast Asia or Central America. To secure orders, Chinese factories are forced to continuously cut costs. The textile industry is not considered a high-priority sector by the government, so it receives little policy support. Workers’ welfare is neglected, and factories are pushed out of urban areas under the banner of "industrial optimization," relegated to regions with poorer conditions.

At a deeper level, I believe the root cause lies in the government's failure to assess whether labor resources and industrial structures in coastal cities are truly compatible. Industrial relocation policies have been implemented blindly, without considering the fate of low-income workers. As a result, these workers have no choice but to sacrifice their own well-being in exchange for a chance to survive in the city.

author: @Cinthia_Lin
text:
I’m truly humbled by the overwhelming response to this video—thank you all for watching, sharing, and caring deeply about these issues. I wanted to address some of the most common comments:

1)To those asking about my safety: Thank you for your concern! I’m completely safe. ☺ Many of the issues discussed in this video are already openly debated on Chinese social media, and I actually drew a lot of insights from those discussions while making this video.

2) Will there be more investigations like this? Honestly, I’m not sure. While I believe in shedding light on these topics, I also recognize that labor conditions in global manufacturing—not just at Shein—are deeply tied to complex supply and demand forces. Real change takes time.

3)To those rethinking their shopping habits: It means so much to hear that this video has made some of you more aware. If there’s one takeaway, it’s that ALL fast fashion—not just Shein—often relies on labor conditions that may be far worse than what most consumers realize.

Again, thank you for engaging with this discussion so thoughtfully. Your comments have given me a lot to reflect on!🙂

Its a global problem. In my country with your avarage payment, you can go to three places to buy clothes. Local fast fashion shops (like New Yorker), second hand places, or shein. Second hand stores are getting more expensive as well. So with our payments we go up on shein, where we can get that same bad quality but more cheaper. I learned as a tailor, I worked in the industry, and left because it was so underpeayed in my country as well. And I have a friend from there who is a literal talent if its about making clothes. He is amazing, but to make good and ethical garments, its very pricey, and there are specific people who are going to him to tailoring, and its definietly not me with my avarage payment. And not me not my friends are happy we are buying from a place like shein, but we cant do better. Sometimes I thinking it would be nice to sew my own clothes, but I work in three different job, and currently studying, because I want to renovate my home, and there is NO time, to do it myself. :(
If people would get better vages, probably would stop buying from shein,  but right now, this is the cheapest, easiest and fastest way to buy clothes... The root of the problem is at the consumption side.

author: @kimjong-un8361
text:
The world is not fair, you know. Countries like Saudi Arabia can live like millionaires just by digging up oil, while the United States can simply print money and buy things from all over the world. The situation for Chinese workers is tough, yet it’s still better than in most other developing countries. That’s because China produces its own affordable industrial goods instead of relying on imports. In contrast, other developing nations have to import such goods from China, which makes the cost of living much higher.

At the same time, China has good public security, social stability, and decent infrastructure. Even in Vietnam, people have to import basic necessities like toilet paper and stainless steel basins from China—because of China’s scale and industrial supply chains, it’s actually cheaper to produce these items in China than in Vietnam.

For example, a DJI Neo drone sells for less than 2,000 RMB in China, but in Argentina, the price can be as high as 8,000 RMB. Now imagine countries plagued by mafia and organized crime—China’s stability and infrastructure are far ahead by comparison.

We Chinese people are not afraid of hard work; what we fear most is unemployment.If you stop buying, workers will lose their jobs, and that is what will truly worsen their living conditions. Companies like Shein will simply move their factories to other countries where the working environment is even worse.

author: @kimjong-un8361
text:
As a Chinese person, I sometimes feel genuinely saddened when I see these Chinese workers. They endure extremely long working hours with hardly any complaints, are exploited by capitalists, and are blamed by Western politicians for “stealing” jobs from the West. What’s worse, some companies now see Chinese wages as too high and move to countries with lower wages but much lower efficiency. As a result, Chinese workers lose their jobs and are forced to beg capitalists for another pitiful position—one that likely pays even less.
Despite all this, living and working conditions today are still much better than they were a decade ago. Around 15 years ago, it was common in China for teenagers who didn’t get into good high schools to go out and work. It’s only in recent years that this trend has improved, with society placing more importance on education, and more young people attending university in hopes of escaping that kind of life.Perhaps only when automation and robots become widespread will the working people of the world finally be liberated.

author: @kimjong-un8361

author: @ArtistFormallyKnownasMC
text:
I’ve watched about half of this video. I’ve got to move on to other things. But I just wanted to leave a comment like several others- excellent reporting. You have good insight and are brave to dip your toe in enough to learn more. Thank you for presenting this to the world. This is actual journalism. Not propaganda from the influencer grift we saw in 2023 when Shein sent influencers to China and put their best face forward. 
I’ve never bought from SHEIN or Temu and I don’t plan to. But I know that doesn’t make any of us innocent in the US. I try to do research, but it’s tough. Things are global. Manufacturing is global. It’s good and bad. We want better for people at the other end of the supply chain. But it’s lose lose. For us, for the workers, for the environment. It’s a shame.

author: @jennyli1468
text: In China, taxi and DiDi drivers have to work all day to make 4-7k a month. My cousin who is a pharmacist makes 8k a month as a contractor. It has been 8 years she hasn't got a raise. My other cousin makes 4k/ low income working for the government environment department only.  Pay rates are generally low if you didn't get into a well-known HS and get into a brand name college. MOST of people didn't make it and have to go to trade schools/HS then you can apply for a no brand name college. You can't really get far. The exams in China are extremely difficult, worst than SAT and ACT. You have to be really smart. In order for you to make good money, you need to be educated or you run a successful business. It's competitive everywhere and everyone works hard. It is part of the culture and working overtime is normal. If you want to call sick, you need to find your own replacement by switching your schedule with your coworkers. They make us look lazy, we care so much work life balance here but it's not like that in China. They just push through for their families. My uncle is a billionaire and he used to work super hard as  vice president. He is genius and graduated as a Chemical engineer. He also has 30 years worth of investing into stocks. He took classes and put his heart and soul into learning. He deserves that life.  If you are not a high achiever, you will live a hard life period.

author: @tim2098sjw
text: 这个世界不过是一部分人获利，一部分人失利罢了。诚然，为工人发声，看起来很正义，但如果像外国的工会那样，动不动罢工，游行，要求加薪，其他跟这个行业不相关的人的利益，不就受影响了。举个例子，澳大利亚train工会此前经常罢工要求加薪，动不动就减少班次，增加班次之间的间隔时间，动不动就是trackwork，这对于普通人的通勤来说，有巨大的影响，那你说，工会工人的福利是福利，通勤人的福利就不是福利了么。国内的trackwork基本不会影响普通民众的出行。总有人得牺牲，总有人要负重前行，不然社会怎么岁月静好。人类是社会性的物种，必然会产生不平等。说句难听的，你努力学习，工作，上进，看起来好像很正能量，其实也是在创造不平等。社会的机会和资源是有限的，你努力了，得到了这份资源，这个机会，这个position，被你占据了，其他人就少了这个机会和这份资源，其他人就只能想办法另外找，那他此前付出的这份努力，就浪费了，你能100%说你比其他人更加hardworking，付出的努力更加有价值吗？你的努力是努力，别人的努力就不是努力了？再者，所谓hardworking，劳动的价值，如何定义，谁来定义，谁有定义权和话语权？如果学国外，搞得好像很公平，一天天的不是这个群体发声求福利，就是那个群体要求加薪。动乱，闹事，罢工，游行，乌烟瘴气的，然后整个社会发展缓慢，效率低下，国力衰弱，然后外国势力就可以欺压，可以bully你，不要忘了，整体和个体，都是相对的，在国家与国家之间，如果人人都是精致利己主义，那这个国家，在其他国家面前，他的利益就少了。需要我提世界上那些或多或少有点乱的国家作为example么。再者，看看英国，造个高铁，造了多少年了，只造了一部分，甚至还没造完。效率极其低下，你要说公平，那这样对英国普通民众，不公平啊，他们为什么没有权利没有资格去享受到21世纪新时代的高速列车呢，还不是因为追求所谓的公平，一会儿这个投票，一会儿那个议会要开会表决，要么就是钉子户解决不了，建筑工人的权利要保障，高铁路线上的人的house拆迁，利益要保障，嗯，是保障了，造了10年了吧，还没造好，那本来可以享受到高铁的其他民众的利益呢，谁来保障。如果中国为了一部分人的利益，而放弃了china manufacturing 的快速发展，那当下的关税战，中国就没有底气发表never kneel down这样的视频，就没有底气反抗trump，反抗美国。一个集体的整体实力，决定一切。如果人人都reasonable工资，哪儿来的低成本，低价格，不用低价格，怎么打入已经成熟的市场，可能么。中国曾经站在世界之巅，保持了上千年，近代的百年耻辱史，中国人受够了。曾经的西方，也有很多这样的血汗工厂，过去了那么多时间，他们发展完了，现在拿人权说事，可我们中国还没发展好呢，我们要发展回当年的世界第一，这样不会被western people bully，要平等对话，而不是西方站的高高得，颐指气使得去评判，批判。等到中国人均GDP跟国外一样了，那这样的所谓‘血汗工厂’，自然而然就没了。那时，中国人也都会去追求高福利，因为那个时候，‘血汗工厂’，会出现在其他国家。整个地球，整个人类，也是一个整体，总得有国家负重前行，才能有国家，岁月静好。当下的能源体系，地球上是没办法做到大家都一样的。除非发展到高一级别的文明，到达星际文明的级别，可控核聚变商业化可以运作，不再依赖化石能源，也不用依靠当下效率不高，成本还偏高的renewable energy。也许可以。但是，我表示怀疑，人这种生物，即使科技发展到了那个程度，一样会有不平等。

author: @zhangburnham7226
text: This is not news. The profit margin in the textile industry is extremely low. In order to make a profit, factory owners must exploit their employees. In the 1970s and 1980s, people in South Korea and Taiwan also worked 15 hours a day. Later, it moved to China, and now it is moving to Vietnam, India, and Bangladesh. If you think that not buying their clothes is helping these employees, you are wrong. Once the factory owners feel that they can't make money, they will close the factory and the workers will lose their jobs. The textile industry provides a large number of jobs, which means that a considerable number of people will lose their income. This is why these employees insist on working even though they know they are being exploited. As long as there are poor people in this world, this will not change.

author: @aaronneng
text:
在全球消费链条中，谁是真正的受益者？我们常常看到像Shein这样的中国品牌因“低价”而被质疑，可很少有人反过来看：是谁在享受这些低价？
美国消费者可以买到几美元一件的衣服，而背后支撑这个价格的，是中国工人的辛劳与高强度付出。这并不是剥削的问题，而是全球分工的现实。我们是一个勤劳的民族，但如果连这些“苦”都不能承受，我们的就业、我们的产业链就会遭受更大打击。
说到底，Shein价格低的背后，并非单靠压榨，而是在为全球市场尤其是美国消费者“补贴”。某种程度上，美国享受了超值的商品，中国承担了劳动力成本。这是一种不对等的交换，而我们在其中苦撑的，不只是价格，还有生存与尊严。 希望你好好当一个中国人，不是香蕉人！

author: @winwinogaming
text: Cinthia, I have worked in the garment and consumer goods manufacturing business for 20 years both in and outside of China. A big reason why Shein and Temu can price so low is that their products have been shipped directly to consumers, bypassing safeguards put in place. For example in the US and EU there are laws against forced labor in the supply chain. There are laws regarding product safety. Laws regarding prohibited materials and chemicals. Normally these are checked randomly on goods as they enter the country and when they are on store shelves. But Shein and Temu can bypass this. It means Shein and Temi are responsible for policing themselves, and as you have seen this is very questionable. I would encourage you to find a way to meet and tour a large-scale garment producer, one that works for major overseas retailers and brands. You will see a MAJOR difference in how they are operated.

author: @lucyanderson9064
text:
Just want to start this conversation by stating that I'm poor, and disabled.  I already live well below the poverty line.  So what I'm saying is not coming from a place of 'privilege'.  I'm also a lifelong activist in more than one country.  But we need to stop making, and buying cheap things.  I don't buy things because they are cheap.  I buy what is quality, what will last, what is non toxic, and safe, and what I love.  I would rather have few things of high quality, that don't harm the environment, or people, than many things of low quality. We can say all we like about providing jobs etc, but these jobs frankly, never should exist.  Ditto to 'fast fashion'.  We need to supply sustainable ways of doing things, and then provide those jobs, AND compensate people properly.  

With the current state of the world, and what we know about something like plastic pollution alone, these things should not be allowed.  Full stop.  Thinking that we 'need' them, because 'what will poor people buy?' is the very classism that they think they are trying to avoid.  So, poor people should not have safe, quality things?  But dangerous, cheap things?  Just because they are poor?  This was not the case historically.  If you don't believe me, you can research it.  Poor people appreciated quality, and had nice things.  They made things last.  It's only a western, modern idea that we need to provide tons of cheap, toxic, low quality things for consumers to buy in surplus, and then throw away.  Never use to be a thing.  People valued what they had, and it wasn't necessarily cheap.  We need to rethink how we do everything, and raise the bar.  I never buy from these companies.  I need to know that what I'm supporting, is something good, and I also need to know that the product I am using does not have any harmful ingredients etc.

author: @wzep263
text:
Two cents of mine to this YouTuber and all who shared their thoughts here:

"It was not by gold or by silver, but by labour, that all wealth of the world was originally purchased."  Read deep behind the lines of this famous quote of Adam Smith in his book The Wealth of Nations. 

Every exchange, whether it's among individuals or corporations or nations, any acts of supply and demand is determined by how each side in the exchange cycle weigh the value of what they give and what they receive from the exchange!

Those factories are not prison cells. No one there has been forced to work at gun points. To work over time or not, they have a choice. So, please have a more balanced view to see how deals are made or break in a commercial world. 

The very simple saying "Money makes the world go round" is naked truth. When you are a consumer, don't you want all prices of things you fancy to buy are 50% cheaper? As a seller, don't you want it the other way round??? Simple life logic here. Just my two cents!

author: @wayne0316
text: It’s disappointing how she manipulates the narrative. She shows piece rate pay only in subtitles, never bothering to explain it in the voiceover, as if hoping her audience won’t notice the difference. She deliberately conflates piecework with overtime, misleading viewers into thinking the workers are being ruthlessly overworked. Then she ignores China’s cost of living entirely and converts everything into USD to make the wages look shockingly low by Western standards. This isn’t shedding light on labor issues, it’s manufacturing outrage for clicks. If you’re going to talk about exploitation, at least don’t exploit the truth in the process.

author: @vixxcelacea2778
text:
Not only are the conditions inhumane and the entire thing is horrific and a great example of everything wrong in our system, the clothes are also not safe. Big surprise that crappy dangerous conditions produce also crappy dangerous clothes (And that's not in anyway a dig on the people working on them. They are insanely overworked, underpaid and given the cheapest materials to cut costs further. The crappy quality isn't on them, it's on the company entirely) The thrift store I volunteered at started to throw away anything from Shein because of the danger the clothes can possess. And it costs them money to throw out clothes they could otherwise sell.

Don't buy from places like this if you can afford to.

And that there, that's the caveat. Poverty feeds poverty. 50%+ of the world is poor to lower class. 

There are also sometimes labor/regulation places in things like this, but they are undermanned and very underfunded so that on paper companies can declare that they're following according to inspection (which never happens almost and when it does, they fake everything.)

Also, super wealthy "high end" stuff does this too. Import non-local language speaking migrants. Human trafficking sometimes plays a part too. Gucci, Chanel, ya know, the stuff to make middle class people feel like they're wealthy when truly wealthy people shop at places we've all never heard of and brand names are for the masses to think they're part of the bourgeois by making you pay for the mark up of the "elite". Because money is what matters because it's access to resources. Money matters. It's why things like this are so heart breaking. You die with out money.

Living is not a right and never has been. Though we try to pretend we all think that, we certainly don't put our money where our mouth is.

The world has more slavery than ever and it sucks that people don't know about it to at least wag their finger at the corporations making bank off of missing fingers, child labor, slow diseases and the like that these places cause.

author: @genniebay6845
text:
just to put things into perspective—if you're being paid $0.05 per piece and earned $1,227 in a month, that means you had to produce about 24,540 pieces that month. Assuming you worked 20 days, that’s roughly 1,227 pieces per day.

If you worked 12 hours a day (including an hour for meals), that breaks down to about 111 pieces per hour, or nearly 1.8 pieces per minute—essentially one piece every 33 seconds, non-stop.

It’s hard to overstate how intense and relentless that is. Imagine doing this every single day, for months or even years, with little to no time or energy left to pursue anything else or improve your situation. If that’s not a tough reality, I honestly don’t know what is.

author: @Therese.Backman
text:
The commercial in the background with models on billboards is giving me hunger-games vibes. 

As a worker in Sweden I wake up at 06 with my kids, taking them to school, then I have a breakfast break for 30 min - 1 hour before starting work. I finish between 18 and 20.30 and it takes half an hour to get home (before I moved it took 1 hour with bus).
Some days I am off work completely and then I pick up my kids from school around 1 pm. During those days I clean the house, do grocery shopping and wash clothes.
I work in retail & they dont give us fulltime jobs.
 I work every second weekend aswell, home between 17-19.
I get paid so I can pay my rent, the bills and buy food. i only buy clothes second hand and sometimes fast fashion (like hm). I buy skincare frequently and makeup occasionally. I buy gifts for my kids on their birthdays and on Christmas. I get by and I think my life is very good compared to others but I don't love in luxury and I don't have any money in my bank account. To buy gifts to my children at Christmas I save 6 months in advance. I never go on holidays with my kids and travel abroad. I am a single mother of 2 without any drivers licence (cause I could never afford it) and my rent is 1350 $/month. I rent an appartement and I have to share bedroom with one of my kids because I can't afford a bigger flat. Food costs around 800-1000$/month because the prices kept going up but not our sallarys so much. I think my life is good, but it's not luxury and I live in Sweden in Europe

author: @monicagibbs7265
text: I spent the first 20 years of my career working in a sewing factory of one kind or another. In 1973 I started in a pants factory making a minimum wage of 1.60 an hour. The only way to make more was to make production, but the production rate would be raised if you started to make a decent amount. Fast forward to the year 1991 same wage base minimum wage’s until production was made the most I ever made was 5.00 an hour until they completely changed the sequence of having the garment made, therefore making it harder to make production. All sewing factory use slave labor no matter where they are. The United States was where I worked in a small South Georgia town.  I went to school became a LPN and didn’t make much more than working as a seamstress.

How can you understand? These people have different lives from you. The world is complicated and not as simple as raising salaries, although that'd be nice, who wouldn't like that? But ffs, stop judging as if you're making a difference in the world. However SHEIN is "diabolical" to you, at least they're providing work opportunities for these people--not you. Also, the factories are outsourced, meaning they're not owned by SHEIN (read the video description.) You people are just looking for reasons to hate the company. For what benefit? Western companies have been exploiting low-cost labor for decades in developing countries and you people are still supporting it for decades, too!

P.S. I don't care for SHEIN as a company, heck, they don't even sell stuffs where I'm at. I'm just sick of people believing that their saints simply by making judgments. Cringe.

author: @tttoria
text:
Some reminders for people in the comments session:
1. In 2024, Chinese national residents' per capita disposable income is 41,314 yuan. That is 3500 per day. I would say 7000-8000 per month is already way higher than average, though. 

2. I would not say the labor problem has something to do with the quality of the cloth, and I would not say that other than Shein other fast fashion companies produce better quality clothes (for example, Zara...)

3. Heavy labor is everywhere, and this is how many developing countries grow their economy. It is true that governments should help provide more protection and restrictions, but if you see this kind of video and refuse to buy from all companies like this, I doubt where you can still buy fair-priced clothes, and people in developing countries might lose their jobs.

4. In other words, it is NAIVE to think refusing to buy stuff or banning a couple of companies won't truly help those people; you just get more lies and fake values (which they never do what they said) from companies, and you are going to pay for them. THERE IS NOTHING YOU WESTERNERS CAN DO ABOUT THIS. IF YOU REALLY CARE, DONATE SOME MONEY TO SOME INTERNATIONAL LABOR FORCE PROTECTION INSTITUTES/ORGANIZATIONS.

author: @dianalovescolors
text: I placed my last Shein order right before the tarriffs went into place. I pre recorded my video to upload to my channel this week. I often order from Shein for my YouTube channel. I'm an American woman who's shopped at Shein for a long time. Currently the tarriffs are stopping me from ordering. Shein has increased prices as a way to get around this, predicting that American shoppers will still place orders because of our shopping addictions to their low prices. However I've talked to many other content creators who are not going to order from Shein anymore. Shein has increased the number of times they sent me texts and emails. Numerous times a day they are flashing sales or letting me know that items in my cart are almost sold out. This bombardment of contact is designed to lure you to the website. I can shut off the notifications but I've learned to just ignore them. After seeing the awful working conditions and listening to the workers say they'll have to work 12 and 14 hour days, I won't be putting any more orders. With their sales down they'll just put more pressure on these poor people to work even longer hours.  The workers are just trying to survive. I'm sure there's a Temu village out there somewhere too.

author: @punxsutawneyphilofficial
text:
I previously worked for a small, family run upholstery factory in the US. The conditions there weren't far off from this. We were more organized with less pieces on the floor or overflowing, but we still had unreasonable quotas, low wages, and a massive wealth disparity between the owners and workers. 3 brothers owned it and at least one of the brothers owns a ~620K house. Most of my coworkers were renting or living with family (including myself). The owners of the factory would pull up in BMWs while the workers had beat up Nissans. 

It was for a company with MUCH higher profit margins too. The brand would sell office chairs for about $1K a pop. There'd be dayS where I sewed hundreds of office chair seats and brought home maybe $700/week before taxes. I totaled an order I was making once of 94 chairs and they would have been about $120K. I made $140 that day (before taxes). We also had to do mandatory overtime of an extra 5-10hrs/week. The money was nice but it takes an extreme toll on your body. 

Health hazard wise, we had an entire wall covered with black mold that the owners said they'd take care of, I got carpal tunnel from repetitive sewing but had to continue working through it, and one of my coworkers once sewed over her finger and the needle got stuck. She pulled it out with some pliers and kept going. We may get paid more, but working conditions in American factories aren't sunshine and rainbows either.

author: @TheNo-God
text:
Developed countries rely on siphoning capital from developing nations,
crafting the illusion of freedom at the top of civilization.

Developing countries, in turn, exploit even lower tiers of labor,
constructing the illusion of prosperity within the middle ranks.

And those nations or regions that lack the power to siphon from others
reveal the raw, unfiltered reality of this slave civilization.

The economic growth brought by globalization over the past decades
has begun to awaken the darkest truth of this system:
Even in developed countries, the lower classes can no longer sustain the illusion.
The facade of freedom is crumbling—giving way to deception and rising authoritarianism.

No one stands outside this slave civilization.
Even those who begin at the summit
will, in time, fall.

author: @TheJoyNinjaNZ
text:
I've worked inside garment factories in New Zealand just like this - cramped, piles of garments everywhere, hot, loud.  I have worked for a piece rate, where you have to be very accurate and very very fast to make any kind of living.  It is the nature of garment production and it was no different in Victorian times.  What has evolved in the 'Western world" is labour protection laws, human rights laws, child protections, etc at the same time as *rampant consumerism*  We know we can't treat workers like this our shores, but we're more than willing to turn a blind eye to other countries' labour practices to get a steady supply of cheap goods.  

The real shame is the skill and craftmanship that goes into garment production takes years to hone - it doesn't matter what country you're in you should be paid fairly!  I think its tragic that Shein's business model obscures the skill and potential a very well trained Chinese labour force has to bring beautifully made, quality items to the world.  The fact is - NZ used to have that skilled labour force but they let the industry die.

author: @believingregardlessforever
text:
When i was young in UK 1980s, we worked these factory jobs. My first job was £1/hour, start 4am at a meat factory an hour's cycle away, you got two half-hour breaks in the first eight hours but nothing afterwards and you were forced to stay as long as they wanted, usually 12-14 hours. There  were several accidents where the person left in an ambulance and we never saw them again. Now, we exported all those awful jobs overseas. 

I used to only buy fairtrade clothes, but all the fairtrade companies went bankrupt after 2008 crash or brexit (UK leaving EU). 

Also, i don't eat meat products made of waste meat parts eg sausages (i eat vegan sausages or welsh cheese ones only) and mostly only meat i know where it grew (i live in the countryside so i mean that literally) unless i'm at someone's house. i know too much!

author: @lynnimaybe
text: This is why I hate the "no ethical consumption under capitalism" concept being referred to people shopping from places like this. You don't need cute cheap trendy clothes, you don't need clothes that are brand new, you don't need that specific outfit you saw an influencer wear. Your new wardrobe comes at the expense of people getting paid less than $5 an hour for $14 hours a day in terrible conditions. It costs people lives just so you can wear something new. I know people are saying that even thrift stores have fast fashion in them, but it's still more ethical to thrift because the stores themselves are funding these terrible companies, it's all donation. So shopping there only gives money to keep running the thrift store. Do research on companies, save up and buy quality clothes, and make sure they're made out of ethical materials that will last. Buy from secondhand websites and never compromise ethics for a new outfit.

author: @ixeliema
text:
God. This is very much my "I knew it was bad but jfc" moment. I bought some swimwear and shirts via Shein back in 2019 after first hearing about them from a college friend, since I'd mentioned many of my clothes and shoes being over 7 years old and beginning to show their age. They'd sold me on the idea with their low prices, and being naïve I really didn't consider why that might have been, namely because I needed to save as much money as possible given my roommate just bailed and left me and my partner paying a rent we couldn't afford. I bought some stuff, and literally found out they were somewhat known for their practices being bad like, a week later. 

Part of me felt like "well if it's just fast fashion bad I can just make this shit last, that's exactly the opposite of what the industry pledges to be," but I had a feeling it was worse, given I found somewhat recent art theft accusations as well. I had considered returning the items, if for no other reason than personal moral absolvment, but I didn't have the energy to go through the hassle of that as a full-time student, so I kept it all. I still have all those things now, but almost entirely bc I barely wear them compared to sturdier, less shrunken, clothing I own (my favorite pair of yoga pants is 15 years old and only just now has holes). Seeing Shein get more and more exposure as a severely shitty company company over the last half a decade, I almost wish I had gone to the trouble of returning it back then, just to cause a bit of annoyance to the higher ups of Shein, but the fact is that a company that huge would never have made it their problem to deal with any consequences...plus 50 bucks is a drop in the bucket for a company like that. I just know some poor underpaid overworked person working some ungodly shift hours would've gotten stuck cleaning up the situation. I still look at those items with a sick feeling in my gut, and I still live paycheck to paycheck. Sometimes it's tempting to try to fill out my waning wardrobe with something new, especially as I am quite low on professional attire atm, but I always remember that whatever money I have and whatever life I am complaining about, I am still nowhere near the level of suffering that the people in Shein's grasp are. And that makes me so angry. But what else can I do? I wish there was anything else I could feasibly do. I sincerely appreciate the people who go to bat for those suffering 8-10s, I share those stories (as many comments have said, this is very much some incredible journalism on your part). I just avoid and encourage avoidance. But that's never going to feel like enough. I wish the average American had any more ability to help. This is just utterly miserable. What a fucking nightmare.

author: @patient-investor
text:
Thank you for this thoughtful and the behind-the-scenes look into the factories producing for Shein. Exposing these working conditions is crucial for holding brands accountable and sparking informed public debate. One point often overlooked in these conversations is the structure of Shein’s supply chain: the company doesn’t own its factories but contracts work to third-party operators. These factory owners, often local entrepreneurs can benefit from such contracts, creating a ladder of upward mobility in regions where economic opportunities are limited. While the system is far from perfect, it can offer a route out of poverty not only for workers, but also for small-scale entrepreneurs.

At the same time, we shouldn’t excuse the exploitative aspects of this model. Reports of excessive working hours, withheld wages, and unsafe conditions are deeply troubling and deserve serious scrutiny. But we also need to place this within the broader context of global manufacturing and labor. In many cases, workers in Shein-affiliated factories earn more than entry-level office workers in Chinese tier-one cities such as Shanghai, raising questions about how we define “exploitation” across different socio-economic landscapes. If our concern is truly worker dignity and fair labor, we must resist reducing this to a single villain narrative and instead confront the systemic drivers: fast fashion’s demand for speed and volume at low cost, and our collective complicity as consumers.

This broader lens brings companies like Amazon into the conversation, despite being based in highly developed nations with strict labor laws, yet still consistently criticized for abusive warehouse practices. Unlike Shein, Amazon directly employs its warehouse staff, which means it holds full accountability for working conditions. And yet, reports persist of workers being injured at high rates, facing relentless productivity quotas, and being penalized for basic human needs like bathroom breaks. In this case, there is no intermediary to absorb the blame. The exploitation is institutional and happening within legal systems that should offer greater protection. 

Food for thought to engage in dialogue

author: @GreenPartyofMalaysia
text:
Shein, a leading force in the global fast-fashion industry, continues to face significant criticism over labour rights violations within its supply chain. Despite public pledges to uphold ethical sourcing and employment standards, recent audits and investigations have uncovered ongoing issues including child labour, excessive working hours, and insufficient wages.

Child Labour Cases

In 2024, Shein disclosed the discovery of two instances of child labour within its supplier network—matching the number identified in 2023. The minors involved were reportedly aged 11 years and eight months, and 15 years and three months, respectively. Upon discovery, Shein severed ties with the offending suppliers and reiterated its commitment to preventing such breaches. That year, the company claimed to have carried out approximately 4,300 audits covering 317,000 workers, as part of its efforts to increase scrutiny over labour practices.[^1]

Excessive Hours and Substandard Pay

Investigative reporting and labour rights organisations have repeatedly documented cases of workers in Shein’s supply chain being subjected to extreme work conditions. Employees at some supplier factories have been found working up to 75 hours per week, far exceeding legal limits. In many cases, these excessive hours are paired with wages well below living standards—raising concerns about the exploitation of vulnerable workers to sustain the brand’s ultra-fast fashion cycle.[^2]

Opaque Supply Chains and Enforcement Weaknesses

Shein’s business model—characterised by speed, volume, and a reliance on a sprawling network of third-party suppliers—makes oversight particularly challenging. While the company has instituted a Supplier Code of Conduct and conducts regular audits, experts and critics argue that these measures often fail to detect or prevent labour abuses in practice. Despite Shein’s recent attempts to boost transparency and engagement with external monitors, allegations persist that systemic issues remain unaddressed.[^3]

Consumer Responsibility and Industry Impact

The controversy surrounding Shein is symptomatic of wider ethical concerns in the fast-fashion sector. The pressure to produce cheap clothing at scale often leads to the erosion of workers’ rights. Growing awareness among consumers, however, is prompting calls for more transparent sourcing practices and for shoppers to make informed decisions that support fair treatment of labour.

In conclusion, although Shein has undertaken measures to improve accountability, the recurrence of serious violations underscores the need for ongoing vigilance and structural reform. Ethical concerns around the company’s supply chain remain unresolved, and it is likely that continued public and regulatory pressure will be necessary to enforce lasting improvements.

⸻

[^1]: The Guardian. (2025, February 26). Shein found two cases of child labour at suppliers in 2024, firm tells UK MPs. Retrieved from https://www.theguardian.com/business/2025/feb/26/shein-found-two-cases-of-child-labour-at-suppliers-in-2024-firm-tells-uk-mps
[^2]: Business & Human Rights Resource Centre. (2023). China: BBC investigation reveals Shein suppliers’ workers face 75-hour work weeks & low wages. Retrieved from https://www.business-humanrights.org/en/latest-news/china-bbc-investigation-reveals-shein-suppliers-workers-face-75-hour-work-weeks-low-wages-incl-co-comment
[^3]: Business & Human Rights Resource Centre. (2024). Shein’s response to KnowTheChain 2024 scorecard. Retrieved from https://www.business-humanrights.org/en/latest-news/sheins-response-to-knowthechain-2024-scorecard/

We get 2 10 min breaks and a 20 min lunch...which you have to walk half a football field to get to the breakroom, so not really a lunch break. We had no air conditioning so in the summer it was H@!! In there. I mean...this is normal no matter what country or buisness. You get a choice whether to work that job or not though...just like the buisness that turned shein down. And of course they are going to try to get prices as low as possible...duh! It's called doing buisness. Good grief.

author: @Starlight-u4x
text: I think this kind of "journalism" is important in that you are showing the situation in as pure a sense as possible. No agenda, no overarching article or message, just "hey this is what it looks like, this is the current situation". What is especially important to me is the fact that you mentioned the working conditions were industry standard and in-line with working conditions overall in China. This is important for consumers to understand when making decisions on where they buy their clothes from. Many "higher end" companies now advertise better working conditions for their employees, but based on what standard? How are they measuring that? If a company simply states "we ensure our factories meet or exceed industry standards", is this the standard they are talking about? At the end of the day, you are correct in that it is not just a Shein issue. This has been going on long before the internet even existed. As long as there is a country willing to let its citizens be paid less for their work, this issue will persist. A lot of garment production is now being shifted to other countries in Southeast Asia and Africa as manufacturers seek even lower regulations and pay.
