author: @SquidandCatAdventures
text: This is directed to English speaking viewers, but Amazon has been criticized for working conditions for US citizens right in the US and I think the blisters photo was meant to get more of a rise, but there are plenty of jobs here that get blisters. People have been talking about a base rage for restaurant servers here in Canada (and in the US) for ages instead of relying on tips (which relies on volume of customers), but that hasn't really changed and yet in some cases, it's the most lucrative work because of the tips. But a big difference is the 14 hour shift. But restaurant industry and hospital doctors work long shifts, so while we have federal laws about being paid overtime after 8h, there are still fields where long shifts exist. I don't think that it's realistic to change the demand through the consumer's concern. It's difficult for the average consumer to stay on top of what is safe to buy.

author: @phyllismay4384
text: I watch a documentary on PBS years ago about this a blue jeans factory in China. It was so horrific. Naive workers from rural villages just trying to make a living wage for their families. One young girl was followed and the hours were like 10 to 12 hours a day. The workers lived in dorms on site of the factory. There was no sick leave...it didn't matter how sick you got you had to work or you got fired. There were task masters to yell at you to wake up if you feel asleep from exhaustion at your sewing machine. The pay was 1/2 yen(might be saying this incorrectly) an hour. The fancy slave labor jeans were then sold in high end shops in New York city in upwards of $275.00. This practice made me so sick at my stomach. After that I vowed to be very discerning about the things I buy. I want made in the USA.

author: @Nabee_H
text:
People always demonize sweatshops and never come to the realization that sweatshops will always be sweatshops. Mass manufacturing clothes is labour intensive whether you like it or not, and this video may highlight shein but it literally looks exactly like this everywhere else in the world (even in the US). The only valid argument this video brought up is the low(ish) wages, which if anything honestly seem pretty reasonable for the Chinese economy. If you think this is bad, compare it with Canadian or American sweatshops, and then compare those to Bangladeshi sweatshops.

But no, people just see and start speaking. This is an industry-wide international issue, sweatshop workers in general need to be paid more all across the board, but you can thank companies like LVMH for how the industry is nowadays. They're literally the ones profiting from all of this, and when the opportunity comes to increase wages they would rather just pocket it instead, both because of money and because of keeping the industry like this on purpose.

author: @lanaleightaylor9587
text: My mom worked as a seamstress in the 1960s in Philadelphia.  These factories were once here in the US. In the 1980s, President Reagan sent most if not all of the factory jobs to China. I remember this because my mom considered returning to the workforce in the 1980s. I was super young, like in elementary school.  My mom was newly divorced and a single mom of 3 kids, and all of her friends were devastated that the factory jobs were gone. The working conditions were different.  My mom told me there was a union for seamstresses; one was called the Malcolm H Union. You could make a good living and had protection.This type of work was a trade for many of our moms, aunties, and grandmoms back in the day.

author: @drunkntigr
text:
The cause of this problem is:
1. Consumer, we want a discount, we want it cheaper.
2. Retailer to wholesaler, the other factory does it cheaper, not, will this price work for you so that the people making it can have real wages?

People,  this happens all over the world, in resources mines, cocoa beans, cashews, all across the third world. How do you think your shit is so cheap?

If a product is $100:
expect 10% to taxes, 
15% to retail wages, 
15% rent, 
20% storage and transport, 
5% misc, 
15% marketing (small figure here)
10% company profits, 
10% cost of product

Things aren't made overseas cause people want to get paid crap, it's because the $8 trillion dollars the US takes it from overseas is a result of their subsidiary international brands needs to make profit.

author: @kingsley9985
text:
People often look at others with an air of superiority, perceiving them as doing jobs they deem unworthy. However, what you fail to see is that these individuals may have come from thousands of miles away, once living in dire poverty, without the privilege of an education or the opportunities that you had. You may question why the world is so unfair. But is your life, blessed by fortune, really fair? When you have more, do you actively try to help others? Have you made any effort for your fellow human beings across the world?

You have the means to contribute through donations, innovation, or investing in companies like SHEIN that offer opportunities for those in lower income brackets. Yet instead of offering your support, you stand with your head held high, pointing fingers and "exposing" the so-called injustices.

We all know that fate is often unjust. We know that in some corners of the world, people are suffering through acts of genocide. We are aware of these realities. But in a world where most of us remain silent, unwilling to take action or speak out, the best course of action is to refrain from judgment. Sometimes, helping others can end up doing more harm than good, especially when it's rooted in arrogance and condescension.

As the philosopher Albert Einstein once said, "The world is not dangerous because of those who do harm, but because of those who look at it without doing anything." It is crucial to reflect on how we use our position, resources, and influence. Sometimes, the most constructive way to contribute is to respect the struggles of others without belittling them from a place of superiority.

author: @dailydoseofchaoslol

I worked for a company that washes laundry for hospitals and it was, well let's say simillar. 
Long working hours even forced overtime, minimum vages. This one company was seeking people that were unstable, struggling educationally, emotinally or financially. 
Tbh, working there was the worst job I've ever had. I felt very miserable and depressed. I felt immediatelly exhausted when I thought about going back there. Thankfully I no longer work there. 
I'm currently working for a startup in a office and omg that's absolutelly different. I actually enjoy working there and I'm looking forward going to the work. I do a lot of stuff from preparing, packaging, sending orders to customer service and web management and it's great.

author: @arthurlivingstone-n9t
text: Sometimes is not about money or the hours you work. Come to Australia and interview any group of teachers in a school, and you'll find the most arrogant, self-serving, demeaning and mentally abusive school principals in the world. Teachers might earn more than someone in a factory in China, but you will go home mentally tormented and bullied by admin staff, principals, parents, students and heads of departments. You'll either have a mental breakdown or suffer abuse for the rest of your working life. Money isn't everything. If the idea is to find the perfect system to live, it doesn't exist. Even the wealthiest people usually find themselves in courts, suffering addictions and discontentment with life. My advice. Work the job to live, learn what you need to get where you want to get. Be contempt with a little, be contempt with a lot.

So if you got two orders and it took you 30 minutes to do those two orders, And the base pays $14, You're only going to get $7 for that hour. 🙄😮‍💨 Corporate predatory crap! That's not enough for gas or upkeep on your car or even to pay yourself!! It's a total rip-off and unless you're driving some sort of Prius or electric car that you charge at home you'll never make but it actually cost to do the job. So we experienced the same stuff here in the states. And you have to hope and pray that the customer gives you a tip to cover the difference for your hour and that's not always the case. Especially if you're doing cheap orders like McDonald's. Those people pretty much never did. Cuz they could probably barely afford the order. Doordash charges the customer or whatever their fee is but the Dasher doesn't see a lot of that money. It's a total rip off!

author: @havootu
text:
Honestly it's not much different than how it was when I worked at Amazon some years back in terms of hours. the pay was crap, even working full time I couldn't afford to rent, he best part was the insurance, And I soon found out why they gave you insurance upon hiring. the work itself was so exhausting. And during the holiday season you have to do mandatory 12-hour shifts. End up getting a foot injury from all the walking and standing that left me out for 6mnths. 🙄 Then I understood why they gave insurance this was a common occurrence for people. I didt get workers comp tho. I feel like a lot of industries in the US and abroad these companies take advantage of people. Aside from certain labor laws it's really not much differently when you think about it. A lot of regular jobs you can't even afford rent by yourself, can barely afford food. And then everything is going up too. All I see is the same and possible situation in different countries. Corporations exploiting people.
The worst part about it is still much of the same, only thing I could afford was either shein or Walmart clothes Maybe a few things on Amazon. And I'm talking about just basics not fashion. They keep things like this to continue the exploitation.

author: @chrisabc123
text: I worked in sourcing based in Guangzhou 2002 to 2005 and then Hong Kong until 2020. Travelled China extensively. So much, that my Chinese colleagues would joke I know it better than them. It was amazing to see the industrial revolution unfold in front of my own eyes and how for example I would take a rickety bus from Hangzhou to Yuyao to visit a light factory in the beginning and near the end of my time there I was taking high speed rail. Generally labour practices that I witnessed were ok although rising labour cost was always the number one complaint of factory owners coupled with the constant pressure of lower prices from foreign buyers because the barriers to entry for competitors was so ridiculously low. I am not trying to explain way Shein. In fact I do what I can to persuade my peers to never buy the brand. That and anything on Temu which is just a covid era dumping ground for defective and unwanted production orders.  But from the economic side, I believe sadly we will only see the labour practices, compensation, etc improve in China with the help of the government. And I am afraid that given the sheer size of the manufacturing world in China, it truly could be an insurmountable task for the government. Great work Cinthia and thank you for allowing me to reminisce about a world I truly miss. Keep up the good work but please 小心.

author: @Timesobserver_Xenia
text: Firstly, such a problem is created by Chinese ppl themselves: they created business where somebody already has been..when 1 or 2 does so,the market can sustain, but it's like wave on Asia: if somebody gets order the bunch of new enterprises will pop up to offer solutions in home to have that order next time. Ad the result, hundreds of companies and production fight fir the money. What they do? Find creative ways, improve quality? No, they go cheaper and less quality, and speed up all others with this. As the result the lie comes up: cheap and fast production for brands..others,who were skilful and clean manufacturers, now follow this ways to be able to stay involved. The client knows now there are hundreds on the market who want to take the order and he goes with offer that he knows somebody will pick up. It's not a brand who did a trouble here. All factories could abort such a client. That's a factory owners and not smart businessmen, who decided to build their life time around such an industry with no real plan. It is very typical for southeast Asia: no cleat analyses and planning and then, uups, the crisis comes. Secondly. 1000usd per month? Guys, many countries do not have that salaries for even educated ppl. Build the factory in Russia or Belarus, and you will get contemporary, clean and well organized factory with all steps counted for efficiency, which makes labor work easier and supported with the smart management and machinery. What I see in this video: a mass in the production facilities. That is all created by factories themselves. And yet,if you get great and well organized facility in Russia, for example, people will never get there more than 1000 used per months even with double shifts. That salary is supposed to be very good for the regions and us paid to quite limited group of ppl. Despite capital,where wages are a way higher,but we also don't talk about Beijing here, we talk about industrial regional city. Many ppl who work in garment industry would argue now regarding salaries Chinese receive. And many things are quite cheaper for living in China. Anyways, the change shall start from the production owner. If you cannot keep high level and find the client fir your level, if you cannot guess that a union between factories needed to fix and regulate business, than leave this industry. You are novice there. Somehow, HK people can create that associations and even launch regulations where they have a black list for those who try to downsize and violate as a client and if those who offered the service with violation of the set up union standards. It is time to realize the real cause of the situation instead of puting all on brands ur others.

author: @KaiNg.09
text:
I can confirm everything about this video is realistic; if you've been to garment factories or any kind of manufacturing in Asian countries, you'll see it's pretty much the same everywhere. From my own experience as an automation engineer at Samsung in Vietnam 6 years back, I can tell you that even if the work condition at Samsung is better than in some places, it's still way worse than in the western countries. They usually advertise the same pay range as you'd see in the West, but only a few workers actually get that, and it's usually the highly skilled ones. For regular laborers, they always say overtime is "voluntary," but if you don't do it, they'll treat you differently—no promotion, no raise, stuff like that.
Yeah, Western countries basically need someone to make their products with cheap labor to maximize their profits, and they often have companies that partner with these factories so they don't have any direct responsibility. It's pretty bad. These workers don't have much choice, and they still have to pay a lot for living costs and feed their families. A lot of them end up with health problems, and then they use all their savings on hospital bills. It's like a cycle they can't escape. And where's the government in all this? Well, the laws in some of these countries make it cheaper for companies to just pay a fine than to actually invest in improving work conditions. So, they just pay the fine, and everything goes back to normal.

author: @aesaphyr
text: The very sad thing is that I live in Mauritius where we have a significant textile/garment industry too. Companies like Zara, Superdry and H&M get their clothes made here. But people are usually paid Rs 10,000-20,000 monthly in our local currency for these jobs, for working hours and conditions similar to the ones in the video. That's 250-500 USD per month (compared with 1360 USD in the video). The garment factories in Mauritius employ a lot of foreign workers from low-income countries. It's a form of exploitation. It's very sad and sobering to appreciate that we are even worse than Shein. For people who think buying their clothes from other retailers is automatically better, it's not. The entire garment industry needs to be held to account.

author: @BirdOfFlo
text: When I used to work in a warehouse in Austria I would walk about 20 to 25km (30.0000 steps+) in an 8-10hrs shift. Working in a warehouse you just walk (fast) all the time, this sums up. Constantly soaking wet due to the hard work and always stressed. Without sacrificing my 20min unpaid break I wouldn't be able to fullfill my work. No time use the toilet. But I only worked 6 days a week, the 6th day not being compulsory. At least I didn't need an additional work-out and the payment was pretty good for that time: 1700€net/month in the years 2013-2015, even higher when doing night shifts (automotive industry). Today, this would be rather at the lower end of what you earn in Austria, yet I still did not earn more throughout the last 4 years. But my most recent job was not stressful at all and I could work outside all the time. No matter what fulltime job you will do in Austria, you will get around and needn't do extra shifts or get a second job (even with a low incom of 1500€; 800 flat, 300 food if you cook on your own, 100 to pay to get to work, another 100 for other costs such as internet, insurance, bank fees and mobile contract, so 200 to spare, not much but enough to pay for some unexpected expenses, go to the restaurant oncec a month or maintain a small inexpensive used car. Then you have, which is unique to Austria, a 13. and 14. salary just like that, so you will definitely be alble to even go on holiday or have some money for hobbies). So I do feel bad for those Chinese factory workers, which are so underpayed. Hope everyone will get a fair salary in future. What keeps employees from being exploited too much in Austria are trade unions. Every industry sector has their own trade union, who's fighting for the worker's rights. So there's a de facto minimum wage for every single industry sector.

Hi ! Thank you for this great video. SheIN is doing a big rebranding because of other countries' blocus that were coming for them and is not doing anything substantially better. People need to know about how manufacturers are working for them, how all the waste or 1 usage garments end. They can't be sold in bags to be worn by people in a 'second life' or if not sold for quality issues and they end up in big open-air dumps in Africa.
This is a welfare issue for Chinese people, and an ecologic non-sense.

author: @gabbythewalnut
text: I wonder what would happen to china if the fast fashion market disapeared overnight. (ok any business disapearing overnight probably causes some societal impact) But really, just the fast fashion ones. (most of them? XD if not high quality production, which I know they are indeed capable of just not ... everywhere I don't think?) how many families and people would be affected, and go hungry? Would it be ok for china to lose this option for income? What kinds of jobs would those workers alternatively get? Would they necessarily be good or better? I have doubts that other local clothing production teams get better treatment. I get the feeling it's just the work culture over there. Take 1 away, another similar problem pops up? Where there is any opportunity at all, there is someone desperate enough (if not several people desperate enough and competing) for the job? Does making money in those kinds of area mean you can't expect to get a job unless you prove you're willing to bend over backwards not to have your position taken from you and given to someone else?

author: @Pookasoul
text: The curious thing, is that that doesn't only happen in China... even in Europe, many brands, like Zara, produce or finish up production in countries like Portugal, to have a made in Europe label, but pay the companies like 30 cents per completed piece, if not less.. I saw prices like, for every sewed label, 0.05 cents.... not even a whole cent.. That generates extreme working conditions, where sewing operators need to work 12h a day, fast producing pace, for minimal wage and no extras. And because the unemployment rate is so high, people need to take those jobs. Right here in Europe.

author: @lay-pj4dx
text: 事实上，视频里这些还算不错的，就是要普遍给加班费才是关键。沿海地区起码有工厂，这些人还可以在工资高低和工作时长上有要求，内陆和西北地区连这种简单的工作都没有。我们这边小县城政府单位的也才三四千一个月，只不过他们劳动强度没那么大，小学老师工作了十几年也才4000多点，实际上这种工作已经算挺好的了，除了工作时间长点，其他的都是小事。你不保持竞争力，产业链转给东南亚那边，我们就没什么工作了，而且东南亚工作条件比我们这边更差，缅甸，越南，柬埔寨那些人工资远没有中国工人高，条件还比我们差。指责强迫劳动只是一种说辞，我承认国内工厂存在一些问题，但是我并不希望像西方那样拒绝和抵制这些工厂，我希望国内工厂通过改革改善工人条件，但是我不希望通过西方叙事让他们倒闭或者丢失订单。和早年指责我们工厂污染一样，这是一个渐进的过程，治理是需要循序渐进的，而不是一股脑的抵制，我更在意这些人真正通过劳动拿到了自己的报酬

text: Honestly, sewing really changed my perspective. It makes you appreciate your clothes so much more. I can't wrap my head around a structured dress being sold for 20 euros. It takes so much time preparing the fabric, cutting out the pattern or even drafting the pattern, the ironing (that is needed A LOT!), the cutting the sewing and assembling the pieces you cut out, doing darts, etc. it takes time. I feel so sad seeing fashion not being celebrated. I have also been from a family with very very little budget. That made me creative, buying from thrift stores, swapping with friends and family, buying second hand online. Even sewing a few little things. Fabric can be expensive too, ofc but you can thrift fabric, curtains, sheets (beforehand please check for the pattern instructions about the fabric needed - knit vs. woven). What I mean to say, even on low budget you don't deserve the SHEIN Quality. You deserve clothes that will make you fall in love with them, make you care for them and keep them as a loyal companion <3

author: @amalhyounis
text: I think the missing part of the conversation that isn’t including young people is providing alternatives. H&M and Zara are very very expensive in comparison especially in third world countries, and they’re still not ethical, environmentally friendly or good quality. So what’s the alternative? Combatting consumerism is definitely the best case scenario, but that’s something that even environmentally-conscious people can struggle with. For example, I’m fairly low income and I try my best to avoid the trends and not buy more than I need. However, when I do need a specific piece of clothing that I cannot thrift, why would I buy an item that I have to save several months for versus something I can afford and won’t break the bank? I feel like we’re raising concerns about this issue, which is good, but we’re not providing solutions. Legislators in my opinion are the ones to blame for not putting strict rules on this, and also simply not giving enough shits about the environment in terms of other laws as well.

author: @regs3941
text:
Speaking for someone living in a Western industrial country: 
I never believed, that there are *so many* people out there, who can't afford somewhat sustainable alternatives to fast fashion. Clothing is by any means so cheap - yes, sustainable options included, if you don't go for brands and regular changes to your wardrobe. I say this as a person, who is poor herself by my country's poverty standard. 
The first sustainable alternative everybody got, is available to everyone: Do not by anything at all. 99% of us have more stuff, we even need.
Then comes repairing what you got. I do it all the time. Clothes - even cheap ones - can be worn so much longer then the average person keeps their fast fashion pieces. So, the low quality of fast fashion was never a valid argument for me to hear - most pieces aren't worn until the break down at all.
And the final cheap option for sustainable clothing: thrifting. You can get some high quality clothes for the price of SHEIN clothes. And not producing anything is *always* more sustainable then the ecologically friendliest production can be.

Oh, and sustainable new clothes do not cost much and are even cheaper if you calculate cost per wear. You just can't go after brands. I buy my leggins, that I wear under my skirts in winter from a sustainable company, who produces in Europe. The last thick winter leggings I bought was 22€ and it lasted me two winters and I wore it *often*. So, I can simply not say, that 44€ per winter is too much for me to pay and I can only afford the 10€ leggings from a fast fashion place (which would propably don't last that long, thus only last me half as long, so no money saved here).

author: @sk8razer
text:
6:56 "low budget" is an extremely important aspect. The discussion around fast fashion & sustainable fashion tends to center on people who can afford to over consume fast fashion. 

This video spends around 15 seconds total acknowledging that some people consume fast fashion for financial reasons. "Girl's got a point, not everyone can afford sustainable." at the absolute end of the video is the extent that the writers & producers decided to deviate from the overall message about financially comfortable people killing the planet via reckless over consumption. 

The message winds up being "You could and *SHOULD* invest the same amount of money in a decently sized, versatile wardrobe of sustainable items." with a whole list of tips like "keep your closet fresh by purchasing a few new items from sustainable designers each season to pair with items from last season!" 

It's great advice for that one specific demographic. But there's a lot of people relying on fast fashion's low price points just to keep themselves and their children clothed at all. 

Especially since another common tip is "Hit up thrift shops and second hand stores to get your fashion fix. Cute vintage pairs perfectly with socially & eco conscious, fashionista!". The increased demand from consumers who are able to spend more is doubly problematic for  people who lack as much expendable income. 

The supply obviously decreases, which increases the price point on its own. But price point is further increased by the fact that people with more expendable income are simply willing to pay more. 

There's a lot to say about the phenomenon of using thrifting as an altruistic way to over consume clothing ("I'm not just keeping *my* clothing out of a landfill in the global south, I'm literally keeping the clothing of other people out of landfills too! It's a win-win!"). But the key point here is that swapping fast fashion over consumption for over consumption of second hand items is not a win-win. 

There is no altruistic or guilt free swap for over consumption. Over consumption is *the* problem. 

Decreased access to used clothing forces lower income people to rely more on fast fashion options, including brick & mortar retailers like Walmart, Forever 21, and H&M. These lower quality new items are worn over and over for as long as possible. Fast fashion items are basically treated as "disposable" by fashion-focused higher income shoppers. But they don't actually become unwearable after a few wears (usually lol). 

The dyes start to fade and print starts to crack & peel very quickly. The fabric starts stretching  and thinning causing the garment to lose its original fit quickly. Wearable/repairable rips are fairly common. Basically, the item starts to look old and "frumpy" early on. But a lot of fast fashion options can be worn & washed multiple times per month for several months to a few years before finally becoming unwearable. 

But wear isn't the only driver behind necessary purchasing. Body shapes & sizes change, particularly if you're trying to keep children clothed. Items get lost or destroyed by forces beyond normal wear. The purchases made by people who rely on inexpensive options may be done in moderation and almost entirely out of necessity, but it adds up. 

More importantly, the percentage of people who rely on inexpensive items is much larger than the percentage of people who can afford more expensive sustainable items. We like to think that the fast fashion industry is entirely the result of shady marketing practices & "fashion industry culture" manipulating consumers in to rampant over indulgence. Like the people in these haul Tik Toks are wholly representative of fast fashion consumers. 

But a massive percentage of the market share lacks the ability to be manipulated into over indulgence. Because you can't be manipulated into buying more than you need if you can barely afford what you legitimately need. 

Fixing the over consumption problem among those in "comfortable" financial positions will only have a modest impact in the overall problem. To textile industry's widespread exploitation of workers and impact on the planet *feels* fairly isolated and easy to fix. "Just make better choices." is the common message. And those privileged people who have the ability to "just make better choices" absolutely should. 

Unfortunately, the problem is incredibly complex and completely enmeshed with the systems that make living so unaffordable for most of the planet's population. We can't do much to fix the social & ecological problems associated with fast fashion until we make significant progress on the sheer unavailability of being middle-income or lower. 

And we can't make any progress on that issue as long as we continue fixating on the personal responsibility aspect of individual finances. The problem goes far beyond simple financial irresponsibility and an unwillingness to "put in the work" to attain a more comfortable financial position. Financial illiteracy is only a small part of the situation. The actual problem is rigid hierarchies is profoundly broken systems.

author: @sk8razer
text:
And *THANK YOU* to this channel for dubbing instead of subtitling! It's annoying when videos are almost entirely listenable, but they choose to subtitle. 

And *WAAAAAY MORE IMPORTANTLY*, dubbing = accessibility. Subtitling is inaccessible to visually impaired people, people who cannot read the language in which the subtitles are written (yes, it's possible to be nearly fluent in a language while being illiterate in said language), or experience sensory overload as a result of subtitles.

author: @akiriwe
text:
I didn't ever buy anything in SHEIN, but after the war began, we were stuck in Germany with a little number of garments, and we used to make a couple of hauls to Primark to replenish the lack of cloth (we left Ukraine at the end of winter so we didn't have spring/summer cloth at all).

What can I say? Not proud of it. Didn't even worth it, really. One little example. Shoes might cost 10-20 euros, but they live only 2-3 months - I ruined 2 pairs of shoes over the 4 months, they were painful to wear, and I had blisters and damaged skin on my feet. You can argue it's justified, but look - I bought a pair of sneakers for 70 euros in October and I still wear them, they are in good condition and I am sure they will survive another couple of seasons easily. By this moment I would throw away 3-4 pairs of Primark shoes already, and, mind you, in terms of money you don't actually spend less. You buy low-quality, low-comfort shoes which will break apart soon enough and you will have to replace them. And the damage was quite significant, it's not like it got one small thing and I am throwing them away. Still, there are some good garments we took from there which are still alive and look good - jeans onesies, and some t-shirts are serving their purpose for a year already. But you have to know what to take.

After this experiment, I decided to go with rather rare shopping, but with better quality cloth from more responsible (or at least less reckless) brands, which will last longer and will look better One-two pairs of good jeans per year is enough. A couple of T-shirts, 1-2 pair of shoes. I am privileged enough now to afford myself to make a better choice, and I am lucky not to have fashion as an interest, but I know there are people as well, who will buy those 10 euros sneakers and will be forced to somehow walk in them for a long time, even if they become damaged, and I understand that and I have no judgment. My only concern is when people prefer to buy 10 pairs of 10 euro shoes just to have the ability to choose them depending on mood and throw them away a month later (I doubt those SHEIN bloggers didn't have money), not when the decision is made out of extreme poverty.

text:
Just like with most things that are bad for us: we do it since there is no other option. 

Buying sustainable and fair-trade products isn't an alternative to buying cheap mass produced stuff. That is because it is not as convenient. 

Being moral is expensive and often very inconvenient.

Yes, giving us a reason to give up a thing might make us feel quilty for doing it, but in many cases won't affect our actions that much. You need to give us a realistic alternative that is just as convenient.

It is more convenient to eat from the packaging than cook for ourselves. And eating out is expensive and so is hiring a chef.

It is unreasonable to expect someone to give up their garden of pleasure for one that mile away and off the normal route. 

You wouldn't trade your house for one that ⅓ smaller and located in Siberia for example.

We only give up a thing when we value the other more. It already has the morals on that side of the scale, but we only need nearly equal price and assesibility to tip it.

author: @Alexanderxu-k1x
text:
Why do consumers have to tolerate SHEIN - a unofficial Gulag?
As most Western consumers know, every successful or failed Chinese company is full of forced labor and various illegal activities, because in China, companies with deep ties to the government like Huawei and Shein can always regard the law as joke. However, even among these companies, Shein is probably the worst one.
There are two main points of shein's bad behavior

1. Ignore the labor law and force overtime through verbal communication from the manager when refusing to pay remuneration. From the beginning of 2023, in the face of competition from Pinduoduo, ByteDance and other similar products from other Chinese companies, shein began to force employees to work overtime without pay through managers at all levels, although most of the overtime hours did not yield any benefits. For example, according to confirmed information, Haiwei Yu, the senior manager of Shein's operations department, requires team members to work 40 hours of unpaid overtime per month. Yi Gao, the manager of the core trading department, requires team members to work at least 45 hours of unpaid overtime per month. In addition, it is said that Bie Shen, the manager of the commodity department, requires overtime to exceed 55 hours per month. Although overtime work is common in China, almost all other companies will pay full overtime pay, such as Huawei and Pinduoduo. Maybe some consumers feel that the market is free, and they can leave their jobs if they don’t want to, but the peculiarity of Shein is that it is good at using non-compete agreements. Go to the so-called competitors, but almost all similar companies in China are blacklisted by shein, which means that leaving shein is basically equivalent to never finding a job. Shein created a folk version of the "Gulag Islands" in this way. Everyone on the island has to work for a long time under low wages, not only office workers, but factories are no exception. There were once factory workers who couldn't bear it. Working long hours puts a distress note in the clothes that are sent to the US. Some Western consumers wonder why employees don’t sue the company and organize strikes to protest, because in China, facing companies like Shein, which can provide high taxes, the rulers have always condoned and severely cracked down on various worker protests.

2. Racial discrimination, the policy of recruiting employees in the United States distinguishes races. According to reliable sources, shein clearly gives priority to recruiting Chinese in the United States, especially those born and raised in China, because in the eyes of shein executives, these people have been subjected to dictatorship and oppression for a long time and are used to submitting, and employees of other races also prefer non-democratic countries These behaviors run counter to American values. Can Western consumers, especially American consumers, tolerate such behaviors that openly trample on the bottom line of society?


Most western consumers are definitely not interested in what happens in China, they only care about the cheap women's clothing provided by shein. However, even Western companies operating in China try their best to respect Zhou’s labor laws and maintain the humane traditions of Western society. For example, Suzhou Microsoft paid N+12 high compensation for layoffs. Shein is not the only one that can provide cheap products on this planet. Hope consumers can resist shein on the basis of universal values and promote it to change.

author: @Maria-EU
text: I've been working in fashion for years and I did my master's in sustainable fashion. When she states in the beginning of the video about how something like this can happen in an era of growing consciousness, I can tell you that 99.99% of fashion companies have nothing to do with sustainability -- it's just greenwashing. And more importantly, consumers care more about buying stuff and saving money. They want to ignore the unsustainable production and they choose to do so. All the research that depicts consumers to be conscious is false as it asks if they think of themselves as being conscious, not if they have genuinely taken action to be conscious. No one will admit that they are purposely choosing consumerism and money over explotation, animal abuse and destroying the environment.

Another reason might be ppp(purchasing power parity).... 
While this price might be low for some countries... It may be normal for other countries.... I'm from India and i would never pay more than 500($6) for a t shirt and normal I would pay ₹200-300($2-4) its normal price.... U could use it for atleast 2 years... And u could afford really good quality clothes within ₹1000(12$) that could pretty much last years. I'm a student at delhi university... I'm privileged enough to be born in a good family... I don't need to work to sustain... My parents support me while I'm still studying... I can get ₹15000(187$) and can still spare ₹4000/month to invest in stock market.... I can live pretty well in delhi with this much money that is capital of india..... I can only do this here.... And not in UK OR US.... What I can get in India for little money.... Will cost 20 times more there..... Like medical facilities is USA is skyhigh... u can get same facilities here for 2-4% or even less... U can even get it done for free if u have good govt. Hospitals in ur city... Clothes, medicine and many other things are way more cost effective here...... People who earn $50000/year in India can live lavish lifestyle with villa and multiple servents... While people who earn the same in USA may struggle. 

While people may find shein products cheap... I can get good product here for the same... What others find cheap may be normal price in some countries....

I’ve never been one for fast fashion.
…when I could afford to be. I think it’s also important to realize that there are times in your life where you can find yourself low on funds and in needs of bulk amounts of clothes. There aren’t a lot of options. Growth spurts, pregnancy, weight loss are just some of the physical ones. But then there’s also when you’re a student and you move to a new climate or you get your first office job or you’re a background actor who needs a varied wardrobe (I know - that’s very specific but I live somewhere where that’s actually fairly common). There are just so many situations that you can be in where it isn’t possible or even advisable to spend serious money on something for “the long term” because you’re either solving a short-term problem or you have limited funds and immediate needs.

Basically, we have to think about the problems that fast-fashion solves and find better ways to address them rather than just finger-wagging and the people who buy it.

Here is *MY* personal opinion :

Again *this is my opinion*

text:
I am guilty of buying clothes from Shein and other fast fashion, but I don't buy the trendy or cheap clothing items, I look for their 'normal priced' clothing items that fit my budget and has good reviews about the material and quality.  I'm a small person with body type that is not typical of where I live, often I found I can only find things that fit me from some of these fast fashion places. I also only look at Shein when I need clothes, and my clothes normally stay with me for years. They are not 'super high quality' , but they are nice and comfortable, and they have lasted. Only a couple of them actually end up break, ironically, the more 'fragile' ones were some of my more expensive items. However I mend them because I simply can't afford to throw them away. Even more ironically, I bought a few things from a local brand that market themselves as sustainable, locally made and environmentally conscious. Despite them being relatively more expensive, they end up being my least worn items and the fabrics are quite low in quality in comparison to the rest of my 'fast fashion' wardorbe. 

I do believe we can still buy from fast fashion places without overconsume. However I do really hate how these brands praying on young people with their ugly marketing strategies. But I think the root of the problem is that people simply don't know how to recognise good products and don't look after their clothes. Also, everything is so expensive these days.

text: Doesn’t seem like the commentators in the video know much about business… Cheaper prices can also result from reduced inventory waste, faster cash-to-cash cycle, and lower CapEx given the online-only retail model. If you look at the balance sheet and income statement of any (fast) fashion company, COGS (the actual production costs) is not all that much compared to revenue. You can significantly reduce other costs and prices with the same pay for workers and same environmental protection in the production stage. Shein simply found and solved the inefficiencies in the non-production processes of its competitors.

author: @AylaMarianna
text:
I don't know. I almost felt pressure to buy from SHEIN because the pricing is just "too good", and I'm 32. 
But that was also the issue; while browsing I got more and more put off by the extremely low prices. The business model is completely unsustainable, except for those at the top exorbitantly benefiting from abusing the planet and its inhabitants to the max. 

I don't know if I could have "resisted", had I been a decade younger. 
Likely not, especially since I was still not only immature as well as impulsive (at that age, I had recently been diagnosed with ADHD, so unfortunately I went through my formative years without receiving much-needed treatment & support for my disability), but I was also very insecure in myself, combined with (still to this day) enjoying dressing up/"decorating myself". 
I also grew up in a single parent-household (after my other parent died in childhood), so my family was not financially well-off compared to my peers' here in Sweden. 
And I became legally an adult right around the global financial crash, which was followed by the European debt crisis. Fun times, I like toootally made it through unscathed without any mental health issues. 🙄 

So yeah: a variety of circumstances were already impacting me and influencing my purchasing decisions, despite growing up during the Internet's Wild West, and seeing my first smartphone in the summer of 2008. It was the heyday of blogging. Steam was massively improving and started to properly win people over. Spotify was just about to launch its streaming service. I created this account when YouTube was still new—about 15 years ago. Twitter too was new. I felt like I was late getting on the Facebook train—joining in January 2008. Etc., etc., etc.

Today everything is just an invasive mass media onslaught: 
*The relentless information overload, the unlimited 24/7 access and availability, the unconsensual aggressive molding & brainwashing of still-developing minds, the manipulative gamification pushing people towards gambling and other compulsive & addictive behaviours with the purpose of trapping them there, the problematically hypersexualized digital environments & extreme pornification of social media, and its excessive, intrusive, absolutely unavoidable content that saturates mainstream culture & disparately affects the every day life of young people within it.* 
*Humans who cannot protect nor defend themselves against this, because it happens regardless—everyone's an intended victim, nobody gets to shield themselves—and it does so without any regard to whether anyone actually ever intentionally seeks anything out.* 
*The sheer volume, the rampant consumerism, the exploitation, the forcible advertising shoved down our throats, the deeply disturbing objectification, the unwarranted pressure.* 
*The total disregard.* 
*The mental assault of it all.* 
*The unprecedented human experimentation on involuntary participants.* 

I certainly would absolutely not like being a Gen Z:er today; I seriously don't know how they manage to cope. 😕

This is the arrogant of the West, before any Chinese fashion company become number 1, there isn't any problem at all, but when a Chinese fashion company suddenly grow so big, the collective west instead of review their marketing strategy they defame the Chinese fashion company instead.

When they sale fashion to another country, they often change their design to suit the local culture, most of the European fashion brand cheap or expensive, they are still operates in the traditional method, and they don't even bother to change their fashion design to suit the local market.

I give you one good example, H&M, they have many large store front in South East Asia, however, most of the South East Asian countries are in the triple zone, which means, we more like having summer all year round, but H&M doesn't really care, they will continue to sale their 4 different season's clothing, if I visit our local H&M during December, they sale thick jackets and sweaters 😂😂😂

If this isn't bad enough, after the 2 and half years lock down, the e-commerce grow like mad, but most of the collective West fashion company cannot keep up with the trend, like Dorothy Perkins for a good example. Asia is a fast growing market, it has more than half of the world population, should any fashion company cannot keep up with the market trend, they will out of business!

tips for genz to be more sustainable with clothing:
-swap clothes with friends
-thrift
-alter clothes you wouldn’t usually wear
-only buy from fast fashion brands when you absolutely know you’re gonna wear the garment for a while
-before you buy imagine a few outfits that would work with the garment with clothes that you already have
-get basics (boots, flannels, tennis skirt, comfy jeans) and use jewellery as statements instead
-try aesthetics (ik not everyone wants to do this), you usually know what you want and will rewear clothes more often
-ask friends and family to give you clothes they don’t want
-learn to crochet/knit/sew

I think I’m in a weird group. I won’t buy Shein because it’s such poor value for money.

Why spend £6.99 on a polyester skirt from Shein  when I can spend £3 on a new with tags, 100% wool from Laura Ashley at save the children?

Why buy a “vintage style dress” from Shein at £18.99 when a similar dress that’s actually vintage dress costs £5.99 (less than third!) at Cancer Research?

I mean I could spend £12.99 on a wool mix skirt from Shein or I could spend £10 on a similar Burberry skirt that’s 100% wool but has some VERY minor damage to the seem from Oxfam- the damage would take maybe 2 minutes max to fix, even if sent to tailors for repair once you factor in delivery it’s basically identical price-wise but definitely not quality wise - and not in Shein’s favour 

Those aren’t the exception, they are the rule.

Moral of the story: Thrift. You will save money and your clothes will last longer since the material is quality, not quality garbage.

If I have to, I stay in between my budget by trying to polluting less. My advices especially to young people are:
- don't buy what you don't really need
- don't buy what you don't really like
- buy what lasts
- buy versatile, basic essentials clothing to mix and match
- only splurge once in a while and try to make it a good sustainable one
- swap clothes w friends, relatives etc or thrift them
- ignore the urge to mass buy you don't need that many clothes

author: @mollie6875
text:
My girlfriend has a 14-year-old sister. When she was a toddler, her mother would soothe her tantrums by buying her a new toy. The effects are obvious. She's constantly buying stuff on Amazon and the like, endless shoes and hoodies and phone cases. When I explained what fast fashion was, she said it was a good thing to get a lot of fast and cheap clothes. The family was not well-off, either, and the little sister uses the parent's credit card. That's what happens when you teach a child that buying things will make you feed better. Now we have it on a cultural scale. There used to be 2 seasons a year for fashion, now it's like 200 micro-seasons based on whatever Lil Nas X or a BTS member wears that evening. 

My girlfriend and I are strong believers in "if it seems too good to be true, it is." We both rarely buy new clothes and thrift a lot of what we do. Still, with things I wear every day, I don't mess around. My most expensive clothing item is my pair of work shoes. I want them to last 10 years so I shelled out.

author: @decameter
text:
I feel like there's an issue within every facet of the pipeline from maneufacturer to consumer. We over bank on the idea that oh we can just reuse something or recycle it via the second hand or since its all plastic, but people are a little blind to the fact that Reduce is the most important part of the Reduce, Reuse, Recycle triangle. And the other issue is that these clothes have almost no value or durability for the second hand. Sometimes people are just so afraid of animal products due to bad industry practices that they'd choose Oil byproducts over Meat production byproducts.

Even to the point that they won't buy heavily used but still good old wool and leather clothing. Or even old woven cotton products. Everything has to be new and perfect. So we have a lot of Pleather, and a lot of Polyester, products that essentially disintegrate while also remaining in the environment for ages.

author: @tamaratheilmann0615
text:
When people think of "sustainable clothing" it always comes with the association that it's expensive. Though, it really depends on the type of sustainable clothing. Because sustainable can be more than 100% of one material, like cotton or linen, that's certified (it could also be 100% rPET... but how is the skin supposed to breathe then). 
Sustainable means to use less and simultaneously use longer or again. For example, secondhand shopping. It's cheap and each piece you'll find is unique. At the same time you can buy just basics too. Secondhand can be from a vintage store, a flee market, from trading with friends, family or online with strangers. In the end, to reuse, reduce and recycle everything we've already produced is the most sustainable we can do.

author: @cormoranoimperatore8413
text:
Ok so for the people preaching the whole “personal responsibility” argument I suggest watching Kurzgesagt’s video on the matter they did a while ago. If we (and I mean it as in “all of us individuals”) were to become carbon neutral by tomorrow we would get only a few years before the climate point of no return is reached (at least that’s what I remember being said there I might be wrong but still the difference between responsibility individuals hold together and what they can do and what companies do is abyssal). Then again, even if our responsibility was truly big enough, good luck educating and convincing 10 billion people to act together on a problem when there’s still people convinced climate change doesn’t exist. I’m not saying you should just not care about what you do at all obviously, it’s nice and good to do your small part and to not just deliberately and carelessly pollute. Also did you know the whole concept of your “personal carbon footprint” was first advertised by an oil company? Personal responsibility is a strategy to shift the blame on individuals to distract from the harm companies cause. Also blaming people is simply ineffective because making someone feel bad doesn’t bring them to action, that’s just not how our psychology works. Anyway, as long as you still should try to be responsible, informed and mindful on where and what you buy, it is bloody damn hard. Imagine you want to buy an item on a new site you learnt about: you need to go look for information about them both on their site and away to see if it isn’t just greenwashing and good luck even finding any if it’s a small one, you would also need to see if they are certified by an authority on sustainability and human rights but I didn’t know and I still don’t know many before so you need to go do research on those authorities and what makes them such and so on and so on... I shouldn’t need a degree in green economy to just buy a scrunchy.
Edit: on second thought take my claims with a grain of salt as I didn’t rewatch the video or done research to be reassured of where I heard these things so I’m basing them on my memory and could be wrong, although I do remain of the same opinion and I think the problems I brought up can be valid so I’ll keep this here to see what you say and I’d gladly appreciate if you could recommend me some tools and ways to understand more quickly if a company is eco-friendly and respects human rights or not.

author: @rockingpinaple
text:
We can't push the responsibility to buy sustainable products onto consumers... Not just because it unfair or unrealistic (even though it is) but because it's not going to happen. It's wishful thinking. You'd need *everyone* to change their habits in order for this to make a difference. "Voting with your wallet", while cute, only serves to make you feel better because if only 10-15% votes for the environment (and I do believe I'm inflating those numbers right now), you loose this vote.

Vote with your... well, votes. The unfair competition could ONLY be evened out by legislation, for example introducing financial penalties for excessive environmental damage caused by the entity. It would encourage the companies to be more sustainable since leeching off of the environment would no longer be that profitable, it would make products that already are more competitive, and companies unwilling or unable to adapt would die a natural death. And there is a myriad other smart legal solutions that could be mixed and matched to help solve this problem.

author: @roxcjetels
text:
Sustainability is not just about buying expensive designer clothes that are way out of price range. Second hand clothing is becoming ever more popular in recent years - you can get pieces similar to/from the same fast-fashion brands like ZARA, H&M etc but for a lot cheaper because they're second hand, and its sustainable as it's not being thrown away but reused.  With trends such that emulate older fashion concepts and movements, second hand clothing can be a great way to build up a wardrobe based on these ideas not only for cheaper but also more environmentally friendly. 

The message of this video is not that people should be buying clothes that are expensive just for the sustainability factor, but rather that people need to step back and look at how much they buy and the influence that consumerism has had on our modern day in relation to the environment and social welfare. The demand for products and trends is what makes fast-fashion so unsustainable and thus it's not the price tag that's the problem and the need to change that but the need to change how much clothing we buy and the rate at which we buy it.

author: @user-ll4cu5dh3b
text:
I roll my eyes when I see comments claiming you need to buy expensive designer brand clothing for it to be durable... 

Even fast fashion brands like Shien, Zara, and H&M have durable clothes if you just look for the clothing made with good material - although you may have to pay a slightly more premium than the $3 price tag. Stop feeling obligated to purchase luxury brands because that's simply marketing. The best recourse to this situation is: buying less clothing, becoming more knowledgeable about durable fabrics/materials so that the clothing you do purchase last at least for a few years, and finally thrifting or wearing second hand clothes. [An additional and helpful investment would be learning how to sew so that you can make repairs on softer damaged clothes, making them last even longer].

It's bizarre when people blame "fast fashion" (usually equating it to affordable clothing companies like H&M and Zara) for environmental problems when it's consumers purchasing an excessive amount of clothing that's the issue. I.e., if luxury goods were as cheap as SHEIN, it would create a nearly identical problem as SHEIN. The only reason why luxury brands are not included in this discussion (when they should be) is because there is a much higher cost of entry on how many and how much people are willing to spend for the products, which doesn't say much about how the companies, themselves, are tackling this issue. 

People should treat clothing like food: you're not going to know you're purchasing healthy food by looking at the brand or the price tag of the item, but you will know if you look at its nutritional label. Likewise, if you want durable or environmentally friendly clothing, look at the material of the clothes. Furthermore, you can't just eat an inordinate amount of healthy food to be healthy; you need to diet properly to be healthy. Therefore, if you want a healthy environment, be mindful of your purchases.

Finally, regarding sustainability in terms of "business ethics": I'm sorry to say but most luxury brands still use imported materials from sweatshops when they can definitely afford not to. Sure, the clothing may be made in countries with better working condition, but the material, itself, is usually going to be the same as those used in sweatshops. So, please, don't be deceived when you see a "Made in Italy" label thinking the entire product was made in Italy because that just means the product was manufactured in Italy; it does not mean the materials were from Italy. The only way to know for sure is if the company has transparency over its supply-side, which won't be the case for nearly all, if not all, large clothing companies, including luxury brands.

text:
The problem with SHEIN is that cheap clothing comes to a time where 1) goods are the most accessible ever 2) even young people own the means to purchase those goods (they have credit cards and phones to order) and 3) societal pressure through social media to be different and trendy and stylish is as high as never before, especially on tiktok I feel like there are new “styles” and clothing trends popping up every minute. When I think back to my early teenage years (I’m early Gen Z) im glad that during that time while yes phones were coming up, social media and everything was still very much in the beginning. But when I look at the 12-18 year olds now, they have phones, are faced with toxic algorithms and have online payment options that I never possessed in their age. 
If I then think about how much pressure there was even back in the day to be stylish and follow trends I can understand why especially these low income groups ignore impacts on climate over acceptance by peers. Unfortunately this hits especially girls, I have so many female friends in my circle that feel immense pressure to wear a new cute top to every party they go, or new boots or hats or bags or dresses. I know that they would never wear them more than once. Maybe if the piece is lucky it changes hands between their friends so it gets worn once by a small number of ppl but usually my friends have whole new wardrobes twice a year minimum. As a guy, I don’t feel that pressure. If you ask me, that’s a whole problem in itself already, the unrealistic pressure put on especially teenage girls these days to always have new trendy pieces.

author: @jerrykreutzer4326
text:
The argument that young people are too poor to afford for sustainable clothing doesn't hold up. If that was the case, they'd simply buy fewer pieces from brands like SHEIN, and this video might have been solely about the working conditions of their employees. But this video is primarily about sustainability and waste, because people are buying lots of pieces from them, because they're cheap and they can afford to buy lots of pieces.

I agree that we shouldn't hold individuals responsible for climate change, but we should also call it like it is. And it's precisely because individuals (or all generations and wealth) are making these choices that we need more systemic action.

author: @lenricofurioso
text:
Thanks for letting me know it. Good shop!

Actually the fact that the store is cheap doesn’t mean it’s worse for the environment than traditional shops. Traditional shops need to carry high inventory, which then gets either sold at deep discount or thrown at season end. Also, they need to refurbish shops more frequently than people think and have to supply their shops with regular deliveries. An entirely online shop close to the production center as the one in the video is efficient as it cuts unnecessary inventory or at least it doesn’t make such  inventory travel across the globe unless it’s actually sold.

text: In conclusion (my thoughts): 1. Big companies bad, but consumers drive demand so for those that have the option (ie people who can afford it), they shouldn't be shopping fast fashion. But you shouldn't be doing massive hauls from thrift stores because that defeats their entire purpose and you are taking away clothes from people who need them and thrift stores are their only option. Buy what you will actually wear multiple times. 3. Plus size people often resort to Shein because of the variety and quality of plus size clothes that they cannot find anywhere else. I think that's an issue of necessity, but massive hauls are still a no no. Buy what you will actually wear a lot, and if eventually you don't, donate it somewhere/take it to a second hand store.

author: @Moolahloolah
text:
I bought 6 pieces of garments from SHEIN across 2 or 3 different deliveries. No compelling QC problems. All the seams are in place, all the stitching is right. The cut fits well, and when it is not perfect the deviation is not outrageous. What i can say about the fabric is that some is outright cheap looking, but is not cheaper looking than H&M which is operating in the same league.  The cheapness is actually an acceptable look if the garment is supposed to/ intended to be worn in an ultra casual context. If the fabric doesn't look cheap, it performs badly in the laundry eg some flaking seen. The misses are acceptable when the price is so low, that the consumer cannot be bothered to get refunds. The thing about Shein is that its quality is not high, but definitely not worse than its competitors in the same target consumer range. Yet Shein delivers its goods in a shockingly accurate and short time. Each delivery from Shein surprised me with its quickness. What i can say is that Shein is giving its competitors a run for their money, and that its competitors are right to be very very scared about this.

I would only buy Shein for casual stuff, not for garments to be worn for work or formal outings

author: @JosMyOpinion
text:
When we try to address environmental concerns, we have to take a step back.

Fashion like this does an excellent job of targeting young women with trendy, and addictive, campaigns precisely because "feeling" good and "looking" good are a primary concern to many people.

A secondary concern is cost. Much like one woman said,  "why would I get that if I can get something else, cheaper?" Great question!

So, why? Well, when we explore environmental pollution in developing nations we always pay attention to the economic conditions of the people. If people do not earn substantial enough of a living to cover all costs and feel secure, they do not care about external factors that require activism.

The same can be said for young people. We know that they are aware of the environmental concerns and a potential climate crisis, but they can't see it. Simultaneously, their own resources are drained constantly by excessive costs in housing, living, childcare, transport, etc... So things that make them feel good become a priority at the lowest cost, rather than as an environmentally conscious choice.

To improve the environmental impact of fashion, we must first reduce the cost of living, or improve the economic conditions of the target markets, create supply chains that don't stop at the end user, but instead run all the way to the recycler, and most of all reduce the influence of marketing campaigns on the psyche of young people about what fashion actually means.

author: @Ubeqween
text:
Love this, thank you. I have ordered from them before, and know I am only going to be disappointed. So I don't buy from there. The quality is worse than forever 21 and the fabric is too flimsy to look professional or work for everyday wear. However, I can see why its so tempting to buy. It looks cute, and it looks great on Instagram models. If you can get the same look for less, why wouldn't you? This is especially relevant when  we have a throw away culture. 

I grew up mildly poor, and my mom would bring us to  the thrift store and super sale sections at the mall if we were lucky, to buy new clothes. As second hand shopping  has become more trendy, the price tags on second hand shop clothing has become more expensive too. I remember my mom telling me "why would I shop second hand if I can get it new from Ross or Forever 21 for the same price?". The competitive pricing of fast fashion vs second hand shopping has gotten in the way of allowing people with lower incomes to have the option to buy more sustainable clothing from second hand shops. I think this is one of the major reasons why shien and fast fashion brands are getting so many customers.
I'm not sure if the slow fashion trend is catching on with Gen Z, but I noticed Millennial, and even younger zellennials like me are getting more attracted to the slow fashion. Trend. I do notice more social media presence for Millennial to Gen x than Gen Z for the slow fashion trend. I think the slow fashion trend has helped me a lot to prevent me from buying as much and helped me be more environmentally  conscious about my clothing purchases. I hope the trend will find its way to Gen z soon. I think it will be the answer to the problem. Consumerism culture is just so toxic for the environment and our mental health...

author: @anuna116
text:
In comment sections underneath shein shopping posts I often see heated arguments, usually with one side calling out people for not caring about the environment, and the other saying not to shame poor people/that there's class issues with buying more expensive sustainable clothes. And I agree that shopping truly sustainably and fairly is hard to attain for a lot of people, and also that realistically people still want to look cute and not buy one 100$+ piece of organic fair trade clothing a year. But it's the HAULS that are just so unnecessary and if you're spending 200$ on a haul every few months to get hundreds of shitty pieces you're going to wear 1-2 times in a season and you're still using the argument that you can't afford better things/the 'you're elitist/classist' defence, you should get a grip. Also the celebs. 
At least try to buy the pieces made of sturdier materials or natural materials, and learn to 'read' the cut to figure out how well it might sit and how practical it will be, and make sure to wash and dry everything properly. At least then they might last longer, and if they come apart, you could still recycle and make something new for yourself if it's cotton or linen, and if you do throw it away, it will not break apart into a zillion microplastics. I have cotton/linen zara/h&m pieces found on sale (in shein price range) that I've been wearing for 8-10 years now. It's doable to slowly change your wardrobe/habits and also thoughts - do you really need to keep up with a 52 week trend cycle? Who is it for? 
There IS a place in between 'i can't do anything about it anyway and why shouldn't i have more stuff' and 'i must be a perfect zero-waste vegan'. Everyone can start moving away from former towards the latter and it will be better to have millions of moderate people than a handful of perfect ones.

author: @nolalove9723
text:
I've heard of Shein, but have never shopped there. I'm not a fan of online clothes buying. The probabilities of the clothing not being the right size or color shown, and disappointment in quality of design and textiles used doesn't make the hassle worth it. Plus the length of time from ordering to receiving is a huge negative. With the probability of never receiving some or all of your order a real possiblity. 

I would rather make the physical effort to either shop local, or to go to Target or the mall and shop at stores like H&M and Hollister, and know I'm happy with my purchase. And I'm not the type to use and discard clothing or accessories. An item has to be falling apart or have developed unrepairable damage from use and time before I will discard it. 

Fast fashion is in theory is a lauded concept. In the sense that it generates a variety of choices for consumers. An amount of choices never seen before in consumerism. 

Colors, cuts, designs.... the world is full of endless possibilities. The direct downside of fast fashion though is that for the vast amount of clothing produced every year; what isn't sold has to go somewhere. And the very concept of 'easy buy-easy discard' has lead to a culture of disposal of unwanted goods as soon as they wear out or become unfashionable. And with quick turn-out companies like Shein that produce their products at such a high volume; the quality of those items isn't of the highest, most durable quality. So this creates a revolving and unsubstable system of unsold and discarded clothing being sold to vendors in 3rd world countries, and landing in waste dumps around the world.

author: @mg4361
text: The real question that everyone should be asking, and not jusy for fashion is, why does lower cost to the environment translate in higher cost to the consumer? Why are plant based milks much more expensive than cow milk, if cows require so much land for pasture? Why are these rags from China cheaper than used clothes sourced locally? Why are European railways more expensive than taking a short-haul flight with Ryanair & co.? The cost to the consumer is almost inverse to the environmental cost right now. In these circumstances, you cannot expect the students and underpaid gig-workers to sacrifice their non-existant funds to be sustainable. Appealing to people's conciousness while also essentially forcing them to buy the cheap stuff is a bit dishonest.

author: @studioghiblets958
text:
I am currently 24. For the last two years I’ve been changing the way I look at my clothes and fashion. I try to only buy clothes I absolutely am in love with and ones that I want in my wardrobe for the next foreseeable future but also ones that will last into that future. The sad thing with all the fast fashion clothing is that it is all synthetic fabrics. Some of the garments are quite cute and can trend for a while, but the low quality of the cheap synthetic materials will give before you have the piece for a year. I try to buy things made with a high or all cotton/linen fabric to help the longevity of my clothes, but that also means I have to be very conscious that this thick 100% cotton shirt may cost 50 dollars but that’s the price I have to pay if I want something that is hand made and sourced from good materials. 

I think for people my age, sometimes even including myself, it is hard to fully be aware of the future and also your impact on it. Plus is you’re making 12 an hour and have to pay rent, spending 50 dollars on a shirt is not possible or is only a rare splurge. It’s hard for a lot of people to see that, though I get 1 shirt for this much, it will last me the next 8 - 10 years and in the time I will slowly build up my collection of nice clothes which will also last a long time, saving me money in the long run. 

I think we need way more education not only from schools, teachers and influencers, but also parents need to step in and stop being so wasteful. Regardless on their opinion of climate change and global warming, everyone should just be teaching that things you own are precious and deserve respect. So many people in the generations above me have taught younger people that things are disposable: food, plastic cups, TVs, cloths, last year’s iphone. This kind of mentality is really ingrained and it’s really sad. 
If anyone is reading this essay I wrote, I challenge you to become more aware of things you’re unintentionally wasting. When you see people close to you doing those same thing, without acting superior or anything, call them out on it. The more we can become aware of our impact the better. 
Also in case this is news to you, recycling is the LAST RESORT in the recycle chain that you can do. 
I hope that if I live to my 40s the world hasn’t been drenched in flames as we work to get pollution towards extinction. I hope I can see you all in that world 🤍 

Be safe and live sustainably

author: @chitru1983
text:
SHEIN is very popular in the Philippines too. In provinces where there aren't a lot of choices, they deliver at your doorstep for cheap. Their clothes are wearable too.  From where I'm from, people wear clothes til they worn out. We don't have an issue with changing wardrobes per season. So while we get it cheap, we maximize its use. it's a different story in the west I guess. 

Also, I read an article how they keep costs low by keeping their inventory very low. They almost always manufacture only what's selling and don't have a lot of excess inventory that other brands have to put on sale in physical shops. Also, they don't spend on maintaining stores and shipping to locations across the globe. In a way that's less waste when they manufacture only what they can sell. How buyers actually use and dispose off these products is another story. Not so much a problem where I'm from since we use textiles to its very last shred - we cut unused shirts and use them to make mops in the home for example and so much more. Or better yet, it is quite a tradition to give clothes you have outgrown to your next sibling. 😂

At the cheapest shop I could get a jacket for 18 quid, at another shop across the street they sold jackets for 800 quid, and on one other it was 80 quid. Obviously I ended up getting the 80 quid one, but if I was someone with a slightly lower budget I could've perhaps gotten one for 50 quid that was still comparably more well sourced and well made than the 18 quid one, and since I had a slightly higher budget I might have spent 150 quid on a high quality jacket meant to last 10 years rather than only expensively branded ones being available in that price range.

These days even to get a slightly higher quality you generally have to pay 10x more.

author: @JasonB808
text: It’s the dopamine rush when people are shopping. Successful retailers know how to use this to their advantage. I never knew about this company until now.  But I knew there were companies doing mystery boxes.  It’s cheap because only a few items in the box are worth keeping, sometimes none of the items are worth keeping.  The Mystery aspect is what I think makes the dopamine rush stronger. Basically the brain experiences a pleasure response with every purchase.  Then with this TikTok and Instagram era, youth are getting a secondary pleasure response when posing their short video or picture of what they got in the mystery box.  When other youth see these posts, they get FOMO and want to be a part of that.

author: @probablyaman
text:
Why blame these young consumers as the problem? We NEED to blame the company and the regulators instead. 
Take Coca-Cola for example, back in the days when they only sell cokes in glass bottles, they actually have a whole system of collecting these used glasses and they would clean them and refill them again. But when plastic came around, THE COMPANY realised it was cheaper to sell cokes in plastic bottle than to have the whole system in place to collect used glass bottles. And now we blame the CONSUMERS for not recycling their freaking plastic bottles. 
Our regulators ALLOWED these companies to destroy our environment. And yet now we are coming up with policies that ban one time plastic, putting the faults on the consumers again! We NEED to regulate these companies from their production, they should not even be allowed to use harmful materials in their products in the first place. 
I know that consumers are to blame for buying these products. But you know what? Most people who buys cokes recycle them afterwards. But those bottles need to be melted and manufactured again to turn into a usable bottle. While the glass bottles before were simply REUSED. And now Coca-Cola are spending millions in ad campaigns saying they are “carbon neutral”, and they use 100% recycled plastics. But in reality these companies know that as long as they keep using plastic and putting the blame on consumers, they can get away with massive profits. 
Back to this video, while it does a great job explaining the problem. I don’t think by simply demanding more transparency from SHEIN would be enough. Even if all people who have watched this video BOYCOT the company would not be enough. We need to regulate these companies from the source!!!

I've had these pants for three years now. And my coat, that I paid 80$ for? I will be able to wear it in the winter for years to come. I think we have been taught that clothes are supposed to be cheap, that it's a consumable product that is meant to be used and then thrown away or given to retail after a year or next season. I rarely shop because I dislike it, and when my band was doing a photoshoot, our vocalist looked in my closet to help me pick an outfit and asked "where do you keep your tops?" I had none. People are used to just buying a bunch of clothes on sale or for a cheap sum, to avoid reusing an outfit at a party or to have a different one every day. We need to somehow change our mindsets about clothes, so we can actually take legal actions against companies like SHEIN, who are underoaying their workers and not reporting properly. I understand why it is as it is, but I do not accept it and I will do my best to be a positive change.

In the end, I might end up regularly wearing only 10% of them (mostly accessories, socks, home wear).

However, given the extreme low prices, I would still "test buy" some items once in a while.
