author: @oldmckayla10
text:
I'm Gen Z ('98). I shop Shein. 

I have never seen these influencers/hauls. My algorithms are more sustainable or handmade, and I see more secondhand or small business fashion content. 

How I found Shein- 
1. They have plus sizes and there's no plus size stores available locally.
2. Cost -- I can't afford $60 Tshirts and $80 pants, but $4-12 price for an article of clothing is cheaper than thrift stores. 

Shein has allowed me to replace years-old clothes, have clothes that fit well and get compliments, and have enough outfits to wear through the week -- Shein was a lifechanger for me. 

And I love that the reviews actually matter! They have measurements, they're written by real verified purchases, and I like seeing different shaped bodies in the same clothes. Like I definitely appreciate seeing unflattering or real lighting photos so I can make a more informed decision. 

Now everytime I order from there, I do think about how the worker's must not be getting paid well enough, and it can't really be sustainable/affordable to ship this internationally. But workers in America (including my household) don't get paid enough either, and for some reason, this Shein company is what working class Americans can afford. 

So until there are more affordable plus size stores available locally, they'll keep getting my business. And as I build my wealth, I hope to make and shop more handmade built-to-last fashion.

author: @tedpiano

As a Gen-Z, I hope we can emphasize second-hand designer clothing services, like Grailed. In my experience you get quality high-fashion designers, new or gently used, for more affordable prices than in the store, so it finds a new home instead of going to a landfill.

At the end of the day, if you want people my age to buy more sustainably, then the products have to show off the sustainability, like it's a status symbol.

author: @yukizheng7280
text: Thanks for this video! Really helps to shine a light on unethical companies like SHEIN. It's really upsetting to see how young people nowadays choose consumer goods over their own planet. Pls cut the "I don't have enough funds nor job to afford for sustainability" excuse when you're wearing branded  clothes, having high end phone or goods/food!! That's not showing anyone that you "don't have enough money." If you can treat your phone well, I certainly don't see why you can even have a reason not to treat your planet well SINCE YOUR phone is LITERALLY made of materials from different parts of this world (FYI just in case u are unaware)! What needs to change is their mindset and how these things come into practice. For sure, capitalist and businesses have a lot to do in this in regards to influencing young consumers' mindset. Businesses love to prey on peoples' insecurities to profit ofc, but learn to change that attitude and get out of the FOMO. Insecurity is NOT all bad. In fact, everyone has them! Learning how to deal with them, and accept you for who you are instead of letting others prey on you to consume more (and ruin the planet) will help you become more conscious about your surrounding environment. Another thing to consider is that, consumers themselves also have to get in the know and understand where these things are coming from and how it came to be. We have the power to change and demand businesses to do for the better. If you really care for the environment, you do it, show it, and voice it. Saying you don't have money just won't cut it. We only have one planet Earth, please treat it well. We are human, and we have to learn how to  give back/practice gratitude in order to see actual changes. Every person on this planet has to do something for real changes to progress over time. Thanks for reading, and not trying to offend anyone here. Simply speaking the truth from my personal experiences! 🙂

author: @fl00ra22
text:
I don’t really understand why consumers push all the blame to the corporations, both contribute to environment issues equally. Overconsumption of fashion, overconsumption of food, and a lot of things people are the ones willing to harm the environment for, for the sake of convenience and greed. Boycotting 1 company is never enough if the attitude and lack of responsibility still persists when it comes to other companies and other aspects of living. Also, consumers are way too greedy for Shein to even be 50% boycotted. They’ll blame corporations for not having a 4XL, not being affordable enough, etc. to them it’s always someone else’s fault, that’s why the issue will never resolve. If people can take some responsibility and realise that we consumers are the ones actually controlling corporations then something will change. But no one believes they’re at fault, or they’ll say 1 person is not enough, it’s the 1% at fault, yadayada. We can’t look at corporations like people with brains or consciousness, there isn’t. We fully control it, we are it’s brains. It supplies whatever we demand. Boycotting Shein but supporting Zara is not enough. If we really want the environment to be better, that’s giving up convenience and many other things, that’s an expense on our side. You tell me if consumers are selfless enough for it, or will they keep their mouth shut and still point their fingers at corporations. People are comfortable SAYING they want to boycott Shein because they know there’s still 1000s of fast fashion companies lined up for them. 

This was a response I made to someone’s comment but I felt it’s fully relevant to this.

author: @Vvvvvvv3
text:
I’m genz and I’ve done my research on fast fashion and the environmental impact on fashion.
I’ve done my very best to completely stop buying from fast fashion clothing brands and now only buy clothes from sustainable clothing brands that use natural materials for clothing to cut down the amount of micro-plastic is released from my clothing when they are washed. 
But most importantly I don’t buy clothes mindlessly I only buy clothes when I need it and I won’t buy anything unless I think I can wear it for the long term. 
Swapping to a more sustainable mindset to fashion has allowed me to find my personal style that doesn’t find the fast moving fashion trends. 

I’m a broke uni student so when I buy from a sustainable brand I know it’s going to be more expensive so I know it’s going to be an investment since instead of buying a new cheap jumper every year that is poorly made, I can buy a more expensive jumper once that’s going to last a lot longer but in the end going to cost the same of 5 cheap jumpers that I’ve had to replace each year.

Another reason I stopped buying from fast fashion is because not only is it terribly made but also the people who make the clothes are not paid well and made to work in unsafe working conditions.

author: @ste5402
text: Absolutely agree. But at least Shein has reasonable prices. Other fast fashion companies like Zara and H&M do the same thing as Shein, the only difference is that their prices are higher. But then you can see what their products are really worth when you take a look at their black friday sales prices: they drop by half, which means they're still making tons of money at discounted prices. So it's not just shein, but all of fast fashion. Also: who noticed the other brands at the bottom of the Fashion transparency index? D&G and Max Mara, more "high end" brands, are also there. This is not to say that I'm defending Shein, my point is: what Shein does is not ok, but at least they keep their prices down for everyone to purchase, while more expensive brands are also not ok but their prices are sky high. Still, I very rarely hear of journalism talking about how brands like D&G or Max Mara are a problem.

author: @syiridium703
text:
"I don't have the budget"
As a student, I bought sweatpants for over 30€ (I don't even remember the exact sum, I think it was more). After 13 years, the fabric starts to wear out a little. I bought a (Patagonia) t-shirt for 30€, five years in, frequent wearing (lovely t-shirt, I wear it to work and for sporting), no sign of wear
Long time ago, I bought technical/outdoor pants on sale for 70€. It seems like a massive price tag back then. In 15 years, I hiked up dozens of mountains, walked hundreds of kilometers and cycled thousands in them. A lot of them in harsh weather - mud, frost, snow. Granted, they have been re-stitched in a few places but I still use them to commute to work.

Let me know how long your 5€ garment lasts for and then we can calculate "price per unit of time".
I bet that in the end, the cheap is actually expensive.

We need to stop this destructive nonsense. It also saps time from people, as you have to spend a lot more time shopping. That's in addition to the workers' suffering and environmental damage.

Buy quality, wear it a long time.

And if you are a student, go for a hike instead of a beer and you will have the money.

author: @Bunny-ch2ul
text:
My pet peeve with people complaining that they can't afford trendy clothes otherwise is that it's basically all middle class people. Lower income people understand that they need to buy a few decent things, and they need to last, or buy second hand. You have to have some means to make a habit of buying throwaway clothing. I feel like that's something that needs to really be stressed. People like to say, "Oh, but I can't afford Zara for cool clothes" (or wherever.) but then they buy four tops that they'll only be able to wear a handful of times each. A piece from a somewhat better quality store is going to last longer. We really need to stress buying fewer quality pieces. You'll look better. It's better for the planet. It's better for workers. 

Everything you buy should sting a little. If it doesn't sting a little, it means you need to be saving up to buy something a bit better.

We use our old, unusable clothes to clean the floor of the kitchen or some other things. I am not buying much cloth for two years now because I want to finish what I already have. I keep 1-2 dresses without wearing them for special occasions. Otherwise I just repeat everything. I don't buy anything that I would probably use one or two times. I first think if the thing is needed or comfortable or I really really like to spend money on. I stopped buying uncomfortable shoes because I wasted a lot of money on shoes that I never wear before. I like that my lifestyle has less impact on the environment. 
I don't have any money problem now, not because I earn a lot. Simply it is due to my changing buying habit. I give the most priority to saving money for medical cost as we have private medicare only. I have everything I need here in a developing country. The lamons in our yard give me more happiness than a bunch of clothes and shoes could ever. I planted some onions on the yard and they are growing well. They make me happy. Life is so much great when you can just lower your expectation a little and stop running the race. Why do we do everything? Isn't it just be happy?

If every neighbourhood has a swap shop where people could drop off what they didn't want and pick up what they did want! Crafters could dismantle the items that are too worn and save and sort the buttons. If this were organized by a jobs programme instead of a profit system it would be worth doing. As more and more jobs get automated there's still lots of work that needs doing that people don't make a profit from without exploiting people, and work that needs doing that doesn't get done. Wouldn't it be great to pull invasive lake weeds by hand instead of using herbicides? Cheerful volunteers do it sometimes and make a party of it but if a UBI programme is to get off the ground maybe it should be built on a 100 hours a month job programme that gets things done that aren't profitable but worth doing.

text:
Fast fashion has a really bad effect on the environment. It is also a problem that the lifespan of clothes is shortened, but I think water pollution is the biggest problem. Synthetic fiber clothing releases microplastics every time it is washed and flows into the sea and rivers. One million tons of microplastics per year come from the process of washing synthetic fibers, accounting for about 35% of microplastics entering the sea. 
Various fashion industries are making a lot of efforts to make clothes with sustainable fibers. But this is not a fundamental solution. The most important thing is to reduce the consumption of clothes and to wear clothes purchased for a long time. A study found that doubling the number of clothes can reduce the amount of carbon emitted by the clothing industry by 44%. (조보민)

author: @Demonomania87
text:
Patagonia has the right idea and are sending a much-needed message, but it's not realistic to think that their way of manufacturing and selling clothes will provide sustainable clothing to the world. I say this as an ex-employee of Patagonia who still loves everything they do. That said, there are lots of things you can do to help even on a budget: 
A) buy second hand clothing. Of course there are thrift shops, yard sales, flea markets, 'vintage shops' and the like but also a growing number of online platforms for buying used clothes so it has never been easier. Every item received from someone else instead of pulled off a retail shelf is one item saved from the landfill and also one item not harming the environment again through production. Not to mention it's often cheaper (or gets you better quality than the same money in a retail shop). 
B) when buying new, don't go for the cheapest or on sale items, because those are often manufactured with bigger environmental impact, poorer worker conditions and worse durability. Carefully look at the garments you are considering, and if possible inspect them physically. You will often find there is a direct relation between durability (flimsy fabrics, poor stitching, quickly fading colours etc) and price, and this is truer the closer you get to the bottom end of the market. 
C) When buying clothes made of cotton, try to get organic if at all possible. Conventional cotton farming is one of the most polluting and environmentally taxing forms of agriculture in existence. Organic cotton farming isn't perfect but it's a lot better.

author: @danaa4508
text:
Nowaday this industry is considered the second largest polluter in the world after oil. This is a consequence of the large amount of items  manufactured every year (80 Milion)
, of which the vast majority ends in landfill. only in UK more than 30.000 tones of clothes ends there annually, being thus the fastest growing category of waste in the country
the environment impact on the planet is so colossal that it's estimated that by 2050, global clothing sales could more than triple
 
*
for example, we buy and sell 2 billion of t-shirt, which are produced on cotton plants that  require 2700 liters water and pesticides that can be carcinogenic, harm the health and the  ecosystem.
as a result of this process, of dipping into bleaches and dyes the tshirts, its released contaminated toxic water That contains harmful  and chemicals compound  
despite that exists organic cottons, but this,  represents less than the 1% of the 22,7 million metric tons of cotton produced 
 
Only in Bangladesh 4.5 millions people are working in the poor conditions and with low wages that this industry generates
 
 
it is shown that  Clothing productions accounts for 10% of global carbon emissions (just for transportation) and the Garment production from 1994-2014 has increased a 400%, (80 billion garments per year)
 
as a plus, clothes requieres per house the use 40 gallons of water and does nearly 400 loads of laundry per year, 
 
We the consumers and brands, in order to need to tackle this fast fashion problem and throwaway culture, we have to change our behaviors.
we can resort to services as “ rent the runway", a clothing borrowing service, or buy  to “Patagonia” , a brand that cleans and repair products in order to break this unsustainable industry
 
 Moreover,We can do little changes as  buying second hand. or  recycled or organic fabric. or recycle or reuse garments as cleaning rags

author: @borninprovidence2965
text: If we taught the neuroscience of marketing in schools we’d empower the public to recognize the ways in which capitalist media are intentionally highjacking our prefrontal cortex, activating our brain stems and targeting basic survival needs  to sell their products and services. Media convinces us we’ll lose status and belonging in the tribe without their product and in some cases in order to survive we’re left with no choice and forced to support an industry. Many have mentioned iPhones. I’m required to have a fully functioning  iPhone or Android for my job. If I don’t personally own one they would issue me a phone. Awareness is step one. Ego strength is probably step two. We have to believe in our innate worth as a human being so as not to feel compelled to fit in and get what everyone else has whether that’s a certain car, children, a house, a partner or a new $20 sweater that will fall apart before spring.

author: @mcpucho
text:
1. It's not hard to donate clothing to the needy, via charity (10,000's of them!) or directly to the homeless on the street. Give out socks, homeless people want and need clean, dry socks. Don't take my word, ask them.

2. It's not difficult for companies to incentives customers to bring back old clothing -  bought at store X or brand X - by offering discounts on new purchases (more revenue!), by bringing in their old store X or brand X clothing to use for upcycling in new products. 

Put the textiles right back in the supply chain.

I'm disappointed by the Economist (a rarity, subscriber for 15 years). 

This video essay made it almost a false dichotomy, you can shop at cheap, throw away stores like H & M, the fast food equivalent of clothing, or go to Patagonia. That's it. That's beyond insincere. 

There are 1000's upon 1000's of small clothing producers, that are sustainable, and do fair trade. 

There is no mention of the big luxury brands, Gucci, Louis Vutton, Dolce & Gabbana, Versace, Burberry, Fendi. That's where the $$$$ is at. How can you have a conversation without including these high end luxury goods? I've witnessed people literally waiting on lines in Hong Kong and New York just to get into these stores to shop.

I also have to disagree that clothing is not being made as well. At H & M, or Old Navy, you are getting the equivalent of a Big Mac or Whopper. What are your expectations? Chanel leather stitched goods? No. Moreover the quality had increased at many outfitters - especially in athletics and outdoor apparel - were new technology has made clothing much more durable. 

Patagonia, is an outlier, (have been for years), but look at the quality jump in sport apparel GIANTS that Under Armour, Nike, and Adidas have made in the last 5 years. You can wear wick gear head to toe, never worry about sweat again. No mention of them. No mention of blue jeans companies? Levi's has really stepped up their game, quality is much better. 

What about shoes? 

The amount of petroleum based goods that goes into shoes - sneakers and boots especially - is far higher than other apparel. The carbon footprint (pun intended) is much higher, and these are not items that last as well, they get a beating. Upcycling them is even more important, I would deduce.

Alright, Economist... get to work and do a real video essay now.

author: @XSpImmaLion
text:
This is a problem belonging to mostly an upper class crust of the world, and the fault lies with high end brands that detached quality from the prices they charge, and replaced durability and quality with quantity.
It is a world apart from mine. I'm currently wearing several years old underwear, a tank top that was donated by relatives, and the newest piece is a couple years old shorts that I got as a xmas gift from relatives.
I personally haven't shopped for clothes for almost a year now, and the only times I do is when my old ones are not fitting anymore, or when they are so torn down and re-fitted that they only serves as rags. For the first case, when they don't fit, I donate to a local church that always have homeless people to attend. They still don't have enough clothes donation to attend all demand. For the second case, that's exactly what they turn: rags for cleaning, for usage in other stuff.
What people who are crazy about clothes like that need is to readjust their obsessions for something more helpful and healthier. Which is likely not happening, but you know.

EBay, Poshmark, Mercari, Depop, etc...most of those sellers are selling out of their own wardrobe.

author: @Thetruthislikealion-t5n
text: Please be careful with the Shein website and their customer service team. Please note that they have no phone support. I placed multiple orders in a large amount and have not been able to access my account. Customer service has not assisted properly, not allowing me to regain access. My account is/was a long-standing account.  It has an appearance as if my account has been targeted by the company. Every message they send supposing to help only makes it appear they are assisting. These messages keep directing me back to what they already know won't work and is the main issue of why I can't log in.They claim the problem is on my end however, all due diligence contradicts that. I have only been allowed one method of customer support, and that is ticket support answered every 24 hours for 5 days so far. The chat method is not operational and appears to be blocked. They are making sure that the majority of the contact I have to  go through almost the full 24 hours for them to keep sending me in circles. If you add anything to the notes within the 24-hour period, the clock restarts. Some of my orders were damaged or required attention, and without me being able to access my account, I was an am not able to do so in a timely manner. Please be careful and make yourself aware. Also, watch unboxing Shein on Hulu. I watched it with someone else and have been well informed of the issues surrounding the company. I only now understand the concerns as I see the power they hold over your information and can block you from access. This is just one of many issues among some of my experiences. Additionally, there is no phone support. Their Facebook page, which I tried contacting support there, I was then blocked from Facebook. All I did was post a message similar to this along with their chat content with me. Many people are posting their issues with the company on their Facebook page. This was an easy fix. But they try hard to frustrate you and keep your money without fully assisting..

author: @sunnirodgers9526
text:
Ok, so I am going to chime in on this SHEIN SOAP OPERA . First and foremost, I am not surprised as much as I am appalled at the treatment of Shein's employees.  Maybe wescoukd start a Go-Fund me page. And  no... I AM NOT BEING SARCASTIC IN THE LEAST. I am curiously serious. On the flip side of this very much talked about topic of late, I have to admit I am and have been an avid shopper of bot SHEIN and TEMU. Now , before you judge, please at least allow me to explain. I come from a family of 7 females and 2 male. To include the 2 parents. My parents worked hard, and soon, we had our very own Custom Tailor Haberdashery. I learned a lot about fashion, style, and the very different things you could create. Now that I am much much older and retired, the pandemic and its financial crisis SHIEN has been a delight in terms of savings. But you also have to know you take care of less expensive/cheaper clothing that one would purchase from a high-end store. I wear it at least 3-4  times before washing on a short wash, cold water, air-dry, or hanging to dry, and depending if it needs it, I'll put in the cleaners for a more pressed look. But that's just me.  I take care of my things no matter the cost, however; I am sorry it is at the cost of someone's unfair treatment to say the vety least. But it is all I can afford to continue to dress nicely and keep my grandkids dressed nicely as well.  

Walmart, Target. Amazon, only to name a few, have been doing this for yesrs.

author: @matreagenyx
text: That whole 'clothes were all made by hand' thing is so exaggerated. Machines have been making fabric for centuries—industrial looms, sewing machines, all that. Even before that, people had tools to make it easier. These days, machines handle almost everything. Handcrafting is for high-end stuff, but the idea that everything was handmade is just romanticized nonsense. And if we can 3D print human organs, imagine what’s coming for clothing tech. Biofabricated textiles and 3D-printed garments are just around the corner.

author: @delia_watercolors
text:
No, spending more on items doesn't guarantee they'll last longer. There is a mass lack of information in this country on what makes an item well-made. Most consumers have no education on textiles and other construction materials. I have plenty of "designer" items from my high school days that literally fell apart within a season. These items were considerably more than my TJ Maxx items, yet many of my TJ Maxx items have lasted 5+ years. 
Consumers need to be educated. It needs to become "cool" to know what you're buying. Only then will we see change. 

Also, we need more gov oversight on fashion labeling. Items that are marked one thing can often have significant amounts of something else in them, such as animal fur. There was a rabbit hair scandal in the early 2010s that was only caught by a suspicious consumer. We need more transparency by labeling what is what, and more consumer education.

author: @smilesam
text:
to be honest, I have absolutely no idea how to know what the "good brands" are. I feel like all I ever see is which brands are bad. And then I see companies that claim to be sustainable and ethical, but then people will say that there's no validating those claims and the bar for making those kinda of statements is super low.
I need a large list of companies that have ethical and sustainable practices, so I can then narrow that list to the styles that fit my personal tastes. Cuz, I don't want to do all my shopping at exactly one store.

It feels unhelpful to tell me where not to go, without giving me tools to tell me where TO go. Because expensive doesn't automatically mean better quality and better practices. I have no clue which companies are charging an extra $50 because they are paying people better and using better materials and which are charging $50 extra because the "brand name is worth that" and people will pay it.

text: This concept is nice. I, myself, don’t shop at these online store because of the quality of the clothing, the environmental impact, and the unfair labor and wages. I think we should also add to the conversation that not all people can afford a $120 knit/cashmere sweater at a brand like Everlane (which has also has its fair share of living wage issues). Let’s think about people who live a working class life with children and bills. Mom and Dad should be able to dress the way they want without recycling the same clothes in their wardrobe. Also, let’s think about people living under the poverty threshold who need clothes for a new office job they might’ve gotten to make more money and have to dress professional. Or, people who just want to look good and SHEIN and Temu are affordable to them. I think it’s unfair to not consider these real life circumstances. Are we forgetting about inflation ? lol there are so many factors that may contribute to a person buying from these companies. So I think we should broaden our thinking a bit. I agree that people need to change their minds about this, but ultimately the opinion of people on the ethics of clothing will probably not change their habits. The reason why people of the past had better attitudes is simple : clothing was really expensive. And now its not expensive, so people dont cherish clothes as much.
Surely advertising does have an effect, but I would wager that fast fashion is mostly enabled by the price. 

So if everyone changes their mind, they might not change their habits significantly, but they might pass a law which somehow bans fast fashion. The law will cause clothing proce to skyrocket, and people's habits will change in response to the pricing. At least that's the only way I see us getting out of this.

author: @CourtneyCoulson
text:
For centuries most people wore maybe a dozen outfits in their entire lives. Now we wear a new outfit everyday. 
I have bought from op shops for years, but it still left me with a massive wardrobe and I only wore maybe 5% of those garments regularly.
Here's my new approach:
1. Make my own clothes, which is what people used to do in the first half of the 20th century. 
2. Never impulse buy. If I see something I like, I will walk away and if I'm still thinking about it later in the week, I will get it. 
3. Pick a colour palette. I never have to stress about colour coordination or having those random items that don't go with anything. I mostly just wear green, with purple, pink and red in the minority.
4. Buy organic and ethically sources where possible. 
5. Buy things that last. It might seem like a big cost upfront, but it's worth it.

author: @Avantime
text:
Just a note: The expensive, high quality item might not be as high quality as you think. The $80 sweater likely has $30-40 retailer markup, and there are costs all around the supply chain - especially distributors and importers, and brand royalties. That $80 sweater may only cost the factory $20 to make, so in other words you're only buying $20 worth of quality.

The higher the quality/price, the higher the markup. Once you get to luxury items, you're no longer buying quality, you're buying for exclusivity and social status.

author: @neothaka
text:
The combination of the desire for attention and social validation through Tiktok and the access to wasteful throw-away products has created another mindboggling ecological disaster. Unironically, most of these platforms are all coming from China, the gift that keeps on giving ... This type of uncontrolled explosive manufacturing is setting back any real efforts to address environmental issues, and just adds to them. The same with China's EV and solar panel market. Just an enormous drive to manufacture EVs propped up by subsidies, but no actual market to sustain that manufacturing. Result? Graveyards of brand new EVs and solar panels just wasting away.

A decade ago, I would have been a bit more optimistic, but with everything that's been going on lately, it's very clear that unbridled capitalism and consumerism are well on track to just destroy this planet for the sake of a few company's revenue numbers.

text: It's inflation, the poor job markets, and the rising cost of living in modern days that turned consumers into cheap clothing because it's the only thing they can afford. When people need to put more money aside for food, rent, and transportation, they need to cut down on something to make it up. When it's hard to get a job, people would be willing to work for fast fashion companies even if they're exploited. As a result, companies like Shein can afford cheap labor, drive their prices down, and keep growing. It's a vicious cycle. We can try to tell consumers to limit their impulse purchases but I don't think we're solving the root cause this way.

author: @marietailor3100
text:
I think we’ve got a few problems here:
1. We broke. No matter how you play it, if you need a tank top or a t-shirt and you don’t have one, buying one from a fast fashion shop is probably going to be the easiest thing for you to do. (This is especially true if you’re a person whose body type isn’t strictly “average”).
2. Even if you can afford to buy something nicer/better quality, it’s hard to tell what is and isn’t good. For example, some things at H&M and at Zara are durable. But let’s be real - Zara ISNT EVEN CHEAP! So when you’re trying to figure out what’s over-priced brand-based luxury and what’s good quality, you kind of just shrug.
3. The internet makes it EVEN HARDER to know when you’re buying fast fashion and when you’re not. With the death of malls, a lot of us have to go online when we’re looking for something we need. But then there are all these sites you may or may not have heard of that have various brands listed on them. Are those in-house brands? Unclear. Some of these fast-fashion sites try to trick you into believing the quality isn’t terrible by charging more than they need to. 

My point is, I can’t blame consumers for this one. It’s too complicated and impractical to really get a sense of what actual prices should be and what they can expect. Moreover, while “fashion” isn’t a need, clothes are. And everyone’s favourite solution of thrifting can be deeply impractical for lots of groups of people - ex. those with allergies esp. pet allergies; people who need a specific clothing item (do they have a  button down dress shirt in you size that will work with your uniform?); people who don’t have cars (in my experience, you have to be able to go to 8 thrift stores in a day to make it work. The bus is not great for that); finding similar items for different people; and so on. 

This issue to me is a classic regulatory one. It’s not one that can or should be reasonably solved by consumers but needs to be solved by governments since industry wont do it on its own.

author: @soup983
text:
This video implies many inaccurate aspects of the global fashion industry and spreads misinformation in itself, which it's trying to debunk. 

I feel like the producers behind this video need to understand that just because something is made outside of North America does not mean it is made unethically or their labour is being exploited and "not paid a living wage," and presuming so if viewing the world in a very "Ameri-centric, western-centric" POV. 

More research should've been done before creating this video, as Temu, which is the Western version of an extremely large Chinese company "Pinduoduo," which manufactures most of their items inside China, allowing for cheap costs and labour, but just because it is inexpensive, doesn't mean its unethical. 

China's manufacturing ability, especially in clothing, is at the world's top. Living costs are low, and statistics show that 95% of people working in factories in the clothing sector in China are paid ethically and fairly. So, if you buy from Temu, you're likely to spend your money paying for something other than sweatshop labour, but rather the skilled labour of skilled workers from China. 

Suppose you buy from a slightly affordable "slow-fashion" brand. In that case, they are most likely manufacturing in China in the same factories that Temu shop owners source from since many designs and patterns can only be made on machines invented explicitly in China by Chinese manufacturers. So unless you're paying upwards of 400$ for a pair of jeans that clearly state they are pretty sourced from ex. California (which also has an extremely high rate of sweatshop workers, much higher than China, but that's a topic for another day), you're most likely supporting the same exact factory workers that you would be in Temu. Still, you're spending more money (and in this economy, cmon now...)

author: @juliegolick
text: I shop exclusively at thrift stores for clothes and had never heard of Shien before this video. That said, the high-cost items you find at higher-end department stores ALSO don't last that long! I've bought (thrift store) jeans from across the retail spectrum, and I will wear through ALL of them at the inseam in about 4 months. Some maybe a little faster, but even the "expensive" ones last me less than a year. And if I had to buy those expensive brands retail, there's no way I'd be able to afford to replace them before they wore out. When I go to The Bay (a pricy retail store here in Canada), "fashionable" shirts can be hundreds of dollars, and STILL don't look that well made! Or they're SO specific to the current trend that there's no way you'd be able to wear it in a year or two. Until there's a real alternative for well-made clothes, it's hard to justify spending more than bargain-basement prices.

author: @BlackGirlInADoggoneWorld
text: I had a comforter for many years that I got tired of sewing and fixing. It was just aging. (I took fashion design in high school, and my mother also sews and knits so I hawk-eye anything I buy anyway.) I put this comforter in my dog's crate, and she had the time of her life with it for over a couple years. When a relative passed and had all of these stained, old sheets and pillowcases that would not have been great at charitable locations, I insisted that my parents give them all to me. My father hesitated, wondering why I wanted all of this old bedding. Once again, my dog (who has destroyed two high-priced dog beds) had the time of her life snuggling up on those sheets. I think people ignore pet shelters and new dog owners who could really use this stuff, especially for enthusiastic chewers!

author: @divyeshchothani8549
text: The “use and throw” mentality prevalent in Western and developed nations significantly contributes to global warming, particularly through consumption patterns like fast fashion, single-use plastics, and excessive resource use. This mindset prioritizes convenience and low costs over sustainability, driving environmental degradation. Below, I’ll outline how this culture fuels global warming and why it’s a critical issue, keeping the response concise yet comprehensive.

author: @divyeshchothani8549
text: The fast fashion industry, driven by Western countries’ demand for cheap clothing, contributes significantly to global warming and environmental degradation. Western nations often source affordable garments from countries like China, India, and Bangladesh, where labor and production costs are low. This reliance on overseas manufacturing fuels the fast fashion trend, characterized by rapid production cycles and low-cost, disposable clothing. However, if these countries produced clothing domestically, the fast fashion model would likely be unsustainable due to higher labor and environmental regulation costs.

author: @AdvocacyAndAccountability
text: As a former clothing designer, this is exactly why I left the industry - I would design based on what I felt would be best for our customers, and then production team and management would nickel and dime the manufacturers and the design itself to the extent that the garments barely resembled what I had set out to design. The designers, production managers and buyers don't live in a different reality though - their very jobs depend on delivering the highest possible margins (retail - cost/retail) for stockholders. It's always about the stockholders and continually giving them the highest ROI every year no matter what. And THAT is what drives all of this immoral production - it's unsustainable by design.

author: @billiemunchen
text: Old news. We MUST do better. Get quality items that you'll want to wear for years to come. Take care of your clothes which means probably skipping the dryer and letting them air dry (I'm in the north of England and I air-dry indoors next to an electric dehumidifier, works great). F trends, develop your own style instead. Trends are for simps. I have items I've had for 15+ years, and almost all my clothes I bought secondhand. I love my very affordable and durable wardrobe. Fix stuff too, even larger wear can usually be resolved in one way or another. If trouser hems have really worn down, make 7/8 trousers out of them or even shorts (use leftover fabric for craft projects). Get shoes and boots resoled/reheeled. I have some garments that just weren't the right colour for me anymore and I dyed them with fabric dye and they now get lots of wear again. Find a repair cafe if you're not yet handy at mending. And your cab alter plain clothes yourself if you want to give it your own twist using patches, decorative stitching, embroidery, adding lace or ribbon, decorative bleaching, tie-dye, stitching on beads or other embellishments.

author: @BroeyDeschanel
text:
Hi everyone, thank you for your comments! They're all very insightful! I'm learning a lot about the contradictory nature of "voting with your dollar" and I also totally agree that the movement against fast fashion needs to be organized (random disorganized boycotting is not very effective). Thank you all for being so knowledgeable - and I'm happy that my comment sections are a place people can congregate about these ideas! These videos are very labour-intensive and since I'm doing 90% of the work (this is a very part time gig for my researchers and script editors - all of whom have their own full time jobs) - there is always going to be room for human error. I am not an expert by any means, and I'm still learning everyday! 

I'm really asking that you please watch the entire video before you make a judgement! I have no problem with you telling me why I'm wrong, but at least hear my entire argument first. There are many comments from people who just watched the intro and judged the entire video based on that, which I find quite disappointing because I do try to engage with both sides as best I can. I am also aware that there is no cut and dry solution to this problem - I am mostly just trying to poke holes in the "classism" argument. 

Also guys - the bags and packages in the background are EMPTY and recycled from my parents' recent move, and from different bags my roommate and I have saved over the years. C'mon lol. 

Anyways, hope you enjoyed the video! Have a happy holidays <3 and hope you all stay safe!

author: @poiseblemiramoon6992
text:
A big undertone in this argument, is still that participating  in such capitalist system of core exploiting the periphery in fast fashion, is something that participants in the global north should feel bad, alarmed or guilt, and that they should be stopped. Even though you did hint that the power of the system, though downplayed, is more powerful than the person, i find that this online argument is still centered around the person by virtue of the exchanges online being literally inter-personal. We are persons that empathise with persons; it is easier to think, and to start thinking, in terms of person as opposed to system. How to thus make this argument be effective needs to make a statement on explicitly why the person needs to feel so personally involved that they should take personal action such as stop overconsuming. For a cosmopolitan audience, this is easy. 

But this is a privileged position. Not everyone is cosmopolitan, and at present moment, not everyone has the mind and energy, to feel the obligation to care for the other that they might be indirectly oppressing that are thousands of miles away, by virtue of living in a system. My family in the more impoverished areas of Jiangxi, China, would more think of how to save budget in spending for clothes from fast-fashion-like products in wholesale markets, and that is a priority over thinking about what system they live in and to act. And I still think what they choose to do would at least be correct for their situation.

Critical thinking, cosmopolitan thinking, thinking of the ‘other’, is still nascent across the population. And we fall into a paradox where when we produce such videos of cosmopolitan arguments we inevitably direct it to a cosmopolitan audience- thus preaching to the choir. The fact that we use English, a global north language, the more major language of some new Gen Zs that think more of the world, indirectly maintains this paradox. We have a long way to go. 

Saying fast fashion is bad (although it is)- not many people would have or feel the obligation to care and put this to a high enough priority to act. We are persons in and of ourselves and its not intuitive to want to think of the “far-away other” under a global capitalist system

author: @Orianaballari
text:
I agree with your point. People are just viewing it through the wrong lenses. Yes, high fashion brands are expensive. But no one is forcing you to have a closet full of trendy clothes. Therefore it’s not “classist” to call out someone doing a SHEIN haul. When, if you think about it. Did this person need all this extra clothing in first place?. I think the discussion is ridiculous. I am from Latin America, and I have to be honest I am so tired of “Americans” trying to be politically correct because they only know their own point of view. 
Here we don’t have an overconsumption problem. If a backpack breaks, we fix it. If we need a water bottle to go to the gym, we just use a plastic one we already have in our house. If we need something to wear, we go to our closet and find something we bought 5 years ago that is still a functional product. The need to having tons and tons of items of the same thing is a capitalist problem. Classism is directly linked with capitalism and the need to buy things you don’t need. Meanwhile we in the “third world” have a different mindset. Basic needs are shelter, food, health, education and clothing (to protect our bodies). Anything extra that we buy comes from privilege.

author: @d.w.stratton4078
text: Time and time again we see businesses funding news hit pieces about how "Millennials are DESTROYING the __________ Industry" and now they've started in with the Zoomers. I feel like these articles are very telling: we are buying less (in part because we get paid far less and in part because we've decided fuck it, we aren't buying) and businesses kick and scream about it. So starve the beast. Consume as little as possible. Fuck fashion trends: they are made up. When you have to buy, try to buy something that is as durable as possible, even if it costs a bit more (hopefully you can save up a little bit by buying less in the first place). If you can't afford that (and I know that's a lot of us), then don't sweat buying a cheap item if that's all you can manage: the point is just to consume as little as possible while still living happily.

author: @Jazzisa311
text:
I mainly started shopping in thrift stores when I didn't have a lot of money. But it used to be normal for kids to basically never have new clothes. I remember when I was in grade school, someone getting a new outfit would cause attention. Like, they'd step on the table in class and we'd look at the outfit. Usually it was for special occasions. But it was rare. Because kids grow out of stuff so fast, so clothes are passed on from someone else, and they're often as new. I still shop at thrift stores, even though I can afford other stuff too, but there's just so much waste in the world. 
We should also get back into the habit of not throwing something away. If it's out of fashion but still ok, donate it. Reuse the fabric (seriously, who the fuck would BUY cleaning rags?? they're supposed to be made from old, worn-out clothes, right??), mend clothes with small holes in it. The whole mentality of just throwing something away as if it disappears is just gross to me.

author: @Jeebus-un6zz
text: Idk it might be a hot take that the concept of poverty isn't ahistorical and is entirely a fabrication of the imperialists. I don't know if giving a handful of examples of how poorness was marked by different things in the past and just pointing out that there was an extreme ramp up of prejudice around it starting with the Victorian era necessarily rules out that poverty hasn't, in fact, always existed and always will. I think a lot of people who get roped into the socialist camp get blinded by these kinds of revelations and think they see the truth simply because they see  more context than the surface level, but it's the allure of that presumption that you now know the whole truth that I think leads to a lack of caution and due diligence in accepting the whole economic theory. What I'm trying to say is it's probably both- yes the imperialists were bad and put a lot of lies in our heads, but also don't get too excited about knowing that and think that the lie they put in our heads is the whole story and therefore socialism has all the answers for us.

text: hello user broey deschanel i hope you’re having a good night or day if/whenever you’re reading this. i just got into an tiktok comment argument similar to the one you spoke about in this video which made me revisit this video because you made me feel better about deleting the comment and stopping the stress, so thank you 🫶 i was arguing with someone about the politics of the barbie movie, which my adversary thought was a “poignant social commentary” while i though to be merely liberal feminism that’s hardly intersectional. i don’t want to spoil the barbie movie but essentially it propagates the idea that misogyny can be solved under the existing power structures of the world, such as with a woman being in the white house or an all-women supreme court. my adversary claimed to be a “marxist feminist” (i ascribed that label to myself and then she said oh yeah i’m one too obviously) but didn’t think the movie’s feel-good liberal feminism was as surface level as i thought it to be. i thought the movie could’ve been so much more and provided such good commentary if it actually tried, but instead i felt it threw out legitimate terms like feminism and the patriarchy as buzzwords that audience-goers faintly know the meaning of. well commenting on your video made me feel a lot better about this whole ordeal, and whether you do or do not read this doesn’t really matter. keep up the good work your videos are the best!!

author: @lemonlin1627
text: I think part of the additional difficulty across all socio-economic backgrounds, but especially for lower classes, is the time and mental load to distinguish sustainable from fast fashion. It’s not just a higher price tag, many companies that parade as high end or haute couture still use the same sweat shops and mass production systems as h&m or zara. There’s a level of time commitment, research, and fashion literacy to cut through the bullshit and understand the true quality of a product, especially with online purchases when you can’t look at the product in person and images can be photoshopped or referencing an entirely different product than what’s being sold. Falsified reviews, taking down negative reviews, advertising through sponsored tiktoks that are not tagged or labeled properly, using multiple brand names with different identities that trace back to the same company, and other similar tactics all make it as hard as possible for a consumer to understand what to buy. If anyone has resources or recommendations for sustainable brands or collections for clothing and more (consumables, food, toiletries and cosmetics, etc) please let me know! I want to be more considerate in my spending and enjoy long lasting, high quality product but I get bogged down in the slog through a very difficult consumer landscape.

author: @bananamanchester4156
text:
OOOOH I wish we could reach through the screen sometimes and give some commenters a slap. Socialist Mommy is completely obfuscating the argument. First of all, the percentage of the population who live in impoverished areas where second-hand clothing stores are rare is likely a smaller number than the percentage of people who DO have access to affordable second-hand clothing, and choose NOT to buy from there, but instead from fast fashion brands. Secondly, the brainwashing of the fashion industry towards the consumer that they need to buy an excessive amount of clothing to "feel good" is PART OF THE PROBLEM. It's keeping people in debt, stopping them from taking meaningful and healthy steps towards supporting their mental health, and PROMOTING classism. It's how we end up with women being looked down upon for wearing the same outfit twice. 

Socialist Mommy is 100% a fast fashion consumer who is wilfully engaging in cognitive dissonance to absolve her of guilt, as are all the little trolls upvoting her. Don't get me wrong, I'm not perfect. I eat meat. I have struggled between feelings of guilt, and the desire not to change, for a loooong time. It's easy to make excuses to avoid quitting meat- "I'm a picky eater and don't want to restrict further", "but I do enough for the environment already, people shouldn't judge me!" or "it wouldn't make a difference if I quit meat, the meat industry is massive!" are all thoughts that have brought me comfort, but in the end they are rubbish. I just don't want to quit. I'm selfish in that respect, and that's uncomfortable and makes me feel guilty. So to summarise, Socialist Mommy, shut your face. You might have a bunch of people fooled with your rhetorical nonsense, but not me.

author: @VWYL900802
text:
I have a cousin that’s close to my mom’s age, her mom, my aunt, my other aunt, and many others were part of the 60-70s factory age in HK. My cousin ended up going to China to continue to do factory work to feed her family, these women didn’t get a chance to go to school. They left school at the age of 12. In HK education is the way out, it meant life will be different, so quitting school to be a factory girl because your own family can’t make ends meet, and you have a lot of siblings in a 100 square feet housing in the projects, sacrifices for the sake of family had to be made. My cousin started to change her life when she started to learn yoga and actually went to India to learn yoga and became a yoga instructor in HK for years, she quit her job as a factory worker at 55, over the years in her youth, her mom did the overbearing mom pressuring her to get married and she just never got time. She worked for long hours for years. She decided eventually to not get married and just focus on yoga. For many people who worked in the factories, that life was all they have, leaving it, they can’t survive, their education is too minimal. This is a systemic issue. 
My dad knew how factory system works in China. He’s not related, those women are from my mom’s side, he’s just from China, particularly the south. Honestly, people there don’t care about your tiny charities. It just won’t get to there. It gets to the country’s respective charity organizations, but it doesn’t change the whole landscape the society problems enough to actually help the factory workers. Not even a charity luncheon could get there. The real change is change from within. This really is about oppression needing to realize they need to leave oppression in their own way. It’s really is about ignorant Regan era west willing to do business with oppressive China and unknowingly helping animal farm China enslave naive and rural Chinese, who are willing because they can escape hardwork in backwards hard farming life. No matter what, once they leave rural life, with such lack of education and lack of resources, will end in extreme class exploitation, either in prostitution, or factory slavery. No matter what, it won’t change the corruption from within. And when technology becomes too advanced, these people will have to go above and beyond to catch up, with no space for actual self improvement or enrichment. So it’s materialistic gains for the sake of materialistic gains to save face for your backwards parents in rural China. It’s now overwhelmingly materialistic, with no real understanding about humanism. They know humanity, and realism in the most practical sense, but no real humanism. This is why now the downfall of China we see now. 
I left HK when I was 3 and grew up in Canada. I never really knew about the slavery and factory in China and how it relates to me growing up, every time my mom talked about it, I just thought it lame and annoying. When I knew about it and the lengths of severity it really is, my cousins and people in the community told their stories, to me these people knew and don’t care because they accepted their destiny. We are all one people and when you left for a better life, you worked really hard to get there and you can be that lucky, that’s your destiny. For me personally, I find the paradox of this makes me feel ironic because I’m buying clothes that could be made by my people coming from an oppressive life, it’s a different life and different destiny. White people don’t pander, don’t pity. If you really want to make a difference, help oppressive countries choose not to be oppressive. If you believe that you can’t force people change their mind, then, let’s see how current world issues unfolds.

author: @pbrown7732
text:
I've seen and participated in many conversations about this and while I do believe some arguments ARE classist (meaning some arguments people make about this subject are classist), I don't think saying fast fashion is harmful and bad IS classist. We are all responsible for reducing harm to both each other AND to the environment and fast fashion harms people and the planet. 

When I was younger, there was this simple idea... if you couldn't afford it, you couldn't have it. And that meant, yes, sometimes I wore clothes that weren't trendy, nice, or even in good shape. We didn't have a thrift store so I had to rely on my clothes to last. I had to learn how to sew holes, to patch, to be clever. I remember wearing plastic bags in my boots in the winter to stop my feet from getting wet because the boots couldn't keep the wet out on their own anymore. We made it work. 

Just because you don't have access to thrift stores and resources for free/cheap clothes, doesn't mean you're entitled to HARM others just to 'look good'. Appearance means nothing. How you look means nothing. What you do means everything.

It is not classist to say: Hey, if you can't afford it, you can't have it. Especially in regards to superficial things like clothes.

It would be classist to say: You're too poor to EVER have a Louis Vuitton bag so we're going to CUT UP AND DESTROY every bag that doesn't sell so that those nasty poor people won't ever be able to touch these ONLY FOR RICH PEOPLE bags. That's classist.

Anyway. I don't think I'm adding much to the conversation... just wanted to leave a comment really.

author: @gemetcetera
text: Buying at the local mall is probably the equivalent of buying direct from a factory in China; most national/international brands outsource production to china, Bangladesh, or wherever labor is cheap. once it is produced at that factory, it will need to be packaged, tags added, then shipped to distribution center in the country, then air travel to warehouse in country where it will be sold, and then transported by freight or trucking (as we see now, rail workers + truck drivers are very much exploited workers as well) and then maybe it makes it to the mall where it will be sold, or if not, it will be thrown away and/or incinerated. HOWEVER, buying from a reputable brand is better IF fiber content is 100% natural materials with no synthetics (acrylic, polyester, acetate, anything with poly-) because when it is disposed of, it will eventually biodegrade.

author: @MaddHattrix
text:
There is complexity with the "fast fashion" debate, which is why no one ever wins on either side of the debate truly. While on one hand, fast fashion is bad for the environment, has been known to exploit workers for low wages, and is usually of a low quality, many of those making arguments against fast fashion are in a privileged position where they CAN question the morality of affordable brands. Everyone wants to point out the problem, but their solutions are short-sighted and often coming from people in middle class lifestyles. It's nice and dandy to shop at some ethical fashion store online, but the higher quality and ethically sound products tend to go for higher prices, as they have to in order to beat inflation. High quality materials cost more to make, and handmade items are charged higher for the quality of the time and work, too. The problem stems from privileged people condemning those who buy cheaper fast fashion options without offering other ethical and affordable alternatives. There can't be headway in the conversation until that happens. 

Second hand fashion isn't always the solution either. 1) Second hand stores don't offer enough variety, especially when it comes to sizes 2) Not all donation and charity shops, like Salvation Army, are as clean and sanitized as one would hope. Some people literally dump junk there. I volunteered at one of the Salvation Army shops and it was horrible what was "donated". 3) These aren't always available in every community. The pull of fast fashion is people who don't have transportation, both personal and public in their area, can order fast fashion online because of their wider distribution practices. People confined to their homes can access it. Local thrift and donation shops don't yet offer that accessibility much yet. 4) Second-hand fashion needs to be replenished, which creates a cycle of buying. We can't all shop thrift if we expect it to be less wasteful.

Now, yes, there are elite wealthy people purchasing fast fashion outfits every week so they can get a new look for social media. That happens. But even for them, it wouldn't happen as frequently if there were affordable and accessible yet ethically sound options out there. For some, that's an investment in outfits for social media, an investment in a future career in entertainment, possibly to make money. 

We also have to understand where fast fashion began and why so many business owners fall victim to it, which is another angle most people don't think about. There are people interested in designing in the fashion industry, and many want to be ethical, but the cost to buy materials, create, and distribute becomes high, especially when you don't come from a wealthy background. I watch Youtubers of people starting their own boutiques and fashion businesses from the ground up. The problem they always run into is meeting the fast demands of the public, trying to make a certain amount of money to pay their bills, while maintaining quality and an ethical method of mass producing. That balance isn't easy to master and can't be talked down with a "Don't buy fast fashion" speech. It needs to be addressed from a deeper angle in order to rid the industry of fast fashion.

author: @DiscontentKiwi
text:
Also one shouldn't forget the lack of larger sizes in stores as well as thrift stores. Larger size people already suffer from fat phobia in society, and when we then get the rare opportunity to buy modern clothes in OUR size, to a price that we can actually afford (I don't care that the jeans costing 400 USD last longer, I CAN'T pay it). Like, we can either go naked, wear ill fitting clothes and get flak for that, wear well fitting but ugly clothes and get flak for that while most likely also get poor buying them, OR buy fast fashion and get to participate in society. 🤷 Like a lot of people in the comments point out, it's also not about buying HUGE amounts ever month. I mean, I buy a pair of 15 USD jeans maybe every six months.

Also remember, hauls on YouTube can be very useful to work against over consumption, especially from plus size people; having ONE person buy a bunch, try them out and show them to the rest of us, very well prevents us buying a bunch of shit we won't like or can't wear.

I think it's very important to point out flaws in industries, and the things we can do, and of course recognize that other groups of people suffer from it, and try to prevent it. But it's also important to recognise that being born in the West doesn't come with all the privileges you might think, and it's wrong to shame individuals for doing what they can to survive in a classist, patriarchal, fat phobic society.

To be clear; I'd LOVE to buy local, sustainable, female and black owned, organic and well made things, OR thrift my entire wardrobe. But I can't afford it, and neither those small businesses nor the thrift stores carry my sizes. So, excuse me while trying to lead a decent life, while trying to work against non sustainable practices in other ways.

 As a person also born in the "global south" it's very frustrating. But let's not remove the agency our peoples have in the conversation. As a Brazilian, I am ever conscious of the overwhelming and bloody history of imperialism. Our disfuncional political environment is a result of this dark legacy. Yet it is also the result of negligence, because at the end of the day the people do have the power to change the situation if they believe in it. That's wayyyyy easier said than done, but again, it is important to acknowledge imperialist history, but even more important to look forward and what we can do about it in our individual countries. It is admittedly hard to stay positive sometimes.

author: @j3nki541
text:
Fast fashion is another one of those astonishingly horrible practices that were allowed to flourish in our globalist systems. The promise was cheap clothing for everyone and saving time by not having to make your own clothes anymore.
The reality is brutally horrendous working conditions out of the western sight, making cheap quality clothing that is horribly cut with awful feels that legit falls apart after two weeks of use (if you're lucky). In the end you end up having to buy clothing 4 times more often than if you initially spent significantly more for an actual quality product that lasts you a while. (Prize =/= Quality of course, but it can be an indicator.) In the end you save money by buying quality over quantity. 
The initial investment might not be something everybody can do on a whim (I couldn't either), but over the course of a couple of years you can switch things out, buying second hand, or go shopping at the start of the month.
That is not even speaking about the insane amounts of water, agricultural area and labor force being wasted for a pile of produced waste that you couldn't even fathom if you tried, or the pointless seasonal fashion trends that the industry has cracked itself into.

author: @frayacinth
text:
It's such a complex problem, it makes you feel helpless before it- Trying to think on a macro scale, I have a couple of ideas:

1. This one's harsh, but all companies should be forced to add a mandatory extra fee on imports from fast fashion brands at checkout; it's playing dirty, but just that little extra cost can make you second guess if you should spend that money- do you really need this? Is it worth the price? 
We've all been conditioned to look for the best deal, so it could be a really effective manipulation tactic. Sort of like forcing you to pay for plastic bags makes you more likely to use a reusable bag instead to save money. If the fee doesn't go to the company, but instead goes to the government or charitable causes AND it makes fast fashion companies profits decline even better.

2. Revive Home Economics in schools, focus on projects exclusively dedicated to making and mending your own clothes- don't just get them to make something boring like a pin cushion, get them to make/pick out their own pattern, something fashionable and cool they'll get really excited about creating and give them the advice and tools to make it. They'll hopefully realise that they can make amazing unique things and don't need to buy something fast fashion, AND gain the knowhow to repair their clothes rather than just throw them away.

text: I love this video essay but one thing near the beginning. The first, second, and third world in Cold War terms initially meant countries that were allied with the US (“first world”), countries allied with the USSR (“second world”), and neutral countries (“third world”). Third world did eventually develop into something synonymous with the term “Global South” (I first came across the term used like this myself, in elementary school in the early 2000s, and only found out about the original intent behind the terms when I became interested in the Cold War as a teenager) but it didn’t start like that and was largely created to denote alliances in the Cold War.

author: @averybaravik7213
text:
Due to the fact that a lot of fast fashion is cheaply made in sweatshops (therefore wont protect you from certain weather conditions or be comfortable), I would recommend someone to who is lower income to possibly save up and buy something that will last longer and will have an overall better garment to wear, perhaps in colder weather (for example: a thick winter coat instead of an assortment of "cute" jackets from shein). Of course, I also am aware that this is not an option for everyone who is in immediate need of clothes. Though, even if some of these brands have higher prices (typically higher price should mean better quality), they STILL have their clothing made in sweatshops. While yes, the clothing IS still better quality and *much* better than clothing from shein or forever 21, its still being produced in a sweatshop. It seems like the only place to get clothing that doesnt encourage underpayed laboring is purchasing second hand or thrifting. But with goodwill beginning to raise their prices, salvation army being homophobic (thus donating to anti-lgbt organizations), and local thrifts not being as popular or have as many clothes, it seems impossible to find any trendy clothing able to match everyones style. So heres my recommendation: Try to purchase second hand if you can, it should always be the first option but if you are unable to access it, the *very occasional* purchase from a non-secondary/fast fashion brand is (not ideal, but) acceptable. Though, dont purchase too many garments (even from secondhand, overconsumption is in every corner of the fashion industry). Simply buy what you need or you are certain you will be wearing often and comfortably. 

p.s - if you are financially stable enough to support (and arent finding anything interesting from thrifts), try to find some small clothing businesses to buy from who cater to your specific style!

author: @InvisibleRen
text:
[Note: the “you” here is general and not speaking at any specific person] I just need people to speak with nuance. If your problem is with mindless consumption, say that. Overconsumption, say that. I’ve never defended someone who spends hundreds or thousands on fast fashion hauls for social media. But I’ll defend people like me buying a few items as needed until our governments do more to help and provide us alternatives. So probably until the day I die. 

While you are fighting for people in developing counties, please also fight for your domestic friends living in poverty and with disability. Also don’t assume things about people’s struggle based on where they live. Not having money to eat is not having money to eat, doesn’t matter what country or city you live in; it’s rough. Homelessness is homelessness. Disability is disability. Don’t rank strangers’ misfortunes based on your own biases.

Also please note that sustainability can take form anywhere in your life. Fashion is just one facet and isn’t even a major facet for all people. Sustainability doesn’t have to be innovative or effortful. Someone who shops fast fashion could actually live more sustainable overall than someone who shops slow fashion, depending on how often they shop and how else they live and practice sustainability. 

There’s nuance, and a general statement or berating left in a comments section doesn’t help a cause or make your point, they just alienate and shame [vulnerable] groups you may not have even been thinking about.

author: @TohaBgood2
text:
Ugh, a lot of debunked socialist "thought" that wants to lump every evil in the world into one neat and false explanation of literally every ill of civilization. Sorry folks, but this entire premise just doesn't make sense. Say the "Global North" left the "Global South" alone and didn't colonize at all. You'd still have the same situation of the "Global North" taking advantage of better technology to procure a much more prosperous material reality for themselves. The "Global South" would still be incredibly poor because they didn't luck out with all of that tech. 

You'd still have the present economic divisions, but with less slavery and suffering throughout history. That is undoubtedly a good thing, or would have been had it actually happened. Nevertheless, you'd still have a bunch of countries relying on subsistence farming and being susceptible to famine and infectious disease outbreaks. Sure, colonialism made pretty much everything worse, but it is parallel to this conversation. The West just lucked out on the tech and took advantage of it. That's why they are richer.

A group of countries, through sheer luck, ended up at the crossroads of a variety of technological developments at just the right time in history for them to take advantage of all that technology. Ok, that means that a fluke of technological development gave them their advantages, not colonialism. Colonialism was a byproduct of a few nations having massively better guns than everyone else for long enough to take over. That's it. 

Reversing this would not fundamentally alter the situation in any way. It would just be a different group of countries having massively better guns and taking over.

author: @OcyTaviAh
text: I think a big part of “there’s no ethical consumption under capitalism” in youth generations is also part of the emotional fatigue. A lot of kids these days grow up being absolutely soaked in suffering and guilt that’s not their responsibility, and it’s definitely gotten more intense over the years. We are asking these kids to accept their terrible inheritance of a doomed world in a way previous generations have not had to, and to be the ones to change and fix it. Sometimes, people just want to exist and not be made to feel guilty for existing. So it’s often being used as kind of a shorthand for “I didn’t ask for this existence and I am tired of being made to feel guilty for anything I find enjoyment from due to the inequitable systems that we exist in that were set up far before my existence began and to which I truly have no power over.” While it’s a limited and privileged take, it’s not necessarily incorrect. I agree that people in the Global North should do better, it is hard to convince someone that is so overwhelmed with suffering coming from multiple different angles in a more aggressive way than ever seen before that they should take the time to individually be more ethical and feel more guilty about wanting a cute top.

You couldn’t buy a dress off rack, but you could buy the pattern or emulate it, then recreate the look with locally sourced fabric. My grandma grew up making dresses out of flour or feed sacks. But then it was a status symbol to buy ready made clothes instead of making your own, and that soon replaced making clothes to the point that now we don’t know how to make our own clothes, and locally sourced fabrics are expensive and rare.

author: @amburn-left
text: As a sociology major, I'm happy to say that the definition of "global South" and "global north" is definitely shifting in the academic communities. Instead of referring to whole countries, it refers to groups of people, communities, etc that are exploited and face heavy repercussions by capitalism. So for example, communities of color in the United States that face high crime, poverty, and increased environmental disaster would be considered part of the global south despite being located in the global north.
