author: @nihilism1652
text:
Hello! Certified person from the global south, especially from South Asia. Not India or Bangladesh, but their third sibling, Pakistan. Firstly I wanted to thank you for taking out the time to not only talk about the exploitation of the global south in terms of fast fashion but also referencing actual scholars, especially people like Spivak who don’t have a colonized and imperial view of our countries because God knows just about everyone and every piece of media does. I wanted to share some things as someone who has spent their entire 24 years of existence in Pakistan. 

Firstly, I know that no one here actually knows anything about Pakistan because no one in the global north talks about Pakistan in such discussions, so let me tell you something fascinating: the biggest industry in Pakistan, like other third world countries, is textile. I had absolutely no idea about fast fashion before like 2019 because it’s not something that exists in my country. Over here store bought stitched clothes aren’t nearly as popular as buying unstitched clothes and having them stitched by tailors, which is much cheaper in comparison and just generally eliminates fitting and style problems. The only distinction between clothes is that there are normal shops and then there are branded stores like Khaadi and Sana Safinaz, that are designer and local. We don’t have any Zara etc here. In fact we only started properly importing luxury items some time in the 2010s. When I was a kid we only had one local brand of potato chips and juice. Normal people wear non designer clothes and rich people wear designer clothes. Everything is local, but that’s also because we literally manufacture clothes on an international scale. 

My dad is in the textile business as well. He has a modest company and we’re not rich but we live comfortably. Because of him I know about how the textile industry here works, and the way in which the cloth is exported to the global north. It is something seen as ironic here that clothes that are manufactured here cost twice as much when imported in the form of stitched clothes. The cloth to make the shirt is say $1, but after being stitched by some western company they cost say $3, which is already over twice the initial cost. It’s a weird scam we fall into when we buy western clothes here, from stores that are branded. It’s funny because we can buy the same kind of shirt from a small market for half the price, but most people don’t because of status. I don’t know if the clothes sold here (I’ve been talking about our desi kind of clothes this entire time) have the same kind of shelf life as fast fashion stuff but we wear the same clothes regularly for years, and as someone else in the comments said, they go through the cycle of becoming home clothes and then dusters. The way in which the global north wastes things is beyond me. 

Tldr; I’m Pakistani, which is global south, our entire economy is based on exporting cloth to global north countries and we’re paid very little for it. Thank you for talking about our exploitation properly and in detail. The entitlement of most global north citizens astounds me. They can’t even begin to imagine how things are for people who are working class in my country, or India and Bangladesh, or any other country in the global south. It’s crazy how unaware, ignorant and out of touch they are with what happens in third world countries and how they continue to add to it.

author: @zo_mi_di
text: This is just another case of something that started as a way to add nuance to a conversation that is now being appropriated by people it wasn’t made for to excuse their shitty behavior. The classism argument is less about “sustainability is classist” and “the impoverished people in the global north are more important than those in the global south” and more about lessening the pressure put on marginalized groups. I 100% agree that people with disposable income participate in overconsumption instead of investing in fewer, high quality items. However we shouldn’t dismiss the way maintaining appearance is absolutely essential to class mobility. With our beauty standards rooted in fatphobia, white supremacy, and anti-black sentiments, fitting into the fashion “status quo” is more than just avoiding humiliation, but rather avoiding discrimination and overcoming others’ internalized biases.  Appearances are important. Sustainability is important. Poverty is extremely limiting. Overconsumption is a huge problem. All of these can be true at the same time without detracting from each other.

author: @isadoralopes3769
text: Great video, inpiring for change and all, but I just have one sidenote: coming from Brazil, I have heard of and personally met people who, for a period of time, worked on sweat shops in my own city. Although their conditions are often horrible and undignifying, given the work environment and extremely low salaries,  these people decide that the low salary is still better than none.  Many of these people would be starving otherwise. I am not by absolutely any means, shape or form indulging the fast fashion industry and the lack of humanity in such work environments. But I often feel some alienation for the people's condition if working for the industry wasn't an alternative. For people to subject themselves to this conditions, it is fundamental to consider how behind, there is a state that can't provide basic survivor needs for it's own citizens. I am also not stating that colonialism is not to blame for this deprived state or the position this people are subjected to. However, I think this perspective is often forgotten on this kind of debate.  Views on the imperialism debate seem to always be very black and white, but from where I stand there is a grey area in between.

author: @will8647
text:
The ideology of Liberalism is ultimately based in minarchism, democratic republic, civil rights, and Capitalism. The ideology first came in the Enlightenment Era, and which was once promoted as a humble alternative to absolute monarchies became something that fueled many revolutions.

However with revolutions, comes contradictions, and many proponents of Liberal democracies attempted to correct these contradictions. On one hand you have Keynes and John Stuart Mill. Neither Classical Liberals nor Socialists, but in between. These Liberals saw the real problems with unregulated capitalism. John Maynard Keynes in 1944 was invited to the Bretton Woods Convention, a multinational convention held between England and the US and other countries to discuss how to prevent nationalism that would cause another world war through a global world market. 

Keynes was was asked to help construct the world bank and the IMF. Countries also agreed structured their economies to promote labor unions, a massive welfare state, and government jobs competing in the private sector. Now this system wasn't perfect, it was essentially an economic pact with "Western" (or the "global North, or First World) countries to simultaneously combat the "Eastern" (Soviet Bloc, Second World) countries, and also exploit the post colonial, third world, "global south." However the system was a step towards further reforms in the future due to it's ideological basis in humanitarian efforts and to prevent future conflict. 

During the 70s and 80s however, economists in the Chicago school of thought, like Milton Friedman, and refugees from socialist nations like Ayn Rand, would try to counteract this new form of liberalism with libertarianism. Many present day libertarians also call themselves "classical liberals" because they want to go back to a time where the market was unregulated, but unlike the enlightenment philosophers these liberals don't view their ideology or societal structure to be a utopia, but they welcome the problems they cause. 

While a Progressive Liberal like Keynes or Mill might want to tweak the problems such as poverty and corruption that occur in a Liberal Capitalist Democracy, an Objectivist Liberal like Milton Friedman and Ayn Rand justify these problems with social darwinism. From the 30s-60s both Democrats and Republicans were Keynes type Liberals. Nixon ended the US practice of control capitalism and the Bretton Woods system, and Reagan introduced the neoliberalism which was essentially the Objectivist Liberals response to Keynesian economics. 

Under the current political spectrum in the US, both Democrats and Republicans are hemorrhaging because the older party members want to remain loyal to neoliberalism, whereas the more radical and populous sides of either party wanting a return to something similar Keynes. 

Whether you're on the right or the left or even the center everyone agrees there's flaws with capitalism, it's just the left right and center have different reactions. 
National Conservatives want to have a nationalist government that enforces laws that de-secularize the country and attempt to limit immigration as much as possible in order to create a more homogenous country that is unified in one nationalist goal.
Democratic Socialists want a more democratic government where citizens have more power in not only their government elections but also the workplace. To bring power to the powerless whether or not they are citizens, and to promote an egalitarian multicultural community, while also advocating for a large federal response to climate change.
But Neoconservatives, Neoliberals, otherwise known as "the establishment" in both Republican and Democrat parties view the deaths and suffering caused by capitalism as "survival of the fittest." They don't deny that there aren't problems, they just don't care.

To summarize this, saying that "there's no ethical consumption under capitalism" in order to justify being a consumer, isn't a socialist argument but a liberal one, or objectivist libertarian one. Just because you address the problem exists doesn't mean you really care. So like sorry socialist_mommy669, but you're not a socialist, you're just an edgy liberal.

text: Some great takes but also some takes I think could have been further developed. It's not all the rat race, some of the background of fast fashion for the poor arguments are valid. I just find it weird how some people are commenting that they either are or grew up poor and have been able to shop slow fashion. I also did and for much of my early life was unable to.  The US is made up of many different people with different backgrounds and factors that affect their relationship with money. I come from an immigrant family who didn't speak English for much of my early life (I also didn't until a few years into school). Some of our purchasing was surely guided by the rat race but buying a few pairs of shoes for the year from a fast fashion cheap store because the only ones we could afford last year were also cheap and didn't last were not part of that rat race. Language, access to information, culture of the family and of the community, are all axes, which affect what we know and how we can access cheap slow fashion. I didn't even know about thrifting until my mom learned English and had another mother tell her about it. The poor community is often very resourceful and sustainable by necessity so the shaming I see in the comments of such communities is really disappointing. Really glad to see comments countering this narrative. Just because you could manage your financial situation while shopping slow does not mean every family can. The difficult decisions the poor have to make here are not solely a rat race concern. Fast fashion was even too expensive for some of us growing up.

author: @StarlightPrism
text:
Great vid! While some people have good intentions, I feel like many of the people who say "classism" or "no ethical consumption under capitalism" in response to critiques against fast fashion are just doing it to brush off the issue and justify their own overconsumption, rather than making a legitimate defense of low income buyers.

I especially agree with your point about the rat race. Something that bothers me in a lot of "criticizing this is classist" arguments is that they ignore that rat race aspect. Companies don't just respond to demand, they CREATE demand. Prior to fast fashion, it was rare to have a large wardrobe. It wasn't expected (unless I guess you were a rich socialite or something) and it certainly wasn't seen as a necessity. But make fashion super cheap, everyone can buy a big wardrobe, and before long it becomes the expectation. They say that arguing against fast fashion is damaging to poor people who can't afford anything else, but most fast fashion purchases aren't done by people who are short on clothes.

Something else I want to bring up... Many people, even those concerned about ethics and sustainability, are really resistant to the idea that anyone (with exception to the ultra rich) have to give up anything substantial in pursuit of a more sustainable and ethical world. Reusable grocery bags and water bottles are easy to sell people on since no one is really attached to the disposable kind. But tell people that they need to give up, or even just cut down on, something they legitimately enjoy, it's a hard sell for most.

author: @hollynotholy
text:
This reducionism that happens so often in Twitter is the reason why I have seen, in the past year, many, MANY lies being spread like wild fire. People will see a situation, oversimplify it to the point it fits a single tweet as reply to another tweet asking "what happened??" and people will take it as absolute truth, no questions asked, no research made, no critical thinking needed.

I love watching lengthy videos on YouTube because they allow the person exposing their point more time and space for nuanced conversation, yet there are still people trying to sum up what a 50 minute video says in a 300 character comment. It's pointless; at best, it's a naïve conclusion and, at worst, it's a really malicious practice. Watching the beginning of the video with the theatrical representation of those kids argumentation just brought back so many bad memories from Twitter drama, istg.

Like many people pointed out in the comments below, people with less financial power usually try to buy things that will last and that don't cost as much precisely because they can't afford to be buying a new set of 5 different blouses every 4 months. So these people arguing this are also fundamentally misunderstanding said poor people's buying habits. There's much more nuance and paradoxes involved in poor people's buying habits than these people are willing to examine.

You'll see loads of people who work shitty, low-paying jobs with nice smartphones in their hand in public transportation even though we all live in a city known to have daily cases of people being mugged everywhere. But they need the phone to last as many years as it possibly can, so they save up a lot, pay in many installments, and also pay a fee to have phone insurance. Then they spend the next 8 years using the same phone, and if they get mugged, they get to buy a new one at a much affordable price if not entirely covered by the insurance they have been paying.

This is not even getting into the subject of rural people's consuming habits who, in many cases, don't even have access to fast fashion (or have a very limited set of fast fashion shops to buy from).

Not even joking, the city my mom was born in is huge (not rural at all; it is the State's capital, actually, so imagine rural areas there), but up until some 6 years ago had only 3 shopping malls in it. It changed after the 2016 Olympics, but my whole childhood the airport was the same and the amount of shopping malls stayed the same, too. And I'd go there every summer from the time I was 6 months old up until 2016 (so, some 19 years of my life). So smaller cities may not even HAVE shopping malls.

You could talk forever about this topic, so it's infuriating to see it being boiled down to "being against fast fashion is classist".

author: @LadyDanielle02
text:
I think you really hit the nail on the head. 'Need' is the key concept here. You need to eat, you need to have a place to live, you need to get to work and you need some basic clothing. But people don't need to participate in trends. It's maybe nice for them if they can, but it's not a life or death thing. 

I absolutely agree that we can't consume our way out of this. I feel like the best way to get ourselves out of this is to consume less...a lot less, and that's something everyone can participate in, regardless of income. Most of us don't really need any new clothes on a regular basis. Sure, it's nice to treat ourselves sometimes, but do we really need to do that every month even? Nope. And when you really look at fashion and how fast trends are cycling, it becomes pretty clear that trends are virtually meaningless anyway. Is something you have 'out'? Don't worry...it will be 'in' again in a couple of years. Seriously...

Just an aside, but I disagreed with that quote about cars being necessities in North America and Europe. Most of North America, probably (I grew up in Canada and don't think it would be easy to go car-free in most of the country), but I haven't owned a car for the entire 16.5 years I've lived in Germany and I don't feel like I'm missing anything. Oddly, even here in Berlin, a city with one of the best public transit systems in the world, people claim that you 'need' a car and that people who claim you don't are just elitist and out of touch. I don't get the 'elitist' thing at all...my transportation costs per year fall within the triple digits (including long-distance train rides). Show me a car that costs that little to purchase and run. As for out of touch, you're never further away from 500 metres from a public transport station or stop, anywhere in the city....it's designed that way. This has gotten long, but where I'm going with this is that even things a lot of us consider to be basic 'needs' aren't always needs – here in Germany, the auto industry has spent a lot of money for the better part of the century convincing people that they need a car to get around freely. But once you live without a car in this country (at least outside of rural areas), it's pretty clear that that just isn't true.

author: @user-kc2uo3el1w
text: Equating the argument that criticizing people for buying fast fashion is classist to prioritising the interests of poor people in the global north over the needs of poor people in the global south and not caring about their suffering doesn't make sense to me. The important question is: can we solve any political problem through our choices as consumers, and I don't think so, so therefore I don't engage in this debate and I don't shame people for buying fast fashion. I'm even a person that's trying hard to "consume ethically" and while doing so you see how irrelevant this debate is. There are so many pseudo sustainable brands that do great marketing and are superb in selling their lies. And no brand is producing anything completely sustainable in this economy, that is a fact. So, are those people who have the money and try to do their research and fail because nobody can be bought free from the cruelty of this system better than those who don't even try? I don't care. If we try to solve this question, we are always going to focus on an irrelevant ethical evaluation of the individual. We can't change anything systemically by supporting this or that brand, it doesn't matter. And we should feel bad for supporting these capitalists but let's not transform this bad feeling into guilt but into action. We need to organize globally, we need to participate in the communist and socialist structures already existing and we need to stop having these discussions about the ethics of individuals.

author: @thetayproducers
text:
Dismissal of any criticism of fast fashion as being classist also strikes me as a defense mechanism to a perceived (and probably often actual) personal attack, which I find incredibly sad because it seems like both people are on the same team. If we--as a society--collectively stopped valuing fast fashion, changing styles and overconsumption, then poor people in the Global North wouldn't feel the need to participate in such an exploitative industry. 

I think the comparison with the need to own a car is a really interesting one and potentially valuable to explore. Many people (especially in the United States) do legitimately need a car to get to work, school, groceries etc. This is a very tangible, physical problem and the solutions to it are also very real and tangible (better public transit, changes in zoning laws etc.) Fashion is almost the opposite in that the entire demand for it comes from societal values. I can sympathize with the people who feel the need to buy lots of clothes for social acceptance. wE LivE iN A sOciEtY, and societal problems can be very real to people. I think that the critical difference however is that as members of society we have a much more direct way to impact these issues than we do with say, urban planning. 

I won't claim to have the answer on how we should discuss this complex issue but I think that ultimately changing the collective consciousness of how we interact with our clothing and shopping habits is the only solution. And that will require engaging in the topic of how damaging fashion (fast or otherwise) is in a compassionate way that acknowledges current pressures while attempting to change them as well.

author: @Jasmine-gv3uj
text: I feel like what some people miss is that many low-income people have a desire (like anyone else) to wear clothes that are "trendy," which often translates to "new" (i.e. currently in style). This is what leads people to over-consume. Many brands continuously put out new products so people can buy clothes on a regular basis and feel as though they are "keeping up." If you are low-income, this shopping process is going to be limited by budget so you'd get clothes from H&M, Shein, etc. But people who are wealthier follow this same process of overconsumption, just with brands that are seen as "more sustainable" than the retail giants like Zara et al. Examples include Parade, Reformation, and countless others — all of which follow sustainable practices to a degree yet also continue the problematic process of constantly releasing and advertising new products. The problem is capitalism, and the ways in which this system brainwashes people to feel as though they never have enough and that they must constantly "keep up." Whether you're taking regular trips to H&M or Dover Street Market, it doesn't matter. People have to learn to see clothing as objects that have intrinsic value aside from how they do or do not fit into contemporary trends. In order to do this, we have to actively disinvest from capitalism and learn our own personal style, so we can approach fashion as it should be... a creative way to express ourselves and have fun with aesthetics. This is about reclaiming fashion on an individual level and ridding ourselves of the voice in our head that tells us to "keep up" and always want more. This is how we can have a healthy and fun relationship to clothes, instead of fueling insecurities that derive from capitalism and harming the environment.

author: @pupu2410
text: We need to talk about how social media contributed a lot to fast fashion.There was unethical fashion before Social media became really popular, but it wasn't as extreme as today.Fashion used to be dictated by the popular magazines(Vogue,Harper's Bazaar,Cosmopolitan etc.).They took their inspiration from the seasonal High End Fashion shows, that only happened about twice a year.The information was limited since the magazines only had limited pages.The limited information,lacking saturation of media, and slow spread of information, is what made trends so few and long-lasting.Nowadays on the other hand,you can watch hundreds of photos and videos, and immediately see every new fashion show.You don't have to wait till the new Vogue is released to see the new celebrity looks.You can just go on their social media, and immediately share their outfit everywhere, for everybody to see, so trends naturally spread like wildfire.The over saturation and excessive exposure to fashion makes us grow bored of clothes quicker too.So trend spans naturally shortened to the extreme, with some trends going viral within days,and then going out of style in about a month.

author: @lamewalrusxd4781
text:
What's funny to me as an argentinian, is that the fast fashion debate was translated word for word into our society, even though we have 50% of the population under the poverty line. I myself buy stuff that i can reuse a lot, as the mayority of people do here, because we here don't have the crazy fast cycling season there are in the US. And still we shame each other for "not buying sustainably" or "ethically". We have the choice of cheap, unbranded fast fashion, or expensive branded fast fashion. I thrift a lot, i got used to do it bc I'm a theatre major lol, but even my thrifting can't stop the brands here dumping unsold clothes into landfills.
A story broke out recently in the local news about the huge clothes landfill in the Atacama desert (Chile), and i was shocked to see people like my mum surprised by it. The segment focused a lot on the fact that they were brand new clothes, and didn't acknowledge the responsability of the industry for overproduction. That story did the same effect you explained, the "sad kid in Africa" produces individual guilt, so it gets overwhelming, resulting in inaction.
We need to think about a collective action against overproduction, even if in our case is very hard (considering the IMF has our asses).

text:
My god, what a pile of incoherent arguments.
I wanna be insulting because of all the disregard  that fills this video but i'll try not to be.

First, you're not defining which consumers you're talking about and that's the base on why all these arguments sometimes even contradict each other. Who are you talking about? author: @jooliver355
text:
I truly do see your point. I don’t buy fast fashion because honestly I genuinely like vintage/ ‘grandma’ cloths and I have thrift stores available to me that are just as cheap. Though you said purchasing fast fashion it was just a rat race that has no consequence other than humiliation but I feel it’s a bit more complicated than that and ending the argument there does a disservice to the impoverished people in America (which by the way aren’t even just ‘lower class’ but often ‘middle class’ as well due to a multitude of things like medical bills and inflation along with lack of raises )The first example I can think of is going to a job interview in an outdated garment is technically just humiliating but can end up costing you the job. Also just from experience of growing up in the southern Appalachians (where there were probably a good 25% of people who didn’t have indoor plumbing and 50% of people who couldn’t rely on their electricity working ) but fashion was often the hierarchy. The better clothes you had (specifically coming to Sunday church) the higher you were because no one wanted to associate with the rampant poverty. Come with clothes that don’t pass ‘inspection’ and that could get you ridiculed and out right ignored by a decent portion of the community. And when you live in a community that has no money then a community life line is EVERYTHING. 

These are just two examples. I feel like this argument could have been stronger if you acknowledge that yes saying don’t buy fast fashion is classist, but that’s not an excuse for the people who can afford to buy sustainably to buy into fast fashion trends. My Cousin buying two pairs of jeans and a shirt from Shein every two months is not the same as influencers who buy 20 full outfits. It also helps to acknowledge that it’s more well off consumers that cause the micro trends and fast fashion cycles that poorer people feel they have to try to at least be in reach of. ( even though impoverished people especially in rural areas are often still 6 months to a year behind fashion trends) 

The arguments “Fast Fashion is bad “ and “It’s classist to tell poor people not to participate in fast fashion” are honestly two arguments that should work together not against each other since the fashion industry as a whole is basically forcing impoverished people to purchase from fast fashion.

as a south american student with low income and high expenses, i sometimes feel as a horrible person when i buy cheaper clothing (knowing that those that made it may be suffering) but im always stuck between a rock and a hard place, because i have informed myself that the fashion industry IS the world´s second most contaminating industries (right after the meat industry).

i´ve found myself being calm with what clothes i purchase after turning vegetarian, 3 years ago. I limited the times i purchase clothes to once or twice a year, and really try to make the most of it. i wear jeans until they rip, and if they dont fit anymore i keep them in case i gain or loose weight. 

to young people reading this: don´t feel bad for your decisions. if you really think there is no other chance, or you just really liked a cute top for once in a while, go ahead. its consistency what will help the environment, not sudden acts of sustainability that won´t be repeated.

author: @jlbeeen
text:
I think fashion in general can be classist. Because I remember in high school being judged for wearing older clothes, even though I did buy some fast fashion stuff to fit in. I wear a lot of thrifted stuff now, and things that I got on clearance, or still fit into from high school. I also make a lot of things myself, and a lot of my tech is older too. 
I've made clothes for really cheap from thrifted sheets, so cost isn't always the same, but it's hard to manage. Also, a lot of things that people in the USA consider "cheap fast fashion," were even too expensive for people I know (probably due to the Canadian dollar), but the people who struggled would buy fewer items, and only ones they know they would wear, as well as wearing hand me downs, and secondhand items. Yeah it wouldn't be as fashionable to wear a big 90s sweater with 2010 skinny jeans, but if that's what you have, make it work. I don't fit into a lot of fast fashion due to my body proportions, so I'm very glad I can sew and make things that work, but I'm trying to be more mindful of where I get my fabric, and how I use it. And when I do buy stuff, I look at reviews, often reviews of the exact same style that are more than a year old (mostly with footwear and underwear, shoutout to TomboyX for making underwear that lasts for a few years), and when I do buy stuff directly from China, it's because it's something I can't make myself, or can't find anywhere else. Like I have chronic illnesses, so I bought some compression socks that have zippers because that's something that makes it easier to function in my day to day life, and I got a Chinese smartwatch to help me monitor my wonky heart rate, and remind me of things. At least the watch was by a subsidiary of a company who is trying to help, by using paper packaging and making products that last (same reason why I got a Nokia phone). 
Because it's not just fashion, but other things that are seen as trends, like you mentioned TVs and cars. A lot of waste comes from fast release cycles of tech too, and where my two interests are sewing and software development, I'm pretty close to both. I acknowledge that my generational wealth has allowed me to buy more expensive things that last longer, but that doesn't mean that we should ignore the issue because not everyone has that. I do what I can by giving clothes to friends or people I know could use them rather than Value Village, and I do try to buy or make things that last, rather than things that only last a season or a year. The next step for me is trying to find a more timeless style that works for me so I can have a wardrobe that won't be too affected by trends or what others say about me.

text: The argument about not being able to do anything but buy fast fashion, though it has some nuance, is still pretty ridiculous to me because people who are ACTUALLY poor cannot even afford to buy fast fashion. There are local sellers that have traditional everyday (or even sometimes a bit fancier) clothing for Rs. 100 - Rs. 200 per piece. So yeah the concept of only being able to buy fast fashion is definitely more seen in the "global north". I personally do believe in supporting companies and organisations that charge more for their products if they are actually dedicated to sustainability and paying their workers fairly, usually also supporting poor women who might have otherwise been working for shitty fast fashion companies.

author: @FelisImpurrator
text:
The real reason not to shame people is that it does nothing but make them double down in their outrage, by and large. Moralizing to people is rarely if ever capable of being a convincing argument.

Also, something many people forget is that in many cases, the people who own stakes in highly commodified, mass-produced goods are the same people who own stakes in more expensive goods marketed as sustainable. This is very evident in the food market, for instance, as vegan food is often provided by - guess who - the same multinationals that own meat companies. The same ones who will send overworked slaves to harvest quinoa and goji berries and sell them at ludicrous markups. Making a problem and selling the solution is the oldest trick in capitalism.

author: @Caroline-hw9yw
text:
In general I think consuming fast fashion is immoral, but some people don't have the option to be moral. People don't acknowledge moral luck enough (the idea that some people are born into different situations that inhibit them from living as morally). We all know the classic stealing bread thought experiment, that's a perfect example. Stealing can be morally wrong but we can also understand that the person stealing bread wouldn't choose to steal if they already had bread to feed their family. And it's not really fair to compare the morality of the thief to someone who is wealthy, because the wealthy person would never have to confront the same moral dilemma.

So consumption of fast fashion, even out of necessity, is still a technically immoral action but it's not fair for people who don't need to shop fast fashion to critique the moral decisions of those who do (unless we're specifically talking about reckless over-consumption and not just material needs). Just like eating meat is wrong, but not everyone is in a position to change their lifestyle. We can make our arguments for why we should reduce consumption in all areas, but we also have to acknowledge that not everyone is equally able to abide by these ideal standards. With that being said, there is no one right argument for every case of fast fashion consumption and there is so much nuance to every case. I am still aggressively anti-fast fashion and I think people are too comfortable disregarding the insane amount of human rights violations and serious negative consequences of supporting fast fashion.

author: @paranoah1925
text:
American overconsumption is like the hungry ghosts in the cycle of life. There is insatiable desire and any attempt to meet these desires just leads to more suffering and even greater desire. And corporations are using the best and latest findings in human psychology to exploit vulnerabilities and trap people in the realm of the hungry ghost. 

"According to Yangsi Rinpoche, the suffering of the beings born in the realm of the hungry ghosts is far more intense than those born in the animal realm."
(The animal realm is characterised by creatures driven by impulse and instinct. The difference, however, is that their needs and desires can be fulfilled and they are not trapped in the endless cycle of ever increasing cravings.)

The good news is that we can break out of the hungry ghost realm and make ourselves immune to the manipulations of these corporations and their marketing. The place to start would be to look within and search for true contentment. Quieten your DMN which makes constant comparisons and finds you lacking, spend time in nature, improve your mental health and form deep meaningful bonds with others.

author: @jena_rese
text:
I remember when I was young, I got all my clothes from DI, savers, and goodwill and all the girls in my class would make fun of me. They were all wearing downeast, H&M, Vans, Etc. It made me feel really bad because I thought my clothes looked fine, and I didn’t realize that where I purchased them was any part of the credibility of my outfit. I still buy all my clothes from thrift stores, and have only ever purchased from online fast fashion brands a couple times for really expensive items. (Jeans, prom dress, and a swimsuit that would actually fit me — it did not fit me). I don’t think I’ve ever bought clothes from a mall, other than undergarments. I try really hard to be ethical and sustainable, but I just can’t afford it sometimes.

I have a friend who buys fast fashion quite often. She is upper middle class (what i’d consider comfortably wealthy, but not rich), and can afford kore expensive clothing. I don’t care if she buys fast fashion even when she doesn’t have to, but she throws away her clothes when she’s done with them. I don’t mean when they’re worn out, I mean when she gets bored and wants new clothes. She doesn’t donate them because it takes to much time and she doesn’t want to drive to the donation center. I adore her with all my heart, but this... it’s literally the only thing about her that bothers me. As a kid who got all their clothes from thrift stores, i loved it when I found a top that was super cute because it can be hit or miss sometimes (and I’m very expressive with my clothing). I’ve talked to her about this before, but she just laughs it off. I really don’t know what to do about it

author: @geleksk
text:
thank you for actually giving nuance to how poverty is differently viewed! low income people from the global north feel compelled to participate in fashion trends while their global south counterparts are the abysmally waged workers who made their fashion forward pieces.

the debate was always weird to me, as a person from the global south, because i felt like their talking points were so simplified, and, quite frankly, reeking with privilege. on one side you say "shop sustainably" but it's upper middle class citizens of the global north hoarding and emptying thrift shops. because it's sustainable. on the other side, it's woke marxist 16 year olds saying "there's no ethical consumption under capitalism" after they either defend a tiktok zara haul or did a haul themselves. because there's nothing they can do about something as big as the culture of capitalism they are "forced" to participate.

what i always thought was the solution was if we collectively decided to change fashion culture, something that I think the mina le video talked about. sure, part of the reason is how corporate overlords have convinced us that to be unfashionable is a sin then changing what being fashionable is on a weekly basis. but if we are socially conscious enough as consumers to have a debate about it with both sides having merits of some sorts, surely we can also identify the fact that we are being manipulated into participating at all? like i said in the examples, both sides use the arguments they champion as an excuse to overconsume, whether it's thrift shops or fast fashion brands. maybe the subject of heat shouldn't be just about where you shop, but also how often, and... why? do you shop for the trend, as in you have no lack of clothing in your closet but you still regularly shop, use, and discard? questions like that.

and to be clear, I am not at all suggesting people stop trying to look good or be fashionable, because I absolutely feel that as well. maybe, small suggestion, stop or decrease dependence on being dictated what to wear? go for to aesthetics of your personal taste (i think mina le also mentioned this), or just wear what you want regardless if it's deemed fashionable today.

author: @EM-iy2nk
text: I'm from a white upper middle class family. My parents saved every dime they could despite us having a very good and sustainable income. The cycle of clothing went from first we went through hand me downs from our older cousins and what we still didn't have after going through that we were allowed to go out and buy at the mall. If we grew out of decent clothing we gave it to our younger cousins. We would wear the new and used clothing as every day clothes until they became "barn clothes" that I would wear to clean the horses stalls and ride in. Once they were too beat up to wear outside they became chores clothes for cleaning. When finally they were falling to peices they were cut into rags. We went to the mall at the beginning of summer and the beginning of the school year to fill in the gaps of our wardrobe. I thought all families did this. My parents are fairly wealthy and I'm not sure why other people don't utilize their clothes like this. My parents aren't particularly environmentally conscious or anything either. My husband grew up fairly poor and his family did the same thing. We still use our clothes this way to this day despite us both having pretty decent incomes. We also go out of our way to buy clothing used or go through what friends are getting rid of. Almost all of my riding clothes are used and I purchased them that way. I just don't see the point in buying brand new clothing and contributing to this industry.

author: @zab416
text:
If people didn't have the jobs making fast fashion etc., in the global south, that would be pretty bad in the short run. A terrible job with an unlivable wage is better than no wage or subsistence farming where it's not working because industry's fucked up the environment or there's just not enough land. Maybe there are better alternatives now, but in the book Half the Sky, it's true that sweat shops help women not have to sell their daughters into sexual slavery in some countries to feed their other kids. Stories of women who got out wound up with happy endings of them selling tchotchkes to t-shirts. Charities to help women often have them making jewelry to sell. How the fuck do we get out of this as a global economy/species? I don't know.

The environment is another (very pressing) concern. I'm not arguing that fast fashion is good. But in the short term, imagine the horror panic if you and your whole family lost their income tomorrow because the fast fashion was suddenly gone and there's just no income.

text: My only comment on this is that the phrase "there is no ethical consumption under capitalism" has been misinterpreted. The phrase means that no matter how hard you try, you are unable to make an ethical decision on the products you consume in a capitalist system. For example, you can buy plastic free items but those are near impossible to find in a store near you. Having items shipped to your home use more fossil fuels and shipping waste than having items shipped to a store. Is the use of the plastic free item outweighing the extra waste used to get it to you? Same with going vegan. Is having an avocado that was shipped in from across another country better than eating meat that was produced in the next town over? I agree that the global north should work to decrease our overconsumption and only buy our necessities. I just disagree with the way you defined the phrase.

author: @_Odyssia_
text:
As some who is in a country that is part of the "global south", but studied fashion design, this was a interesting, enlightening and telling video. It wasn't the first time I see this time a video like this, but it brought up some great points I never thought of.

I also find it interesting that the fast fashion epidemic is completely different in South Africa than it is in the "global north". Most people buy their fast fashion clothes from local stores and little boutiques that source these cheap brands like Shein, that are owned by local or foreign people. Other international fast fashion brands like Zara, H&M and Forever 21 are actually a lot more expensive to buy than that at the local clothing stores, and they aren't at every mall. Only malls where people can spend a lot cash and leisure time. However, with the rise of the internet and globalization in fashion, it has become easier to for the youth to buy online from these brands. Although, the gap isn't as large as it is the USA and Europe, and most people still prefer to buy at physical stores, there has been a rise in online shopping since 2015 or 2016. However, we do not buy so many of the same products or "mall haul" as I like to say, because it is actually wasteful. Most children and teens wear school uniforms when attending school and only need to buy clothes during the school breaks, and also limits the amount of clothes you can buy a year. Also, it's a seen as a disgusting habit to throw away your clothes if you have excess, so we universally practice giving them away to orphanages or children and people who don't have enough clothes. We are currently dealing with the effects of climate crisis from all the pollutants coming from factories and industries from the "global north", and we don't enough land for land fills, so we are urged to consume clothing products sustainability. We are urged to buy from thrift stores, wear hand-me-down clothes from our parents, and if you have the skills to sew, sew your own clothes and support local businesses, instead of adding to the fast fashion epidemic.

author: @argusfleibeit1165
text:
So many questions. What happens to all of the workers in the "Global South" who got sucked into working in the garment industry, if fast fashion demand lowers enough to make an ecological difference to our planet? What is a different outlet for the dissatisfactions and stress of our lives in the "Global North", if we turn away from self-expression and self- amusement by consumption of the "next big thing", enough to make a difference? How can we of the richer nations decide to be satisfied with less, enough to rebalance the wealth of the world, enough to make a difference? Experts have said that there is no way the planet could survive the effect of letting all the poor nations of the world consume at the rate the rich nations now do. Our food and energy consumption could not be fairly redistributed without ecological disaster, which is already happening without all of these people leading Western-style lives.
I think those of us in the richer nations are going to have to have a massive re-think about our consumption habits. Meanwhile the beauty and fashion industries (to name only two) will continue to design, manufacture, ship, advertise, and sell at an ever-increasing rate, with discarded packaging and unwanted old product coming out the other end.
We have to stop shopping for amusement. Find a different way to "express yourself" than buying new clothes and expecting people to admire you for them.

author: @KateRafter
text:
I'll try to keep this short. The sweatshop and fast fashion industry has made a concerted effort to brainwash American consumers into thinking cheap clothing is a right. As kind of a fashion and costume history hobbyist, I have come to understand that clothing simply MUST BE EXPENSIVE. It cannot be cheap.... unless we cut labor costs far, far below what is reasonable. The only way to make clothing cheap is through unforgivable exploitation. There is no way around this equation. Labor is the greatest consideration of clothing manufacturing. No one has yet invented a robot that can sew a garment (besides a couple of tech firms are still tinkering with mass-producing t-shirt machines). Skilled human hands are required.

For most of Western history, clothing has been expensive! I found a blog post that references what a servant in Holland in the 15th and 16th century would pay for a shirt: between 3 and 10 days of work. In today's money that starts at no less than $320 and I'll let you imagine the rest.

In 1927, a very simple everyday dress cost $5-10. That's $80-160 in 2021. And no, it's not the textiles and materials. A yard of cotton fabric for $0.15/yard then would be $2.40/yard today. Fabric has increased relatively in cost, but not too much. But everyday garments have become shockingly less expensive. Today it is cheaper to buy a garment than to sew a similar one from scratch at home, not even accounting for equipment, and that makes no sense and should scare you.

In 1960, Americans spent over 10% of their income on 25 garments a year. Today, Americans spend under 3.5% of their income on clothing and shoes and somehow get close to 70 garments per year.

So, forgive me, but the only way to right this wrong is to Make Clothing Expensive Again. Of course towards fair labor compensation, not luxury markups.

author: @camipco
text: "No ethical consumption under capitalism" seems to mean two completely different things, or rather inspire two different conclusions. One is "don't think that you can fix capitalism by buying stuff" - rejecting ethical consumerism as a complete solution to the problems of capitalism anda  replacement for structural activism. The other is "fuckit, buy whatever you want".  And this comes back to my least favorite leftist idea - that marginal improvements in oppressed people's lives are counter-revolutionary because they make people complacent / comfortable. This is the idea that condemns the centrists who legislate minimum wages or welfare improvements while maintaining capitalist structures as doing more harm than good. It sucks. Make people's lives better if you can.

author: @flamingo6828
text: Tbh I'm in a pretty unique position in this debate, because I'm poor, and I work at a fast fashion retail store, but I go to a private school which is mostly populated by upper middle class students. From what I see and experience, most poor people do not actually go shopping fast fashion or otherwise often, I rarely see the same customers in a month, and most are only there for special events like back to school, a dance, going on a date, moving in, holidays, or they just need to replace an old item. I think while it's great to update your wardrobe once in a while a lot of poor people wear hand me downs and a small collection of clothes they already cycle through, like a capsule wardrobe. Upper class kids however, it's a different story. No one wants to be seen wearing the same outfit twice. author: @FrancescaGeorgiou
text: Anyone feeling uncomfy about the video - please watch the whole video. Coming from low-income situations myself, and as someone whose dopamine gets a hit from pretty outfits, here's what I do: I still have clothes bought from fast fashion stores 10+ years ago, and a few items from less time than - I will keep and wear them for a long time. Charity shops in the UK (other than in London) seem to be a lot cheaper than thrift stores in the US, and I get plenty of pieces from there. Since I were a teenager, friends and family have been giving me their clothes too, and that's most of my wardrobe now. It can all be reused, restyled, re-altered with some safety pins. It means I can go some years without buying clothes at all (like this one), and the most I would do a clothes shop otherwise is usually once a year. I can still mix things up - enough to put together a new outfit most days, and then I can give my clothes to loved ones or charity shops regularly too. I hope this helps. And Broey, thank you so much for covering the academic concepts that you do and making the information as accessible as you do :] - my brain appreciates it!

I know accessibility varies and classism should be discussed.... but my sustainable fashion choices were mostly born out of necessity - not desire. 

I bought at goodwill and from the clearance rack almost exclusively in my early 20s because i couldn't afford things that were more expensive. i also used to accept any clothes my friends or coworkers were throwing away and whatever didnt fit me i would take on the responsibility of disposing of it myself (donating to another friend, or to goodwill, textile recycling, etc)

so the sustainable fashion debate.... isn't entirely classist. there are options. and even if you have none- promoting the bad options isn't the move. 

(I know the clearance/outlet stores are not *entirely* sustainable - as you are still, essentially buyiny fast fashion - but a lot of times its several steps removed from the original profit, and/or keeps a lot of clothes from making it to the landfill.)

author: @orladdin
text: I watch a lot of these videos and there's often a discussion of 'buying dozens or hundreds of pieces of clothing every year.' I figured, maybe some people do do that, or maybe it's a harmless exaggeration to get the point across. But now I'm wondering if it's not mischaracterising the situation. Maybe Zara occupies a different price bracket elsewhere, but where I live, only upper middle class people shop there. The only Zara in my state is in a very wealthy neighbourhood. Working class and poor people shop at Kmart, and usually try to get stuff on sale. I do a lot of op shopping (thrifting) now, but I only have the time bc I'm currently unemployed. The sizes can be very limited, the shops are often inaccessible for wheelchair users, if you struggle with body image it can be very triggering to be constantly trying on things and finding them to be too small, and if you have a chronic illness or disability you don't want to waste your energy going somewhere if you don't think you'll find what you're looking for. I know some people will think I'm making stupid excuses but I have several friends who want to buy more stuff second hand but don't have the time, energy or access. I totally agree with everything you're saying, and I think the people in that TikTok comments section were misusing the argument. However, this lavish overconsumption, hyper-fast trend cycles and wear-it-once attitude is not something I see a lot of in the working class. Maybe it's more of a middle class thing, I don't know.

author: @nadinesnyder3926
text: On a personal level I know I buy clothing because my appearance is something I can control. We can't control the pandemic. We can't control horrible events we hear on the news or even what our leaders do (I live in the US so obviously we as a collective do have more control here, but as an individual your opinion does not matter) but we can control how we look. So the more cynical I feel about the world, the more into fashion I become, knowing it's an escape from facing the pain of existence.  Growing up we are constantly given the message that your appearance is what matters. That owning things will make you happy and envied. I know that this is bs but it is so much easier to give into it and buy clothing and makeup and play the game and be accepted by society. Instagram and tiktok have made this worse those apps value appearances over all else. We (in the global north) need to completely change what we value as a society. All of us (middle/upper classes) could own a suitcase of clothing and live in smaller homes that use less land and resources and still be healthy and happy.  Most people though do not have the energy to deviate from the norm. We are all tired and feel our lives our meaningless so we fill them with material objects. I don't have a solution to this. I just know that we must start devaluing objects and valuing intrinsic things as a collective, as other comments have said, because not enough people have the courage or time or energy or means to make individual actions that defy societal expectations. Honestly this is a place influencers could be very helpful. If Addison Rae or the Kardashians said hey, all the clothing I own can fit in this suitcase, I'm moving into a smaller home, and have decided to stop purchasing things I don't need and seek joy from experiences and relationships, it might help shift culture a little. Of course they make a living making us feel like shit so they can promote shit we do not need, so that will never happen.

author: @Peasant_of_Pontus
text:
Buying fast fashion does not necessitate overconsumption. Yes the business model of those corporations does rely on oversupply and giant wastage, but they drove cheap sustainable clothing options that existed prior to extinction. I'm poor living in a country that's in the global north, but not really (E. Europe). My two options are buying fast fashion made in the global south that looks okay or buy horrible looking clothes from independent market stall vendors which are likewise made in the global south sweatshops. Most of my clothes are a few years old, including T-shirts and underwear. I spend less than $100 yearly on clothing unless I have to make a big purchase (winter jacket, new shoes). I'd like to buy clothes made locally or sustainably, but they don't exist in my price range. I don't go out and buy 8 t-shirts every month to keep up with the latest trends, yet I rely on fast fashion for basic necessity clothing.

I think the onus is on the lawmakers to force corporations to create affordable alternatives not based on exploitation, but I'm scared that as always the cost of such legislation will be born by the customers while the corporations won't see a dent in their profits.

author: @SecretTwilightGirl
text:
Sometimes I hate the classist argument because as someone who grew up poor and forced to shop fast fashion because my mom could only afford clothing us through layaway or department store credit cards it's not a free pass. I'm not gonna speak for all low-income folks but when you don't know whether there'll be another back to school sale as good as this one or if you'll be able to buy anything even if it is you make clothes LAST. My mom takes such good care of the clothes some of her wardrobe is around 15 years old. And this is basic Old Navy or JC Penny brands. We weren't just buying clothes to buy. We usually waited for really big holiday sales that only happened a couple times per year. We usually only shopped for things we "needed" that had worn down like shoes that somehow always eroded on me by the end of the school year or jeans I'd grown out of. We also rarely, if ever, purged our closets just for our shopping trips. At the end of high school, most of my wardrobe was made up of clothes accumulated since elementary school. I've changed completely fashion-wise and thrift mostly but this still lingers with sleepwear. The last time I bought a "new" pajama set was three years ago. Everything else I've had since I was a teen.

I'm glad people are trying not to scapegoat us but overconsumption is something everyone can indulge in. Overconsumption and the ethicality of fast fashion have overlap but they're not always a zero-sum thing. By virtue of being poor a lot of us already do "eco-friendly" methods out of sheer necessity. Even with plastic bags, yes, plastic is never good but my family would re-use them until they literally tore apart. Or memes about POC parents constantly using random old butter containers (we were partial to keeping the plastic containers from cold cuts) for leftovers. There's definitely a choice being made in having Shein hauls every 3 months. We can definitely get into how poor people (anyone really but especially low-income folks) often have scarcity mindsets that influence their spending and consumption but that's another tangent. Overall, poor ppl aren't some Internet argument gotcha you can use or should white knight for. We're all trying to make the best of an unjust, complexly cruel world. These conversations should have more nuance and far less moral T-posing. None of us should be into social justice to feed a morality complex, it defeats the whole purpose of what we're even working towards.

author: @lisamedla
text:
16:54 is amusing as someone from a third world country, cutely nicknamed "the global south"🤣but I digress.
It used to be that majority of the "waste clothes" ended up in third world countries as second hand clothes thanks to thrift shops and the US assistance that it needed to balance trade with the third world. Poor people then gained the habit of purchasing clothes with enthusiastic consumerism. I mean c'mon a dress costs the same as a pinpop. I can be as trendy as I want.

Which should be a good thing right?

Yeaaa...no. It means local textile industries died. If that was your skill (vocation as churches train them to be) you had only the chance to work in fast fashion industry under death defying conditions to put food on the table. 
Thrifters and donaters thank you for rigorous participation in fast fashion. No honey, you were not being good by helping those poor people in Africa and Asia. It's the thought that counts, right?

And then China happened.

US has a population of just north of 340 million or there about. China multiplies that to get over billion. If US killed dressmaking China made sure it was dead for good. It shipped tons and tons of "waste clothes" to these countries. If that was not enough it corrupted the textiles that were used for local dressmaking. This destroyed the last allure of getting your dress tailormade which was the quality you got. Yey, more people in the mass clothing supply chain.

Of course that moved everyone over to second hand clothes largely from China or "original clothes" imported from...you guessed it...China. The "original clothes" are actually fast fashion only that of course what is cheap in the first world is not cheap in the third world (currency, inflation, exchange, disposable income and all that good stuff).

So how do we stop this....😉

author: @guitarlover1204
text:
One thing I am struggling to make sense of —even as a person from the global south who has direct contact with places affected by fast fashion— is this dismissal of "the right to dress fashionable".

As a socialist man it's pretty easy for me to say "don't dress fashionable if you can't afford sustainable fashion"... But I still need to dress up for a job interview; and while I might get some slack if I go there with a used t-shirt and jeans, a woman is probably not going to get the same reaction.

As far as I understand, from reading and asking female friends, not looking "good" and "put together" is a big deal for them, and while most of what this video is about is not formal clothing, that show of status and respectability through clothes doesn't stop at work. It is everywhere. 

I'm not here to say "it IS actually classist to say these things" of course not, but it is more complicated. I don't feel comfortable saying "don't buy it" because we can't stop either, and because the intersection of patriarchy would mean those women would be facing legit setbacks.

The most influential organizations in the history of humanity create these systems, expecting individuals to solve it by voting with their dollars is... Kinda strange. Not to mention that actual, legit classism can affect someone just for "seeming poor", or do I need to remind anyone of "no shirt, no shoes, no service"?

Why not advocate for actual action against these companies? Why not sabotage them, even? Why not advocate for a stop to the south's exploitation? 

This debate about fast fashion feels like it's about privileged people trying to figure out who is better than the others, instead of actually understanding and solving the issue

author: @ChristieBrewster
text:
A few things come to mind here, about mine and general individual clothing choices, and the reasons behind them:
• Purchasing beyond the high street is alien to me. I have a bunch of trouble with clothes I get by post without trying them on. This gives the kind of places I habitually browse from one particular thing in common. If those places were different, I'm sure that would change my habits too.
• Clothes disposability is an interesting idea, because it's a collaborative problem between retailer and consumer. My clothes from places mentioned in this video will tend to stay in my collection for years (having been bought for their visual appeal or their unique aspect that I didn't already have multiple options for.) Of course it's the fact that I have a large wardrobe that makes them last that long, if each one only had x many washes before it unravelled for good. I'm not designing a look set to match anything specifically modern, and it's not an urge I understand.
• I would like to support deserving manufacturers, but I feel like the whole world of clothing retail is out to lie and cheat and bamboozle you into believing in a saintly reputation for them. As such, there's no-one I actually trust to honestly be better than H&M (unless they're small enough that they can introduce every worker in their process). Consider the negative reputation of Nike and its factory process, when they're still not providing appreciably cheap garments by any means.
• I'm surprised this video didn't bring up what we call the 'Boots theory of socioeconomic unfairness', as exemplified in a very famous Terry Pratchett quote. It dovetails into discussing poverty and clothing choice very neatly.
• And with all this in mind, buying the more hardwearing expensive options does run into a bit of a niche where interested buyers overlap with people interested in clothes as a hobby especially their care and maintenance. Even the cheap jeans on the market could still be repaired, customised, recycled into shorts if the knees give out. But the person with those capabilities with some disposable income will be biased to pick something harder wearing, on the basis of that knowledge and appreciation.
• Lastly, women's clothing (yes, gender is a social construct, but so is clothing ourselves in the first place) have so many more options – just evidenced by square footage in any given general audience clothes store, and more than half of specialist ones. This will make gendered purchasing habits and options illuminating to compare on this topic. I'm a man, myself, and I have a feeling that doesn't come as a surprise based on how I talk about my habits. I'd love to know more about this. And about all of the above.

Legit interesting video. Would love to see a follow-up, if the response makes another crack at the topic a viable one for a video.

author: @ems9616
text:
Ok so i think what people mouthing off in this comments section need to understand is that poverty is not a competition. Poverty does not suddenly become safe or untraumatising just because others have it worse. Additionally, there is a difference between saying- 'companies like primark would still thrive even if middleclass people boycott them cause wc people have to shop there to stay clothed' and 'how dare you critisise shein hauls, dont you know its classist to see someone wasting 1000s pounds on abusively produced clothing and comment on it?' In the first case, the problem is systemic- those cheep stores are providing subsitance clothing to enough people that they can stay in buisness anyway, so a boycott would mean shaming people for subsistance. But in the second case they are clearly using hypothetical working class people as a sheild for their own wasteful behaviour.

But having said that, though i agree broadly with your conclusion, im not sure you got the balance quite right in your reporting.

To expand on this- working class people in many (not all!) Global north countries benefit from being in countries that were colonisers rather than colonised- they have good transport networks, free education, powerful banks, possibly good access to electricity, clean water, and internet. This is the source of much of the 'privalage', and lower overall rates of poverty. 

But...speaking as someone who grew up poor in england- i cant eat my internet. I grew up, like many of my UK peers; food insecure/food poor, a child carer, in a class segregated society which demonstrably does not give a fuck about our survival. And that is still traumatising, and destroys your mental and physical health. It felt at times that you were primarily approaching the impact of poverty on global northerners as... a social game. An issue of keeping up with the joneses rather than a thing that literally shortens peoples lives. In some ways, this tainted your argument about the social side of over consumption- as any wc poc person can tell you, the impacts of classist and racist descrimination from not 'looking respectable' can be severe in there own right. This isnt to defend those shein hauls- their vile and clearly only using 'class conciousness' as a way to dodge consequences for their behaviour.

 But i feel a greater emphasise on pointing that out rather than diminishing the overall argument surrounding sustainable clothing and class as a whole might have supported your argument better. As it is i cant help feeling that the video pits 'real, serious' poverty in the global south against 'social, unserious' poverty in the global north- which is deeply unhelpful and only serves to center global north middle class people as the 'virtuous savers' of the situation by chosing to prioritise sustainability over suffering. This approach is good for shein, and i will not weep when they go belly up- but its a limited perspective, because fast fashion is more than just shein, its a whole industry- and to overhaul that industry will take both indevidual AND structural changes. And to make those structural changes ethical and efective, you need to deal with (and empower!) the exploited people on both end of the chain- those living and working in dangerous conditions during production, and those living subsitance at the consumer end too. 

The people here in the comment section quibbling that somehow global north wc people should be fine, or arnt that seriously affected by poverty just because it affects us in a different way or because a different dollar number is attatched to that suffering....are being classist. As are those that dismiss the damage done by these fashion brands in the global south. But the way to resolve that is to acknowledge the multifacited and damaging nature of poverty, rather than to suggest that one type is unharmful or more frivolous than the other. And perhaps what feels frustrating about this video is not that your wrong per say... but that it feels that the end product is more interested in hashing out a theory argument between middleclass people, litigating the best way to deal with a singular moral challenge, than actually addressing the stregnths and weaknesses of boycotting as a tactic against fast fashion. 

This is in no way meant to be a hate comment, and i hope it hasnt come accross that way. But in terms of addressing the argument you set out to address, i felt this fell short. Sorry if i was incoherent or confused, i just wanted to put my thoughts accross.

author: @HumanNeighbor
text:
The thing is we also can't ask people not to enjoy their lives because they can't afford to do it sustainably. And I know this was already a pretty long video, but the bait and switch move of it's pour responsibility to consume ethically, rather than its our responsibility to create the structural changes needed to solve the problem is a big part of this. We can't boycott our way out of this, and I'm sure if you looked hard enough you'd find out so called "sustainable fashion" isn't sustainable. The trick here which was touched upon is the disposability part of our consumption. It's fine to be into fashion it isn't fine to be constantly cycling our wardrobes to keep up with the 50/year micro trends. 

Also like the misery of poverty is comparative. The visibility of economic inequality is the main problem. If I see rich people living lavish lives I feel the suffering of my own poor life more acutely. It's about the comparison. It is less miserable to be poor amongst the poor, then poor amongst the rich. You feel the unfairness of that acutely. It's why despite the fact we live in comparative luxury to the kings of old we still feel like our lives our awful, because we know how much better they could be, but have no power to actually achieve that better life. 

The main point though, is that capitalism and climate change can not be stopped by individual consumer choices. We need real institutional change,  but again we lack the power to make that change. So we're left with this weird, I must not buy nice things, because it was made with child labor choice that just leaves us miserable regardless of which choice we make. It's awful, and it's designed to be awful and paralyze our ability to act.

Buying sustainable brands is not her issue — its buying unnecessary stuff in general that props up a vastly inequitable system. We shouldn’t be arguing about where to shop, instead we should be criticizing when and with what frequency do people shop. The “vote with your dollar” concept is capitalist in nature because it implies your impact is only through money. YOUR LABOR IS A MUCH MORE POWERFUL FORCE. Withhold your labor, demand your labor be compensated, if you have the ability to advocate and protect others’ labor, do that too. If you are privileged enough to by a “haul” of clothing, you are privileged enough to be educated about fast fashion, capitalism, and you have access to secondhand goods. I hate this argument because people like mommy socialist are undermining anti-capitalism and virtue signaling about classism. Wield your labor. Participate in politics and policy creation. Do as little harm as you can (buy less).

They rule in most countries and are international influencers. What they say is not taken in the local context, but a global one. Granted, many are stupid and young, but then that is all the more reason to ask ourselves if we want to give all that power and influence over us to a damn teenager living in a beverly hills mansion. author: @sbclaridge
text:
If I were to spend $1500+ on a clothing haul, with the assumption that I were buying new, I would invest in a small capsule wardrobe from a good-quality brand such as White House Black Market (WHBM) or Ann Taylor, over a whole bunch of cheap clothes I might never wear.  Namely, I would want something that would last me years with the maximum-possible versatility.

All those massive SHEIN (et al.) hauls you see here on YouTube and on TikTok tend to come from people with a lot of discretionary income (to my understanding).  If you have that kind of money to spend, why not spend it on something that you will actually wear and that will last you for years to come?  I'm also not spending the money on a super-high-end item, either; to clarify my point, dresses (just to point out one type of garment) at stores like WHBM and Ann Taylor tend to fall into the $150-$200 range (at the time of this writing).

author: @zkme2734
text:
“The reason that the rich were so rich, Vimes reasoned, was because they managed to spend less money.

Take boots, for example. He earned thirty-eight dollars a month plus allowances. A really good pair of leather boots cost fifty dollars. But an affordable pair of boots, which were sort of OK for a season or two and then leaked like hell when the cardboard gave out, cost about ten dollars. Those were the kind of boots Vimes always bought, and wore until the soles were so thin that he could tell where he was in Ankh-Morpork on a foggy night by the feel of the cobbles.

But the thing was that good boots lasted for years and years. A man who could afford fifty dollars had a pair of boots that'd still be keeping his feet dry in ten years' time, while the poor man who could only afford cheap boots would have spent a hundred dollars on boots in the same time and would still have wet feet."

- Terry Pratchett, "Men at Arms"

author: @shanisokay
text:
I think it's a fine line to tread. I can't help but feel the discussion of the evils of fast fashion is almost exclusively hurled at women online (to be fair, they are posting most of the hauls) and has become an excuse to get away with harassing women and it's totally fine because they are "unethical". It seems like people spend more time feeling smug because they thrift or shop at Reformation instead of actually trying to solve the issue at hand. 

Obviously some TikToker getting bullied for wearing a top from Zara is not at the same level of suffering as the exploited workers of the global south, but unless we spend our time actively organizing to fight against these poor conditions, it comes across as a hollow gesture. We all have personal responsibility, but we need to find a balance in spreading the message and organizing vs. shaming people for their personal shortcomings as they fail to be a perfect ethical consumer in a corrupt system. I would argue that is where the lower-class frustration comes from when they are being shamed for shopping Shein. Like is that where our energy is really best spent? Targeting individuals rather than companies? But is there really a way to alter our culture of over-consumption without pointing out these problems to the individuals who profit from it directly (i.e. influencers)? I don't know! 

I think these are interesting conversations to have so thank you for the video Broey!

author: @deaf-tomcat
text:
this conversation is also complicated by the fact that fast fashion is not meant to last. you compare older Doc Martins and they're sturdier and were made for longevity, meanwhile, I have worn work shoes/boots until the soles and fabric are actively peeling and breaking. People used to be able to wear the same shoe every day for many years, and we are now at a point where affordable work shoes only last me a couple of months. Maybe half a year or more if I just ignore the pain. Sustainable brands are expensive, but fast fashion is even more expensive in the long run and it's difficult to mitigate that when you only have so much money left every month. 

And of course, fast fashion has opened up a lot more options for fat folk. It's bc of Shien and etc that we get to participate in trends without breaking the bank. My older sister constantly buys clothes from companies like that bc they offer an affordable alternative. Very often, I'll see people talk about sustainable fashion and just ignore this glaring omission. I like going thrifting, it's a lot more affordable but I can not reliably find pants in my size at shops. I'm like a US 20-24 and there just aren't a lot of options available. We can't win in this conversation because individual change will not improve things by and large. Structural change only happens with collectivist action, not by individuals. 
But yeah, great video!
