author: @marsha_melloww
text:
Can I also add as an environmental researcher that natural leather is NOT more sustainable than faux one. Ppl seem to think natural leather is sustainable because it's animal skin when in reality it's animal skin that was treated with hazardous for humans and animals chromium to literally prevent it from biodegrading. The process is called tanning. In one research they tried to biodegrade natural leather in lab controlled environment with added biodegradability acceleration, and it barely started biodegrading. Imagine how it's going to behave in a landfill where there is no biodegradability acceleration.
Faux leather is mostly made of polymers. And some are more biodegradable than others. "Vegan" leather, for example, is usually made of cellulose from plants like mango peel, cacti, banana peel, pineapple peel etc sometimes mixed with other kinds of polymers. And it does biodegrade faster than natural leather, has no poisonous chemicals that get into water supplies and of course has nothing to do with animal industry that is the second largest industry by carbon dioxide emissions.
Lecture is over.

author: @TheMusicalFruit
text: If the problem is systemic, then isn't a systemic solution warranted, maybe even required?  Sure, we can all buy locally, but practically that doesn't work because not enough people will do it.  Organic labels didn't get rid of factory farming and that's been a thing in the US since the 1990s.  Changing the laws seems like a much more effective tactic, but that's hard, expensive, and those in power oppose it.  So instead, we get these fig leaves to place over exploitative products to make everyone feel better about participating in an inherently unfair system.  I would argue that "buy local" as a slogan may even do more harm than good because it alleviates consumer guilt that could be more properly mobilized to change laws and oust corrupt politicians from office.

author: @morethanyourbasics
text:
less than 4 mins into the video but I want to add my two cents. Keep in mind that I am a black woman so I can only speak to the global majority and not the #FFFFFF perspective. 

It's an intersection of classism and racism and elitism to tell someone who is BIPOC that they shouldn't buy fast fashion. BIPOC people are generally the people who are dealing with the effects of climate change, police brutality, lack of networks for career etc so the idea of not buying a $4.99 t-shirt to vote with their dollar doesn't take in to account the full scope of the problem or why one would feel so entitled to point out the costs involved in the t-shirt but not take take their oppression in society as seriously. It's a double edged sword - someone who isn't rich wanting to stretch their dollars and be seen closer as an equal in "nice things" is not the same thing as the people who do $1000+ hauls. Its like people pointing out all the plastic waste involved in pre chopping up vegetables when people with disabilities would prefer the option and don't want to be shamed for it because they aren't the ones responsible for overproduction of plastic.

Don't buy fast fashion just go to the thrift store...but aren't women discriminated for not wearing make up and some states in America just passed a law to NOT  discriminate against a black woman's hair. That makes sense in theory but in practice? not everyone has the time or knowledge to shift through so many options. 

Don't buy fast food / eat healthy or vegan....convenience exists for a reason. 
Don't spend so much time on the screen be more active .... again it's one thing if you are talking about one person but when you start pointing fingers at a large group of people doing the same thing it's probably systematic. 

There's no winner in an argument because there is no ethical consumption and people need to do more reflecting before they decide to die on the hill they're pointing from. systematic problems need systematic solutions

author: @BroeyDeschanel
text:
Hi everyone, thank you for your comments! They're all very insightful! I'm learning a lot about the contradictory nature of "voting with your dollar" and I also totally agree that the movement against fast fashion needs to be organized (random disorganized boycotting is not very effective). Thank you all for being so knowledgeable - and I'm happy that my comment sections are a place people can congregate about these ideas! I am also aware that there is no cut and dry solution to this problem - I am mostly just trying to poke holes in the "classism" argument. 

Also guys - the bags and packages in the background are EMPTY and recycled from my parents' recent move, and from different bags my roommate and I have saved over the years. C'mon lol.

author: @colonelweird
text:
Say, does your researcher Nick have a famous anarchist grandpa by any chance?

Great video -- I agree with every word -- but I was waiting for that one word at the end: revolution. I feel like its absence in these discussions is the primary factor that distorts understanding. Broey's interlocutors at the beginning were borrowing leftist language to excuse their consumerism. Surely they were able to do this because leftist language has been widely used by liberals for non-leftist purposes. But the horizon of our discourse should always be revolution: the system must be destroyed, the powerless must seize power, etc. Broey used the word only at the very end, said nothing more about it, so it's not really clear what she means by it. Is revolution going to come because consumers change their buying habits? No. We should still do what we can when we make purchases, but we should not have any illusion that that will lead to revolution. We need to think a lot more about what actually will lead to revolution.

I guess this is my way of saying: Broey, please do a video on revolution and its meaning for us living in wealthy countries with moribund leftist cultures.

author: @cenobiten
text:
I really like the conclusion that you came to at the end of the video, as someone who was sucked into "radical" online spaces between the ages of around 17-21, I definitely have some familiarity with the frustration you feel. While I agree that just the tiny character limit of tiktok comments or twitter posts fails to foster meaningful analytical discourse, I think the main issues with online culture (and the for profit organisations that host them) are deeper than that...
I feel there is an appetite for ideological opposition to neoliberal capitalism, which I consider to be a good thing. However this appetite is being met through platforms such as twitter and tiktok that see stimulation (intellectual, analytical, or otherwise) as a highly competitive free market. Their apparatus encourages creators to churn out loud attention grabbing content, Just like the micro trend driven fast fashion industry encourages garment workers and designers to put out attention grabbing collections every fortnight.
To me, radical online discourse mirrors fast fashion in this way; content consumers are encouraged to engage with new ideologies so long as it is fun and exciting, and creates a niche that the market can cater to, until no more pleasure can be squeezed out of it, and a slightly different ideology gains traction. And what results is members of these online spaces not actually interested in social critique or achieving some sort of revolution but a group clinging to the power it fought for in the marketplace of ideas.

Commodity fetishm of fast fashion can lead to a momentary escape from the meaninglessness of life under late capitalism. Being radical online can feel like this too; the pure bliss of dunking on someone 5-15 years your senior in a tik tok comment section is more enticing than any advertising could be. But ultimately it's just promoting the longevity of your niche internet ideology, and acting as a sedative to the meaninglessness of postmodern living.
The term “revolutionary procrastion” springs to mind when describing genuinely well meaning radical teens, who lace their drags with jargon invented by boring German guys from the 1870s and the veneer of radicalism, without actually challenging the status quo.

What I'm trying to say may be too big and sprawling to fit in a youtube comment and all of this comes from a former radical online teen myself. A lot of this frustration I'm expressing is also directed at my former self. The remedy that I am suggesting is not that teens get any less radical, it is that their movements become less tainted by the demands of the platforms their culture exists on.

author: @karmeljon
text: The cognitive dissonance I get from at the same time reading about historical dress and modern fashion industry. It's maddening how we went from honing for centuries the longevity and quality of clothing and the art of dressmaking to poorly constructed clothing made out of materials that fall apart in a month or so, that neither fit anyone or properly even protect from hot or cold in about a century. We went from dressmakers being respected professionals to under-payed and exploited workers, whose conditions more closely resemble slavery. It's such an obvious and glaring counter argument for the capitalism's promise of market forces naturally driving up quality and innovation. The exact opposite has happened. We have lost a lot of dressmaking techniques and skills to history directly because of capitalism. It didn't fulfill any need, before industrialization people, even poor people had high quality clothing that lasted long and were easy to fix. Poor people didn't have a lot of clothing, but they didn't need a lot of clothing because their few clothes were such high quality, much more durable, fitting and protecting than the clothes of the average rich person's today. The need capitalism answered with the clothing industry was the need of the manufacturer and the business owner to make more money.

author: @abelabel3664
text:
Nice video. One problem we often have in the left is becoming cynical about anything that can improve conditions if we are not tackling problems at a systemic level. Yes, companies are powerful and are able to manufacture demand, while this is a result of capitalism. This fact, however, does not mean that we shifting the demand will not affect the market or even its regulation, causing better outcomes for those being exploited and for the planet. I have a similar impression of many leftists who are cynical about charity, as it usually does not address the causes of the issue. Sure, but it does make a difference to real people who cannot wait for a systemic change. Are you worried about the people or about being right on your socio-political analysis?

Sure, there is no ethical consumption under capitalism, but that does not mean that all consumption is equally unethical.

On that note, I see you are making the same mistake when you mention veganism. Like buying dozens of tops is unnecessary, so is the consumption of animal products in the majority of cases when people have a choice. Moreover, the very definition of veganism acknowledges context when it states "as much as possible and practicable".  

Additionally, you perpetuate the misconception that veganism is classist and that it is a diet (and not a stance on ethics), one in which you require fancy mock meats. Plant-based diets are often cheaper than diets containing animal products. Many traditional diets in the Global South (where I am from) are mostly plant-based, and use the cheapest ingredients: beans, lentils, rice, pasta, legumes, seeds, potatoes, fruit, etc. Moreover, the issue addressed by veganism goes beyond capitalism. The capitalistic exploitation in animal agriculture is that of the workers, who are usually underprivileged (after all, who wants to work in slaughterhouses...?), but on top of that you have the fact that animal agriculture inherently involves the killing and exploitation of animals (by the billions). Sure, one single consumer stopping will not solve the issue, just like in fast fashion, but markets do respond to demand, obviously.

author: @songbirdlyricz
text:
I get BIG triggered by this debate. I’m someone who has always had solid financial security and have been living in global north countries my whole life. I’m hyper sensitive to the issues of sustainability and workers rights and the liberation of those in the global south. At the same time, anxiety around these issues (in my personal case) is debilitating, paralyzing, and self-destructive. So the argument of “you are morally wrong for buying from fast fashion” is very difficult to navigate because, for me, it usually ends up in severe panic and paralysis. (Not super productive in changing the tides of the climate crisis or working for liberation..) 

My first instinct after hearing the back and forth in those tiktok comments is “I want to hear directly from those “poor people who can’t afford clothes” and those “exploited factory workers in the global south” that these tiktok users (who seem to not be from either of these groups) are so feverishly discussing.” Like, what do people from these groups experience? What would they say would ACTUALLY help?? 

I have several big issues with these debates that even the most well-intentioned tend to dance around. 

1) I DONT believe that shame is a productive tool to catalyze change. I feel like when we hyperfixate on minute actions of individuals and label them “morally correct/incorrect,” we just end up cultivating a bunch of neurotic, hyper anxious, paralyzed, or cynical people (who often turn to anxious consumption to cope anyway..) This was touched on in the video, but I don’t think it stuck with the conclusions being made. Why not lead with solidarity instead? What I mean by that is: i think when you care for people and make these ideal practices more accessible and sharable, then people will be more likely to engage in them. I believe the vast majority of people don’t actively want to fuck over people in the global south and destroy the planet. So what’s REALLY stopping them from adopting these preferred habits? 

2) even when addressing the larger systems outside of the individual, we still tend to place the site of change on individuals. Again, this was addressed in the video, but no alternative sites for change were really provided in the end. Of course we can’t neglect the actions of individuals and how they play into and perpetuate these systems. But for the love of god, do we not have the capacity to look outside of individuals’ lives?? Many of us are neurotically pouring over our little habits with our heads down. And then we see other individuals, and neurotically pour over THEIR little habits, and constantly compare ourselves to that person. In this way, change-making gets atomized. 

Here’s a personal example of when this self-adjustment doesn’t work very well. I mostly take care of feeding only myself. With my level of function and my lifestyle, buying in bulk turns into expensive food waste. Cooking in bulk turns into expensive food waste. Making nutrient-rich, delicious food is difficult with my time and energy resources. And without cooking and with my food needs it is extremely difficult to get the nutrients I need delivered in “purely(?)” “”sustainable”” or “”ethical”” foods. What would actually help me in this case?? Having communally prepared, delicious, nutrient-rich meals that are easily accessible and affordable (much like someone in a big family cooking for the whole family, or a church pot luck, or a school cafeteria). Instead, I rely on a lot of pre-made foods to successfully feed myself. So do “easy vegan cooking hacks” or “12 tips for cheap sustainability” offer some direction? Yes. Do they solve my main barriers to incorporating these preferable habits? No. What WOULD help in my case? Communal meals, for one. Can I get there by only hyperfixating on my individual habits? Nope. 

3)What is a “necessity”? This gets really tricky when there’s so 
many conflicting messages being launched at us. I don’t think we can honestly apply a universal standard for “needs.” In this assessment of “true needs,” we tend to neglect things like “art” and “fun” and “comfort.” Some ultra-minimalist influencers might present to you a bare, furnitureless house and conclude that this is what meeting ones bare needs looks like, for example. I think this type of ideal is an overcorrection for the “manufactured needs” that are constantly marketed to people (that many of us pick up on and try to reject). However, I think on the individual or single-case level, it helps to draw the distinction between what WE determine we need and what we are TOLD we are SUPPOSED to need. What were you told is supposed to make you happy? What really makes you happy? I think weeding out “manufactured needs” through this process alone is immensely productive.

4)Lastly, what is with the conclusion to this video essay?? From what I understand, it’s a scathing critique against putting the right of people to participate in the “fashion rat race” over the right of people to live in habitable conditions. Agreed! But.. that’s it then?? I’m not sure there’s many people engaging in this debate who would disagree with that point. So what is the conclusion of this video accomplishing with a fierce sermon and ominous outro music? What are the recommended directions? The next steps? How do we get out of this endless debate cycle and actually actively fix all of the issues addressed in the debate?? 

My word of advice (take it or leave it) is:
Go touch grass. I’m serious. Go take a walk around a neighborhood or something. Go interact with physical spaces and the people in them. The internet is such a great place for communication, but I think we all need to pick our heads up a little more often..

author: @reyandknight
text: I'm commenting while this idea is still fresh in my head, so, just right after the intro. I think there's nuance to buying fast fashion when you're poor. I'll use myself as an example. I'm plus-size, so I have to buy my underwear and bras new (except for three perfectly sized bras I lucked into at Goodwill are you KIDDING me), and socks I tend to buy new just because I kind of assume everyone wears their socks until they resemble swiss cheese. I can very easily remember the past few garments I bought fast fashion: a clearance sweatshirt from Walmart last month, because the weather took a nosedive and I needed something warm, and three pairs of pants from Walmart over a year and a half ago, because I needed pants for work and the thrift store was closed due to the pan.dem.ic. I do manage to thrift most of what I need, even if it takes me a while, and I think I'm classed as a largefat. I also live out in the country with the closest thrift store being a 20 minute drive. Yeah, it's convenient for me to say that me buying fast fashion for literal near-emergencies is more in gray territory, but also, I'm not showing off my fast fashion purchases for social media clout, I'm literally wearing them so I can move about the world. Everything else I've bought, from work shirts to blouses to skirts to pajamas, I've thrifted for the past three years. I've also started learning how to mend clothing so I can make my clothes last longer. It's the mindset more than anything. Am I just trying to put fabric on my body, or am I trying to get likes on TikTok? For me, it's the former. And I'm still fashionable about it too, when I have the energy for it. I buy the fabrics, cuts, and colors that I know will look good on me and if I don't find the item I'm looking for, I keep an eye out for the next time or see if a thrifting site has something. There *ARE* a lot of options out there that aren't buying a freight load of clothes from shein or aliexpress.

author: @ayanna6327
text:
This video was great. I will definitely be putting "How Europe Underdeveloped Africa" on my reading-list! I definitely agree that fast-fashion is a problem, and it's a problem that we're largely apathetic to. I remember when I was a freshman in high school, I did a project on why Walmart was an extremely unethical corporation (and still is, it was even worse back then) that exploits people in the "third world." Some of my classmates rolled their eyes. Back then, I was a lot more reactive and extremely anti-Walmart, so I understood as I probably came off preachy (I was 15, so I'm sparing myself) I definitely would have addressed the topic a lot differently now that I have 9 years worth of hindsight. 

Basically, you said everything I wanted to, and much more with a lot more tact and nuance.

As someone who is plus-sized, I have ALWAYS struggled with thrifting. I do buy fast fashion on occasion, however when I do, I always do it with the intent of buying clothes I actually like, and not just participating in some trend for a few months, and then I'll never wear it again. That's sort of how I make "peace" with it (though I def wouldn't say equate that thought to peace.)

Also, you brought up an interesting point about how those world crisis campaigns put the emphasis on the average individual and not the machine. As individuals, we are a part of the machine, but at the same time, we are essentially forced to be a part of it. If the corporations that have created this monster were actually held accountable for the crises they create, then the situation at hand would greatly increase. While we can, and should, make efforts to be ethical, those campaigns should also directly directly target the government(s) at play for failing their people.

author: @fridakahlo894
text: I think a point your missing is that ethical consumption is often depicted as some kind of 'activism'. By buying ethical produced commodities you can wash off your sin of belonging to the global North. That's how the whole ehtical 'movement' started. Buy biological eggs and thanks god your not to blame for the shredding of fledglings. Boycott H&M and you basically solved  starvation wages. The quest of ethical consumption keeps you busy and gives you a good feeling - because after all you're doing sth! It's a proxy for real political action. Individual consumer choice will NEVER be enough to change the underlying mechanisms that produce the bad and unfair stuff that is happening. Yes, it'll do no harm if the whole global north questions their own overconsumption but it's by far not central for change and the narrowing on consumption takes so much time you have less time to read, inform yourself, organize on a local level and figgghhht. The concept of ethical consumption grew out of a very white, affluence, apolitical context and I think that's important if you want to critize the 'ethical consumption = classist' take.

text: im from the global south, not exactly the countries where imperialism took place but we were indeed colonized by first world countries. here in chile, even tho we have more social and economic stability, you can still see the effects of colonialism in some parts of the country. for example, the public transport, our economic dependance to china and other first world countries, the deep rooted classism and xenophobia and also, more related to the topic of this video, the poverty that you can see in the streets. here poverty does not look like a family in a dirty house with a semi-empty fridge. here poverty looks like camp sites, with no water or electricity, with kids who can't go to school because they need to either work or take care of their families and the constant fight to make money if you want to eat. and i can't even imagine how it should be in countries where workers exploitation is still a thing. people from the us or other first world countries love to defend the lower class by letting things like fast fashion happen when in reality they are just protecting their own, and not thinking of those who have suffered for centuries the consequences of imperialism, colonialism and capitalism.

author: @Tofu_va_Bien
text:
Excellent video. I hope people will begin to realise that this argument applies to veganism as well. You'd be surprised how many western leftists outright dismiss the voices of vegans in the global south because their input isn't welcome apparently. I've actually seen groups of westerners dog piling on a vegan from a minority indigenous community in India, accusing him of being privileged by virtue of him following and advocating for a vegan lifestyle. 

I think perhaps it comes down to ignorance, and I don't mean that in a derogative manner, I mean that lots of people genuinely don't know much about this issue. Anyone who has been to both developed and over-exploited (my preferred term for developing) countries can see the difference in what poverty looks like in the global south vs the west. There is simply no comparison. 

To be clear, I am aware that there are food deserts in the United states, and people who rely on animal products for survival, but the majority of folks that I've seen claiming veganism is too expensive, or too much of a hassle for them are privileged as hell (looking at you Vaush 👀).

author: @lauraigla6319
text:
There's a fine line between constructive discourse and "these third world people are poorer than you, and it's your fault their conditions are bad." Props, for trying to walk it.

It should also be considered that these people working in such terrible conditions are simply trying to earn money to live. author: @upsetstudios1819
text:
I'm currently in fashion school and I had a very striking conversation with a classmate during my first year. 

Personally I've always been aware of the horrors of the fashion industry and ever since I chose to go into it I've had the existential dread of "what if I have to join the fast fashion industry in order to work in fashion?". Choosing to be hopeful and aim for slow fashion to the best of my abilities. 

But in my first year, I met this woman, let's call her Pam. Pam was a grown woman with children who had fled Pakistan only about a year prior and came to Norway. She was now enrolled in a one year textile course and she seemed very excited. 

A few weeks in, we were talking about where we'll end up after fashion school. She was very excited to work with her hands and sew lots of garments, possibly with some pakistani influences. Hoping to give her some perspective, I decided to tell her the truth: 

Fashion graduates in Norway don't sew. We sit in offices at Bik Bok, Zara, H&M, designing clothes and sending said designs to factories in the global south. We're usually in the top few, deciding the workload for thousands of garment workers that make breadcrumbs compared to us. My teacher in a different university even designed clothes for Bik Bok for over a decade. In Oslo alone there's like 4 different fashion schools. If we decide to design indigo dresses, then thousands of liters of dye and chemicals will be dumped into the river systems in the global south. 

And I could see the same heartbreak in her as I have felt, only I can't imagine fleeing a country of war to seek a better life in the world's richest country, only to learn your true passion might lead to suffering to those who couldn't flee

author: @KalynnCoy
text:
Thank you for putting this out! I have a degree in fashion and ultimately ended up leaving the industry because it was so unsustainable and contributed to my climate anxiety to such an extent- I'm now in academia studying the effects of influencer endorsements, so this video is right up my alley. I've gotten into a lot of internet fights with people who argue that fast fashion is "good, actually". There tends to be a lot of privilege in that perspective- even from economically marginalized communities in the "global north". 

We should also emphasize how quickly fast fashion has emerged, especially sites like Cider and SheIn. Because the growth of these sites has been so, well- fast- most of the data we have if from before the emergence of these retailers. Instead a lot of the data on fast fashion figures only look at Zara and H&M versus the hyper fast fashion retailers. And although Zara and H&M are bad, they are less horrible than the online only fast fashion platforms-consequently, our understanding of the environmental and social impact of fast fashion is likely underestimated, given that these platforms are incredibly opaque about their sourcing, production, and shipping tactics. We literally do not know just how bad these firms are to the planet.

author: @ritiaggarwal995
text:
9:46 Sati was actually outlawed by a combination of attempts made by Indian reformers and Christian missionaries. Hindus, Muslims and Christians worked together to abolish that. The Raja of Nadia and Mughal kings had also tried to abolish it, but to no avail.

Think about it, even India back then was a patriarchal society. Women were BURNING themselves on their husbands' funeral pyres, sometimes bride as young as 13-16. And this because? Widow remarriage was considered "bad" in India, and you weren't supposed to marry again if your husband died.

Say what you want about postcolonialism, but this was a really stupid and frankly insensitive example to use. Sati, along with child marriage, has been one of the biggest issues (and wins) for Indian feminists.

author: @BryanBMusic
text:
I definitely agree that the Global North often erases the struggles of the Global South.  

However I also struggle with when/where to discuss individual behavior, especially because at one point all of us will be throwing stones in glass houses.   For example, someone could call out your waste of resources unnecessarily to add decor to your video (excess shoeboxes, paper bags, print outs, etc).   Also most of us across class lines have more than one pair of shoes in the global north.  Where should the line be drawn?  

At the same time I definitely see people weaponize “no ethical consumption under capitalism” as a pass to be inconsiderate.  I think it’s really dicey with no clear answer. 

Overconsumption is an issue but to overly frame it as an individual choice lets the system stay in tact.  

I think we should encourage each other to do our best and also offer grace when people fall short of perfect.  And still place most of the ire at corporations and people in power.

author: @tyquira
text:
The thing that's so frustrating about the argument with fast fashion/poverty is that people will go out of their way to blame corporations. Like I had an argument with a friend where they were complaining about shoes and how they kept falling apart, and I gently pointed out that a store nearby had good quality shoes and the cost was only slightly more expensive (like I think $10-$30, I don't gouge shoe prices but they're not more expensive than what I see at bigger retail stores), and I had a few pairs that had lasted me several years, as opposed to other places where I'd be lucky to get 6 months wear out of them. I then added, kinda as an afterthought because this kind of stuff is important to me that it was a small business and the shoes are cruelty free. Immediately they went on the defensive like I was judging them and said "it wasn't their fault it's the corporations!" Which like, is true. But it highlights this weird disconnect we have with taking care of our planet. Like, I hate to tell you this but if climate change legislation does go through its not just going to impact the lives of people at the top, it's going to impact everyone. Society's fabric will have to change from the bottom up. Everyone will have to change their lifestyle to consume less and we will probably consume less animal products and buy less shoes. Like this will happen if taking care of our planet takes the center stage of society's conversation, whether we like it or not. The disconnect is incredible.

Edit: so clearly this comment was taken in the worst way possible where people automatically assumed that I was talking about poor people where it will break the bank if they spend one cent more than what's being asked of them, and it's their fault there's a climate crisis; I'm not having this argument with people, it will go in circles. 

My point was specifically if you're a person who can afford to choose, you should be able to choose, when MOST people I KNOW personally CAN choose to not take Ubers or not shop at fast fashion or spend a little bit more upfront to get a better quality item, and they DON'T because it's slightly more comfortable for them in the short term not to. The person I was talking to in this situation has more money than I do (I've seen her paychecks!) and could easily afford a better quality pair of boots if she wanted to; if that doesn't apply to you, fine. Most of us can be honest with ourselves on whether or not that's true and do the math.

My point was that a lot of people don't seem to understand that if the care of our planet is made the priority it should be made, then everything will change. For example, if factory farming has caps put onto it, animal product production will be much more expensive which means that a lot of us who consume animal products now may not be able to in the future. Animal products will probably be considered even more of the luxury item it is now and much more expensive. If abusive factories stop being a thing, the fast fashion aka cheap clothes will not be as readily available, meaning that better quality items will be. They seem to think that they can go on living their life as is because well, blame the coorporations, it's not our fault, our way of life is 100% sustainable but these damn coorporations, screw em. 

And it starts with people who have a choice in whether or not they will support ethically made items/ethical companies and by and large, they are not making that choice.

My mom comes from a low class family (we are a mid class now but if you are from a third world country that's basically low class for a first world country) and she always prefer to buy high quality clothes  over low quality, because with low quality clothes it only lasts for 3/4 months meaning you have to spend money for clothes again while with high quality you can use it for years and still look good (sorry if my English is not that good)

Edit: a clarification when I'm talking about "high quality" clothes I'm not talking about super expensive brands like some first world country has. I'm talking about the regular brands from my own country or nearby countries that are almost the same price that the low quality clothes

text:
“The third world is not poor. Most countries are rich. The Philippines are rich. Brazil is rich. Mexico is rich. Chile is rich. Only the people are poor.

These countries are not underdeveloped. They’re overexploited!”

- Michael Parenti

author: @JamisonMyth
text: Fast fashion kills the planet and exploits workers, and at the same time class is enforced through fashion. The contradiction is baked into our social, political, and economic reality. If you worry about which side of the argument you stand on or which side gets more likes in a comment section on 1 video, you're already missing the bigger picture.
